clues.text: Started antihypertensive medication two months ago. Multiple comorbidities including diabetes and hypertension. Wears bifocal glasses but admits to not wearing them consistently indoors.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Romberg test positive with eyes closed. Tandem stance unable to hold >3 seconds. Gait shows reduced arm swing and shortened stride. Ankle dorsiflexion strength 4/5 bilaterally. Cognitive screen (MoCA) 24/30 with slowed processing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows age-appropriate mild cortical atrophy and small vessel ischemic changes. No acute stroke, no subdural collection. Cervical spine imaging normal.

puzzles.explanation: Multifactorial falls in elderly result from combined balance impairment, sensory loss, medication effects, and environmental hazards.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 3 has swollen knee joints and morning stiffness lasting over an hour.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking after waking, refuses to run, play, and refuses to bear weight on legs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 2 months ago, no fever or trauma, and otherwise healthy development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral warm knee effusions with restricted flexion/extension, positive antinuclear antibodies, and no systemic signs of infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows symmetric synovial hypertrophy with inflammatory enhancement in both knees; no bone erosion or cartilage damage. Findings consistent with early inflammatory arthropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Oligoarticular JIA commonly affects knees with morning stiffness and swelling, requiring anti-inflammatory treatment and careful monitoring for uveitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient Child age 10 has pain around the kneecap during activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform jumping and running without knee pain; stairs and deep squatting particularly provocative.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma, insidious onset, and active in sports rapid growth.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive j-sign with knee flexion, weak hip abductors bilaterally, tight rectus femoris, mild effusion on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal, MRI shows no cartilage damage or meniscal pathology, slight lateral patellar tracking on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain in children results from biomechanical issues and muscle imbalance, treated with strengthening and activity modification.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 20-year-old college athlete struck in head during game, reports seeing stars.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Returned to play initially but later developed inability to focus on coursework, persistent headache with activity, and unsteadiness when turning head quickly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous subconcussive impact 4 weeks earlier. Current symptoms evolved gradually over 6–8 hours post-collision rather than immediately. Reports ongoing sensitivity to light and noise.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: GCS 15, pupils equal and reactive. Balance impairment on single-leg stance (left > right). Positive King-Devick test (delayed number-naming). No focal weakness or speech disturbance. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows no structural lesion, contusion, or microhemorrhage. Neuropsychological testing 2 weeks post-injury reveals slowed processing speed and impaired executive function relative to pre-season baseline. Symptoms persist despite physical and cognitive rest.

puzzles.explanation: Mild traumatic brain injury (concussion) in athlete with transient altered consciousness, post-concussive symptoms, and increased vulnerability to second-impact syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly gentleman with hip discomfort in both groin and lateral regions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with stairs and getting up from chair; better with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive worsening over several years. Remote hip injury in his 20s with persistent symptoms since then.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Antalgic gait, significant hip flexion contracture. Pain at end-range movements.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows cartilage loss, bone marrow lesions, and joint space reduction.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced hip OA with secondary effects on gait and flexibility. Previous trauma accelerates degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with progressive, insidious shoulder pain over several months. Pain is diffuse around the posterior shoulder.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform overhead reaching or throwing. Activities aggravate pain, particularly cross-body adduction. Progressive functional decline over months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior trauma or injury. No history of shoulder instability. Pain developed insidiously without clear precipitant. Patient denies neck pain or radicular symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked infraspinatus weakness (4/5) on manual testing. Supraspinatus strength intact. Positive infraspinatus atrophy noted on inspection. EMG shows denervation pattern in infraspinatus; supraspinatus EMG normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows suprascapular nerve compression and associated paralabral ganglion cyst visible.

puzzles.explanation: Suprascapular neuropathy secondary to paralabral ganglion cyst compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old presents with exertional breathlessness and fatigue that began 2 months ago. Reports swelling in one leg and feeling anxious about physical activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Limited walking tolerance, getting winded easily. Swelling in saphenous vein harvest leg still present. Anxiety about returning to normal activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Aortic stenosis corrected with mechanical valve, on warfarin. Concurrent CABG performed. Hypertension, diabetes type 2.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternal scar well-healed but hypertrophic. Left lower leg shows pitting edema, skin pigmentation changes, and reduced skin turgor. Heart sounds reveal an audible mechanical valve click in systole.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram: normal mechanical valve gradients, no regurgitation, ejection fraction 50%. Chest X-ray: clear lung fields, normal cardiac silhouette, no pleural effusion.

puzzles.explanation: Late post-cardiac surgery phase with emphasis on graduated return to activity and managing anticoagulation effects. Older age and comorbidities affect rehabilitation timeline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports recurrent painful mouth sores and skin rashes. Recent eye discomfort with some visual changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty eating and speaking due to oral sores. Pain with sitting and friction on lower body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent ulceration pattern for over 10 years, with progressive involvement. Genital ulcers preceded eye symptoms by several years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Shallow oral ulcers with erythematous halo. Scarring and induration at genital sites. Pustular lesions and nodules on lower extremities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ophthalmologic findings: anterior and posterior uveitis with retinal vasculitis. Inflammatory response consistent with systemic vasculitic disease.

puzzles.explanation: Behçet's disease requires ophthalmologic monitoring and immunosuppressive therapy given risk of blindness, management of mucosal ulceration, control of vasculitis, and rehabilitation focusing on maintaining function during flare periods and preventing complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with severe burning pain in a band-like region of the trunk or limb that began weeks after a shingles rash resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient avoids wearing clothing or touching affected skin due to severe pain; activities requiring contact with the affected area are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain persists months after acute varicella zoster infection; severe in elderly or immunocompromised.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Decreased sensation to pinprick and light touch in dermatomal distribution; hyperalgesia to pin-prick; residual erythema or post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation in the affected dermatome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Diagnosis is clinical; imaging and serology not routinely needed. Consider MRI if atypical presentation to exclude other compressive neuropathies in the same dermatome.

puzzles.explanation: Post-herpetic neuralgia shows neuropathic pain persisting after varicella zoster with dermatomal distribution and severe allodynia.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young patient with widespread joint pain and chronic pain complaints.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Repeated joint dislocations with minimal trauma; difficulty with high-demand activities due to instability and fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hypermobility since childhood; family history of joint laxity or connective tissue disorder; multiple joint dislocations across different sites over years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized hypermobility, skin hyperextensibility, and tissue fragility poor wound healing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging normal; structural degenerative changes absent despite clinical hypermobility; genetic testing available to confirm specific EDS subtype.

puzzles.explanation: Ehlers-Danlos syndrome is the classic hypermobility presentation with widespread pain and systemic connective tissue involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old female at 8 weeks gestation with early onset pelvic pain. Anterior pubic pain and lateral hip pain bilaterally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 26-year-old female at 8 weeks gestation presenting with bilateral pelvic and hip pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First pregnancy with very early PGP onset. History of sacroiliac joint dysfunction pre-pregnancy. Pelvic hypermobility syndrome.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: History of sacroiliac joint dysfunction and generalized ligamentous laxity prior to pregnancy. Unusually early symptom onset at 8 weeks gestation, despite first pregnancy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Positive SIJ provocation tests bilaterally. Palpable asymmetry of pelvic landmarks. Generalized ligamentous laxity evident on physical examination.

puzzles.explanation: Pregnancy-Related Pelvic Girdle Pain shows early onset first trimester PGP in first pregnancy with underlying pelvic hypermobility contributing to increased instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Female patient reports chronic vulvar pain described as burning, rawness, and irritation without visible pathology. Pain constant but variable in intensity. Dyspareunia severe; sexual activity avoided.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sitting intolerance, particularly with pressure on perineum. Symptom triggered by tight clothing. Standing and walking preferred over prolonged sitting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive vulvar pain over 3 years with no identified cause despite extensive gynecologic evaluation. Multiple treatments attempted without sustained relief. Pain affecting relationship and psychological well-being.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Normal vulvar skin appearance; no obvious dermatologic pathology. Pain reproduction with light touch to vulva (allodynia). No visible erythema, ulceration, or lesion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Vulvoscopy unremarkable; biopsy if performed shows only scattered perivascular inflammation with no infection, malignancy, or lichenoid changes. No neuromas or abnormal innervation on histology.

puzzles.explanation: Vulvodynia requires multidisciplinary approach including pelvic floor physical therapy, topical and systemic pain management, neuromodulating medications, cognitive behavioral therapy for pain and relationship impacts, and validation of often-dismissed symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Parent notices child walks with stiffness and one-sided weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Right hand function limited; child prefers left hand despite being right-handed family member.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Child cannot climb stairs or run smoothly; fatigues easily during play compared to peers.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right-sided hemiparesis with increased tone; right foot in plantarflexion with tiptoe gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hyperreflexia in right lower limb; positive Babinski sign bilaterally; scissoring gait pattern observed; minimal right hand dexterity with fisted posture at rest.

puzzles.explanation: Spastic hemiplegia is the most common form of CP, affecting one side of the body more than the other, often from unilateral perinatal brain injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Office worker reports low back or mid-back pain that develops during work hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting and poor posture; improves with movement breaks and stretching.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 6-12 months ago; patient reports no trauma or prior back history. Does not improve with rest days or weekends off.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited thoracic rotation and extension; pain reproduces with sustained slouching posture held for 5 minutes. Abdominal strength graded 4/5; lumbopelvic stability impaired.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI lumbar spine normal; no disc herniation, spondylosis, or instability. Postural analysis via photography shows loss of normal lumbar lordosis and anterior pelvic tilt pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome shows work-related positional pain from sustained postural stress in office setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old with recurrent pneumonia and chronic cough dating back to infancy presents for evaluation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot keep up with peers during play. Morning cough productive. Recurrent ear infections and sinusitis.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Neonatal intensive care admission for prematurity and respiratory distress. Three episodes of pneumonia by age 5. Immotile cilia suspect.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Clubbing noted in fingers. Bilateral fine end-inspiratory crackles at lung bases. Hyperinflation evident on auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: HRCT chest shows dilated bronchi with bronchus-to-artery ratio exceeding 1 in bilateral lower lobes, bronchial wall thickening, and bronchiolectasis. Bronchiectasis pattern is cylindrical.

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric bronchiectasis from primary ciliary dysfunction (PCD). Early-onset recurrent infections from infancy and associated features (chronic sinusitis, situs inversus possibility) are clues.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic male with anterior hip pain during high-demand activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with deep hip flexion and internal rotation; mechanical catching sensation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent episodes over several years with periods of relative relief. Training modification sometimes helps briefly, but symptoms return with intensity. No prior hip surgery or significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive FADIR test, limited internal rotation in flexion. Posterior hip tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging confirms cam morphology with alpha angle >60 degrees; acetabular coverage normal. Labral pathology evident on high-resolution sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Cam-type FAI involves a focal femoral head-neck deformity causing labral impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male retired painter presents with difficulty lifting his arm and a deep ache on the outside of his upper arm that worsens at night.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot reach overhead to change a light bulb. Reports a 'sudden worsening' event 3 months ago when lifting a heavy bucket, though he's had on-and-off problems for years.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of overhead work as a painter. Progressive weakness in the affected arm. Difficulty with hair washing and dressing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive drop arm test. Weakness in external rotation and abduction. Positive empty can test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the supraspinatus tendon (2cm retraction) with early fatty infiltration of the muscle belly (Goutallier grade 2). Associated superior labral fraying.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic degenerative rotator cuff tear with acute-on-chronic deterioration, positive lag and drop arm signs, and MRI showing full-thickness supraspinatus tear with muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Chronic construction worker reports progressive finger pain, numbness, and color changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot grip or manipulate objects when hands are cold; symptoms worsen significantly in winter months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Persistent symptoms despite ergonomic modifications and tool changes; progressive despite reducing vibration exposure in past 3 years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant thenar and hypothenar muscle wasting; two-point discrimination >10mm in digits 2-5; blanching follows typical asymmetric pattern (index/middle > ring/little).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Doppler ultrasound shows intermittent digital artery occlusion on cold challenge; EMG/NCS demonstrates demyelinating pattern consistent with vibration-induced neuropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Vibration white finger shows advanced hand-arm vibration syndrome with significant vascular and neurological damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old male presents with progressive inability to fully straighten his right ring and little fingers. He cannot lay his hand flat on a table.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty putting his hand in his pocket, pulling on gloves, and shaking hands. The contracture has gradually worsened over 2 years. No pain associated with the condition.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Northern European descent. History of heavy alcohol use (now sober 5 years). Type 2 diabetes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable firm nodule and cord in the palmar fascia extending to the ring finger. Fixed flexion contracture at the MCP joint (40 degrees) and PIP joint (25 degrees) of the ring finger. Positive Hueston tabletop test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Clinical diagnosis — imaging not typically required. Ultrasound shows hypoechoic nodule in the palmar aponeurosis with fibrous cord extension. No imaging features of tenosynovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Dupuytren's contracture with classic ring/little finger involvement, palmar nodules and cords, Hueston tabletop test, and risk factors of Northern European ancestry, alcohol, diabetes, and family history.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain and stiffness that worsened acutely 1 week ago. Swelling present. Protective guarding limiting active movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to achieve terminal knee extension or full flexion. Limp noted during gait. Difficulty with stairs and rising from sitting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent invasive knee procedure performed for investigation of chronic knee pain. No structural damage identified intra-operatively. Expected recovery window 4-6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee flexion 85°, extension lag 10°. Soft end-feel to flexion. Moderate suprapatellar effusion palpable. Quadriceps strength 4/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Arthroscopic visualization confirmed intact menisci and cruciate ligaments. Patellofemoral joint showed focal cartilage softening. No loose bodies or synovitis noted.

puzzles.explanation: Simple diagnostic knee arthroscopy recovery is typically rapid with early range of motion restoration, minimal pain, and quick return to function when no significant intra-articular pathology is identified.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent fatigue and malaise that has worsened over recent months despite adequate rest. General sense of low energy affecting daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain work shifts or household activities; becomes exhausted after minimal exertion; struggles with cognitive tasks and concentration throughout day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent history of cancer diagnosis and completion of chemotherapy/radiation approximately 3–6 months ago. Fatigue onset coincided with treatment and has persisted through recovery phase.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Six-minute walk test shows reduced distance covered compared to pre-morbid baseline. Grip strength reduced bilaterally. Heart rate rises excessively with minimal exertion. Mood screening indicates depression/anxiety symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cardiac imaging normal; metabolic panels show iron deficiency and low vitamin D. Inflammatory markers (CRP, cytokines) elevated. Thyroid function normal. All findings consistent with post-treatment recovery state rather than primary cardiac or metabolic disease.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue shows persistent post-treatment fatigue from late effects and deconditioning in cancer survivor.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 65-year-old man reports tremor in right hand that is worse at rest and improves with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Slow movements when picking up objects, difficulty with fine motor tasks, stooped posture when standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tremor began 2 years ago and is slowly progressive. Family member noticed loss of facial expression. Writing becoming smaller.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting tremor (pill-rolling), cogwheel rigidity, bradykinesia, masked facies, shuffling gait with reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DaTscan shows significantly reduced dopamine uptake in bilateral striatum, confirming dopaminergic denervation. MRI brain normal, ruling out structural or secondary causes.

puzzles.explanation: Classic idiopathic Parkinson's disease with rest tremor, rigidity, bradykinesia, and characteristic gait disturbance in typical age group.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old breast cancer survivor presents with progressive dyspnea and fatigue 18 months after completing anthracycline-based chemotherapy.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Orthopnea and lower limb edema noted. Symptoms worse when lying flat; legs swell by evening after minimal activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cumulative anthracycline dose 450 mg/m². No prior cardiac history, hypertension, or diabetes. No recent infection or fever. Symptoms began insidiously 12 months post-treatment completion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild tachycardia, normal blood pressure. Baseline lung exam clear. Displaced apex beat.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiogram shows EF 38% (baseline 60% at end of treatment). Global hypokinesis without regional wall motion abnormality. Diastolic dysfunction present.

puzzles.explanation: Anthracycline-induced cardiotoxicity with late-onset systemic symptoms and reduced ejection fraction. Cumulative dose near threshold for cardiotoxicity. Requires initiation of HF therapy and close monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athletic patient reports diffuse body pain triggered by minor injuries that don't resolve.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to baseline training; minimal exertion produces pain lasting days, leading to progressive deconditioning and fear-avoidance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset following concussion or viral illness; accompanied by cognitive difficulties and persistent fatigue.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender points elicited across all body quadrants with minimal pressure; normal strength and reflexes; no focal neurological deficits or signs of structural damage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Normal structural imaging; functional neuroimaging shows abnormal pain processing in central nervous system.

puzzles.explanation: Fibromyalgia shows post-traumatic or post-infectious onset in younger athlete with prominent central sensitization and neurocognitive symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with anterior knee pain that is difficult to localize, pain quality variable. Intermittent mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered unpredictably by knee flexion activities (stairs, squatting, prolonged sitting). Swelling or catching sensation may accompany episodes but not always present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic intermittent symptoms over months to years. No clear traumatic onset. Prior physiotherapy and anti-inflammatory interventions provided temporary relief only. Imaging workup negative for meniscal tear, ACL injury, or patellofemoral osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along medial joint line and suprapatellar region. Knee range of motion normal. Lachman, anterior drawer, McMurray tests negative. No effusion. Plica-specific tests (flexion-extension with medial palpation) elicit pain inconsistently; reproduction not reliable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates thickened, fibrotic plica impinging on patellofemoral joint space. Arthroscopic visualization confirms plica with synovial irritation and patellofemoral cartilage reaction. Plica resection resolves symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Diagnosis of plica syndrome requires careful exclusion of other pathology and should be made with caution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old new mother reports wrist pain that started 6 weeks after her baby was born. She finds it increasingly difficult to grip things.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: is worst when lifting her baby with an open-hand grip and when wringing out washcloths. Gripping. Twisting movements are particularly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms are worst when lifting her baby with thumb abducted and wrist extended, and when wringing out wet cloths. Fine motor tasks like fastening buttons also provoke pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The pain developed insidiously after hospital discharge. She has been performing frequent repetitive hand activities (baby care, bottle preparation, laundry). No prior wrist injuries. No systemic inflammatory disease history.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Localized tenderness and mild swelling over the radial styloid process. Finkelstein test strongly positive. Crepitus palpable over the first dorsal compartment during thumb abduction and wrist extension.

puzzles.explanation: De Quervain's tenosynovitis is common in new mothers due to repetitive lifting and holding. The Finkelstein test is the hallmark clinical test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old female with recurrent episodes of severe spinning sensation lasting 20 minutes to several hours, accompanied by nausea and vomiting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During attacks: completely unable to move or work; nausea and vomiting severe. Between episodes: fully functional, though reports progressive difficulty hearing conversation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recurrent episodes over 6 months. Progressive unilateral hearing loss, particularly low frequencies. Fluctuating tinnitus (roaring quality).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During acute attack: vertical and rotatory nystagmus present. Weber test shows conductive-pattern hearing loss on affected side. Between episodes: nystagmus resolves, Weber normalizes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss at low frequencies bilaterally, worse on affected side. Caloric testing reveals reduced caloric response on affected side. MRI normal — rules out acoustic neuroma.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's Disease shows the classic tetrad of episodic vertigo, fluctuating hearing loss, tinnitus, and aural fullness in a middle-aged female with recurrent attacks.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with ankle pain following inversion injury; symptoms persist beyond typical resolution timeframe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting/pivoting movements; pain and instability with activities requiring ankle rotation. Difficulty with weight-bearing on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial injury diagnosis may have been missed, slow recovery. Previous ankle sprain complications, recurrent episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness present, positive squeeze test. with dorsiflexion-external rotation, positive crossover test. Weight-bearing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms chronic syndesmotic inflammation and ligamentous thickening. Stress radiographs demonstrate persistent tibiofibular widening compared to contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic high ankle sprain symptoms may result from incomplete healing or missed associated injuries.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young adult (25 years old) with progressive lower limb weakness causing frequent falls and difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Marked sensory loss in hands and feet affecting proprioception and fine motor tasks. Difficulty with balance in darkness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Noticed foot deformities as teenager. Father has similar condition. Slowly progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Stocking-glove sensory loss (vibration and proprioception worse distally). Widespread areflexia. Peroneal nerve thickening palpable in legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows demyelinating neuropathy with slowed conduction velocities and genetic testing shows PMP22 duplication.

puzzles.explanation: Type 1 CMT with demyelinating features, characteristic distal lower extremity involvement, and inherited pattern confirmed by genetics.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Master's level athlete with anterior knee pain localised below the kneecap, persistent despite training load reduction, affecting jumping and sprinting performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Explosive movements like jumping, bounding, and rapid deceleration are highly provocative; symptoms worsen with repeated plyometric activity and running on hard surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms have persisted for over 18 months despite physiotherapy, corticosteroid injections, and multiple rest periods. No significant improvement with conservative management. Patient reports occasional swelling and stiffness on mornings after high-load training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Focal nodularity palpable in tendon, significant with resisted knee extension, and eccentric quadriceps loading highly provocative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows chronic tendinopathy with significant structural disorganization and possible tears or partial rupture. Considerable degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic patellar tendinopathy in mature athletes may show structural damage resistant to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly patient reports gradual blurring of central vision over months, affecting ability to perform fine tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot read small print or watch television comfortably. Difficulty navigating in low light and bumping into objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 78. Long-standing smoker. Mother had late-onset vision problems requiring low-vision aids.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced central vision, scotoma in central field, and drusen present on macula.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: OCT shows macular drusen and atrophy consistent with age-related macular degeneration.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related macular degeneration causes central vision loss affecting reading and recognition, with significant functional impact.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports medial-sided knee pain that began insidiously 2-3 weeks ago, no history of trauma or acute injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: with stairs descending, running, symptoms worse with hip adduction, internal rotation, and morning stiffness present.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent history of repetitive running or gym activity, prior knee or hip surgery, or persistent hip/hamstring stiffness despite stretching.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness maximal over proximal medial tibia, pain with resisted hip adduction and internal rotation; hip flexibility significantly limited (adductor and hamstring tightness); knee effusion absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI shows bursal fluid, tendon changes, bursa may be thickened, and associated muscle strain visible.

puzzles.explanation: Pes anserine bursitis results from inflammation at the insertion of hamstring and adductor tendons.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall swelling and pain at the second costochondral junction on the left side. The swelling appeared suddenly several days ago and has been progressively enlarging.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 28-year-old male presents with acute onset of localised chest wall pain and visible swelling. Symptoms began several days ago and have been progressively worsening.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He had a mild upper respiratory tract infection with a productive cough two weeks prior. He denies any chest trauma or heavy manual work. Of note, there is no fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms that would suggest a sinister aetiology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: A visible, firm, fusiform swelling is palpable at the second costochondral junction measuring approximately 3cm. The swelling is warm and tender to direct palpation. Respiratory examination is otherwise clear, and cardiovascular examination is unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray is normal with no cardiopulmonary pathology. CT of the chest wall confirms focal non-suppurative inflammatory swelling of the costal cartilage at the second costochondral junction, with no features of malignancy or infection. These findings, in combination with the characteristic clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Tietze syndrome is a severe inflammatory presentation of costochondritis with visible swelling requiring imaging confirmation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged athlete noticing a clicking sensation in the hip during running and jumping; pain gradually worsening over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform running drills or high-intensity hip movements without triggering the clicking sensation; limiting participation in sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Started 6 weeks ago after ramping up training volume; initially just a painless click, now sharp pain with certain hip movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable snapping sensation directly over greater trochanter on passive hip flexion/extension with external rotation; no hip ROM restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows tensor fasciae latae snapping over trochanter; may see underlying bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Extra-articular snapping from the ITB can become symptomatic with associated bursitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive arm swelling and heaviness over the past 2 years. Symptoms began following left breast surgery. Recurrent skin infections have become increasingly problematic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform overhead activities, carrying, or prolonged use of affected arm. Significant functional loss in activities of daily living despite conservative treatment attempts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Left-sided mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection 2 years ago. Patient completed adjuvant radiation therapy to the chest wall. Attempted early compression therapy and exercise without sustained improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pitting edema in proximal arm, non-pitting in forearm and hand. Skin thickened and fibrotic. Stemmer sign positive. Circumferential measurements show 4-5 cm difference compared to contralateral limb. Evidence of old cellulitis scars.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates delayed tracer clearance and asymmetric drainage pattern. Absence of axillary lymph node activity on affected side. Ultrasound confirms diffuse subcutaneous fibrosis consistent with chronic lymphostasis.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic stage 2-3 lymphedema requires intensive compression therapy, potential surgical intervention (lymphaticovenous anastomosis or vascularized lymph node transfer consideration), infection prevention, skin care, and psychological support for functional limitation and appearance concerns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Newborn with persistent head tilt to one side; parents report asymmetric head position since birth.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty turning head past midline in one direction; resists passive rotation away from affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficult breech extraction at delivery; birth weight 3.8 kg with large frame.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Firm, non-tender mass palpable along sternocleidomastoid; passive rotation to opposite side blocked; no apparent neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows sternocleidomastoid muscle echogenicity increased with muscle thickening; fibrosis present.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital muscular torticollis results from sternocleidomastoid muscle injury during birth, causing contracture and head posture abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Active individual with gradual onset of pain affecting work performance over several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain repetitive gripping and fine motor tasks; symptoms worsen throughout work shifts.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decade of assembly-line work with poor desk ergonomics; symptoms started in dominant hand and have progressively spread proximally over 18 months despite rest periods.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive nerve compression signs at wrist and forearm; weakness in finger extension and grip; tenderness along flexor-pronator mass; sensory changes in radial distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows nerve swelling at multiple compression points (carpal tunnel, pronator teres region); selective atrophy of thenar and first dorsal interosseous muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational overuse syndrome shows chronic multi-site involvement from years of heavy manufacturing work.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 52-year-old woman with pain in multiple joints, particularly hands and feet. Symptoms worse in the morning, with stiffness that takes 2 hours to improve. Symmetric distribution.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Symptoms are worst in the mornings before activity, then gradually improve throughout the day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset 6 months ago. Progressive functional decline. Smoking history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable warmth and soft tissue swelling at MCPs and PIPs bilaterally. Morning stiffness reproduced on examination. No nodules or deformity palpated.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays demonstrate uniform joint space narrowing across multiple MCPs and PIPs with periarticular osteopenia. Serology positive for rheumatoid factor and anti-CCP antibodies.

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis is the classic presentation of early RA in a middle-aged woman with symmetric polyarticular involvement, morning stiffness, and positive serology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adult with groin or hip pain, pain worse with activity and improving with rest. Morning stiffness present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with loaded hip flexion (stairs, car transfers, bending) and sustained walking. Pain particularly felt in groin, worse later in day with fatigue. Can walk short distances without difficulty.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset of pain, possible previous hip injury or surgery. BMI elevated, pain progressively worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited hip internal rotation and flexion, pain at end-range hip movements. Trendelenburg gait present, pain with log-roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays show joint space narrowing, osteophytes, and sclerosis characteristic of osteoarthritis.

puzzles.explanation: Hip osteoarthritis causes progressive cartilage loss and is common with aging and previous injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with a significant smoking history presents with sudden onset severe shortness of breath and loss of consciousness. Onset was abrupt during a routine hospital visit.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to walk more than 10 meters without severe breathlessness. Cannot lie flat. One syncopal episode occurred with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent immobilization for chemotherapy over past 3 weeks. No recent surgery or fracture. No family history of clotting disorders. Patient noted calf swelling two days ago but dismissed it.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severely hypoxemic (O2 sat 78%), hypotensive, tachycardic. Distended neck veins. Right heart strain signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT chest with contrast shows filling defects within bilateral pulmonary arteries. Echocardiogram reveals acute RV dilation with reduced function. Elevated troponin and D-dimer markedly elevated.

puzzles.explanation: Massive PE presentation in cancer patient with hemodynamic instability and right ventricular failure. Malignancy is significant thrombosis risk, and presentation requires urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Athlete reports bilateral shin pain limiting training capacity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate impact loading; pain worsens with increases in training volume or intensity, improving with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic symptoms from high-volume training; poor recovery between sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral tenderness along medial tibial border; pain reproducible on palpation of posteromedial tibia. No focal bony step-off or point tenderness. Calf strength intact; tibialis posterior and soleus demonstrate mild weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bilateral periosteal changes on imaging, no fracture line; significant training-related stress evident.

puzzles.explanation: Shin splints shows bilateral medial tibial stress syndrome from sustained high training volume.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 70-year-old female presents day 1 post-operatively with significant right knee pain and marked loss of bending ability. She reports difficulty with basic mobility tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to independently bed-to-chair transfer. Marked quadriceps inhibition with significant extensor lag noted. Active knee flexion approximately 70 degrees.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Four-year history of progressive, treatment-resistant tricompartmental knee osteoarthritis. Pre-operative ROM 5–95 degrees. BMI 33, no significant comorbidities affecting healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wound clean and dry, staples intact. Moderate swelling present. Palpable soft-tissue resistance at end-range; pain limits further flexion testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-ray shows well-aligned cemented total knee prosthesis with no evidence of periprosthetic fracture. Component alignment within acceptable parameters on AP and lateral views.

puzzles.explanation: Day 1 post-TKR rehabilitation focusing on early mobilisation, ROM recovery, quadriceps activation, and discharge planning — the standard acute post-surgical physiotherapy pathway.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old runner reports medial foot pain that has developed gradually over four months, worse with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to run; pain limits prolonged standing. Experiences foot instability during walking, particularly on uneven surfaces.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recalled specific injury; pain developed without trauma. Patient reports family history of flat feet and hypermobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Single-leg stance reveals marked arch collapse. Navicular drop test ≥8 mm. Dorsiflexion and eversion weakness noted. Pain on spring ligament palpation between medial malleolus and navicular.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows ligament disruption; ultrasound demonstrates gap in ligament.

puzzles.explanation: Spring ligament injury compromises arch support and can lead to progressive flatfoot deformity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old female reports sensation of heaviness and dragging in the lower pelvis, worse by end of day. Reports difficulty with bowel emptying.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged standing or walking without worsening symptoms. Finds relief only when horizontal. Manages light activities but avoids leaving home due to concern about symptom progression during day.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three vaginal deliveries with difficult labor. Chronic constipation and straining over 40 years. Post-menopausal for 20 years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Grade 3 cystocele with anterior wall prolapsing beyond introitus. Significant rectocele with posterior wall prolapse. Weak perineal body.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic MRI with straining shows extensive pelvic floor descent. Defecography demonstrates rectocele. Cystocele extends beyond introitus.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Organ Prolapse shows severe multicompartment prolapse (anterior and posterior) in an elderly female with long history of straining and childbirth trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports posterior knee swelling with associated calf pain and tightness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to fully extend knee; symptoms worse with prolonged standing and walking. Occasional acute exacerbations with sudden increase in calf swelling and pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of gradual onset swelling over months to years. Patient reports episodic knee pain exacerbated by activity. Recent increase in symptoms following increased activity level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable mass in popliteal fossa, tender on deep palpation. Knee flexion limited to 90 degrees. Primary knee joint crepitus and mild effusion present. Calf soft tissue swelling present but neurovascular exam normal, capillary refill brisk.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows large cyst with possible rupture, fluid extravasation into calf. Underlying knee joint changes associated with OA or meniscal.

puzzles.explanation: Ruptured Baker's cyst can mimic serious conditions and produce calf symptoms requiring careful differential diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old football player experiences sudden burning pain down arm after tackle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift arm overhead or abduct shoulder; grip strength markedly reduced on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute impact during contact sport and neck distraction or shoulder compression mechanism.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Deltoid and rotator cuff weakness; sensory loss in C5-C6 distribution; intact motor branches distally; no Horner's sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI may show nerve edema, often normal in neurapraxia, and assess for avulsion in severe cases.

puzzles.explanation: Acute brachial plexus neurapraxia (stinger) from contact sport mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient with midfoot pain following an ankle injury months ago. Pain worsening despite initial treatment.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate single-leg stance or push off during walking. Pain significantly limits running and jumping activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute inversion ankle injury 12 months ago. Initially treated conservatively for ankle sprain, then discharged. Midfoot pain began 2 months post-injury and has not resolved despite rest and physio.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive midfoot abduction stress test (forefoot abducted passively with hindfoot fixed). Increased midfoot hypermobility compared to contralateral side. No tenderness over syndesmosis or anterior ankle. Subtle increase in height of medial midfoot arch bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing X-rays show increased gap between first and second cuneiforms (>5mm). CT demonstrates articular surface irregularity at the tarsometatarsal joints with early post-traumatic OA. No acute fracture; findings consistent with chronic ligamentous disruption.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Lisfranc injury results from missed or inadequately treated acute injury causing long-term dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly patient presenting with marked functional decline and persistent cognitive slowing weeks after hospitalization.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk without assistance, requires supervision for ADLs, forgets recent instructions despite appearing alert.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent hospitalization with sepsis and delirium resolved but recovery slow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Proximal weakness (hip flexors 3/5), gait slow and unsteady, attention span <2 minutes, unable to follow 3-step commands reliably.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows no structural cause and cognitive testing shows impairment.

puzzles.explanation: Post-delirium recovery requires intensive rehabilitation for deconditioning and persistent cognitive effects.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Active person with mid-back pain that worsens with certain movements during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead or rotational movements required for sport; restrictions noted in morning, ease during activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Activity-related, training demands exceed mobility, and responsive to intervention.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Segmental restriction in mid-thoracic extension and rotation; rib cage symmetry asymmetrical on one side. Neurological examination and strength normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows normal alignment and no fracture. CT or MRI unnecessary; clinical presentation and movement pattern restriction confirm functional origin.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction is activity-related functional limitation responsive to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child with previous normal neck development now has head tilt.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Recently limited neck rotation, prefers head in one position, and possible dystonia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot rotate neck fully to one side; head held in fixed position despite attempts to correct.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Acute onset following viral illness or fever; recent history of upper respiratory infection or vaccination; no prior neck trauma.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Unilateral sternocleidomastoid or trapezius muscle spasm/hypertonia; head tilt away from affected muscle; neck extension and ipsilateral rotation limited; pain on palpation of affected muscle; no clear trigger point or mechanical restriction.

puzzles.explanation: Acquired later-onset torticollis may reflect dystonia or CNS pathology requiring metabolic and genetic investigation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old male athlete presents with intermittent knee pain that has been worsening over the past three months. He plays competitive basketball and notices the knee sometimes catches or locks during games.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by running, jumping, and pivoting movements. He reports occasional episodes where the knee feels like it locks momentarily before releasing. There is mild swelling after intense activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No specific traumatic injury recalled. The symptoms began insidiously and have gradually progressed. He has increased his training load significantly over the past six months in preparation for the competitive season.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: There is tenderness on palpation of the medial femoral condyle. Wilson's test is positive, with pain on internal rotation of the tibia during extension that resolves with external rotation. A small effusion is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals a well-defined osteochondral lesion of the medial femoral condyle with evidence of partial fragment separation. The overlying cartilage shows signs of instability with fluid signal tracking beneath the fragment.

puzzles.explanation: Osteochondritis dissecans most commonly affects adolescent athletes, with the medial femoral condyle being the most frequent site. Wilson's test is a useful clinical screening tool, and MRI is essential for staging the lesion and guiding management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee swelling and pain 6 weeks after reconstructive surgery of the knee. Quadriceps weakness noted with difficulty walking. Minimal sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking on flat surfaces improving but stairs remain challenging. Difficulty with pivoting and cutting movements. Unable to bear weight on affected leg for dynamic activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ACL tear sustained 6 weeks ago during soccer with pivot-shift mechanism. Concomitant meniscal tear. Treated surgically. No prior knee surgery or significant past medical history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Effusion present, Lachman test shows improved stability compared to acute injury. Anterior drawer test moderately positive. Quadriceps lag evident strength approximately 4-/5 and range of motion limited in extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows appropriate graft positioning and early integration into bone tunnels. No graft rupture or retear. Associated meniscal repair healing appropriately.

puzzles.explanation: ACLR rehabilitation requires progressive loading phases respecting graft healing timelines, aggressive quadriceps activation and strengthening, hamstring conditioning for dynamic stability, proprioceptive training, and sport-specific preparation for safe return to activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman with known osteoporosis presents with sudden severe pain in the right hip and groin after a fall from standing at home. She cannot stand or walk.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to internally rotate or adduct the hip. Pain is significantly worse with any attempt to move the leg into these ranges compared to other movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Osteoporosis, postmenopausal. Fall from standing, multiple prior fractures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexed, abducted and externally rotated at rest. Severe pain on internal rotation and adduction. Log roll test produces severe groin pain. No crepitus. Neurological exam intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray demonstrates a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture with varus angulation and shortening.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporotic femoral neck fracture from minor trauma in elderly with severe bone loss requires surgical fixation and osteoporosis management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older adolescent with chronic thoracic pain and postural concerns.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty maintaining upright posture; pain worsens with prolonged sitting or activities requiring trunk extension; forward-curved posture develops over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive kyphosis noticed by parents, symptomatic since teen years, and adult residual symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Rigid thoracic kyphosis with loss of extension ROM; vertebral segments appear stiff rather than mobile; posterior chain tightness; pain reproducible at apical kyphotic level on palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show anterior vertebral body wedging (≥5° across multiple consecutive thoracic vertebrae), irregular endplates, and loss of disc space height—consistent with resolved Schmorl's nodes and mature structural change.

puzzles.explanation: Scheuermann's disease presents in an older adolescent with mature structural changes and chronic pain pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational golfer presents with gradual onset inner arm pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with grasping, throwing, or flexing the wrist and relief with rest and immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive gripping activities at work over the past 3 years, with pain onset following a weekend golf tournament. Previous left shoulder impingement treated conservatively 2 years ago, now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on palpation of inner bony prominence and pain with resisted wrist flexion and mild weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon inflammation, thickening, and X-rays show no fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Classic medial epicondylitis from golf-related repetitive flexion and pronation activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 68-year-old female presenting with pain and swelling at the knee, with difficulty regaining movement after recent major surgery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty achieving heel-to-buttock flexion and full knee extension. Struggles with stairs and sit-to-stand transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe osteoarthritis resistant to 6+ months of physiotherapy and injections. Open surgical approach used. Patient reports good pre-operative functional level relative to disease severity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant effusion present. Knee flexion 60 degrees, extension lag 10 degrees. Quadriceps inhibition evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show appropriate prosthetic alignment. Component position good. No instability.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Knee Replacement shows early post-operative period (3 weeks) with expected swelling, stiffness, and need for intensive rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient reports a painful area of skin that has become red, swollen, and tender over the past week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to reposition or change position independently; spending 12+ hours daily in same position on a single surface.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prolonged immobility, inadequate nutrition. Diabetes, infection risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Open wound with partial or full thickness loss, exposed dermis or deeper tissue. Purulent drainage.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound or MRI shows tissue depth and any undermining; wound culture positive if infected.

puzzles.explanation: Deep pressure injuries with tissue loss require aggressive wound care, offloading, and management of infection to promote healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old office worker presents with persistent mid-thoracic stiffness and aching that has worsened over six months. She describes a constant dull ache between the shoulder blades that worsens after prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain posture for extended periods; pain increases with reaching forward or overhead activities; some relief with manual therapy.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No specific traumatic event; symptoms coincided with new desk setup and remote work three months ago; no previous spinal issues; denies trauma or falls.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced thoracic rotation, tenderness over mid-thoracic segments, and no neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging usually normal and functional restriction without structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic spine dysfunction is mechanical restriction from postural and movement factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Elderly with persistent hip pain after surgery for fracture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to weight-bear or ambulate without pain and instability; functionally immobile.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Proximal femoral fracture fixed 12 weeks ago with internal fixation. Recent onset of increasing hip pain and giving way despite apparent initial healing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked pain with hip flexion/abduction; positive Trendelenburg sign; audible clunk on hip rotation under load; no neurovascular compromise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows lateral cortical perforation of proximal femur; loss of fracture line reduction; severe osteoporosis limits revision options.

puzzles.explanation: Subtrochanteric fracture complications from osteoporosis may require revision surgery with different fixation strategy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with progressive leg weakness and difficulty with breathing that worsens toward evening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot climb stairs, requiring mechanical ventilation at night, and weakness improves after rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Started with eye drooping 1 year ago, progressed to generalized weakness over months. Recently hospitalized for acute respiratory decline requiring intubation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ptosis and ophthalmoplegia bilaterally. Weakness of facial and bulbar muscles (nasal speech, difficulty with chewing). Strength deficits worse after sustained muscle contraction. Tensilon test shows transient improvement in strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Repetitive nerve stimulation demonstrates >10% decremental response in proximal muscles. Anti-acetylcholine receptor and anti-MuSK antibodies negative.

puzzles.explanation: Seronegative myasthenia gravis with generalized weakness, respiratory involvement, and characteristic decremental response on repetitive stimulation testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: School-age child reports feeling clumsy and uncoordinated. Struggles with balance and dislikes certain clothing textures, bright lights, and loud noises.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot participate in organised team sports due to coordination difficulties. Prefers solitary or structured activities. Trips frequently and seems to lack spatial awareness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of developmental delay identified at age 3. Currently receiving occupational therapy and speech pathology support. No specific injury or recent change in function reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait pattern irregular and energy-inefficient. Difficulty coordinating bilateral limbs during crossing midline activities. Hyper-responsive to light touch; defensive reactions to textures. Proprioceptive testing shows reduced body awareness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Functional MRI demonstrates altered connectivity in motor coordination and sensory integration networks. Standardised motor development scales confirm delays in gross and fine motor milestones.

puzzles.explanation: Autism with motor coordination deficits requires comprehensive evaluation for dyspraxia and sensory processing differences alongside social-communication support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old female with recurrent episodes of room-spinning vertigo lasting seconds to minutes. Triggered by lying down or rolling in bed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo occurs with specific positional changes: head tilted back, rolling in bed, looking up. Can predict and avoid triggering positions. Between episodes, completely normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient avoids head movements and certain positions to prevent vertigo. Between episodes, able to walk, stand, and perform ADLs normally. Hesitant to lie flat or turn in bed independently due to fear of triggering attack.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vertigo onset insidious, 3 weeks ago. No recent head trauma or significant illness. No prior balance problems or vestibular disorders. Lives alone; symptoms causing anxiety about falling and social withdrawal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hallmark feature: brief (< 60 seconds) rotatory nystagmus with Dix-Hallpike maneuver. No imaging needed typically. Otoconia from utricle confirmed by characteristic nystagmus.

puzzles.explanation: Benign Paroxysmal Positional Vertigo shows recurrent BPPV in an older female after trauma with classic positional trigger and diagnostic Dix-Hallpike response.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with groin and anterior hip pain, worse with prolonged sitting and certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with hip flexion and adduction; limited external rotation in flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over months; no clear traumatic event; symptoms gradually worsened with running and hip-heavy activities; no prior hip surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: FABER test reproduces anterior hip pain; hip external rotation in flexion is limited to 15°; adductor tightness; pain with log-roll test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging shows over-coverage of acetabulum; coxa profunda or acetabular retroversion.

puzzles.explanation: Pincer-type FAI involves overcoverage of the acetabulum, often with retroversion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman in residential aged care presents with progressive unsteadiness and increasing reluctance to mobilise over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She has become increasingly dependent for transfers and walking. Staff report she frequently forgets to use her walking frame and becomes confused about where she is going mid-task.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnosed with a progressive neurodegenerative condition three years ago. Gradual cognitive decline with recent acceleration. Multiple falls over the past six months, now requiring two-person assist for mobilisation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked postural instability with backward tendency on perturbation. Parkinsonian features: rigid trunk, reduced arm swing, shuffling steps with freezing tendency. Minimal arm swing despite instruction to swing arms. Difficulty turning; requires multiple steps to redirect.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT brain shows marked generalised cerebral atrophy with disproportionate frontosubcortical involvement and substantial ventricular dilatation. Basal ganglia appear hypodense. No acute findings. DaTscan shows reduced striatal dopamine uptake bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Progressive cognitive decline leads to mobility deterioration through impaired motor planning, spatial awareness, and safety judgment. Physiotherapy focuses on maintaining function, falls prevention, and carer education.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient experienced sudden onset paraplegia following a crush injury to the lower back at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk or stand; complete loss of lower limb motor control bilaterally.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Crushed between heavy machinery at work. Mechanism suggests high-energy axial or compressive force to the lower spine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe bilateral leg weakness (grade 1/5), saddle anesthesia, absent anal wink, absent bulbocavernosus reflex, and flaccid paralysis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows conus medullaris compression with hemorrhagic infarction and significant edema.

puzzles.explanation: Conus medullaris syndrome from lumbar spine crush injury with bilateral leg paralysis, saddle anesthesia, and complete sphincter loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged patient with progressive involuntary movements and behavioral deterioration over the past 2 years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Tremor and uncoordinated limb movements worsening with intentional tasks; difficulty with fine motor control and balance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mother had similar symptoms and died at age 60. Behavioral issues began 2 years ago, followed by choreiform movements 1 year ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized dance-like involuntary movements, difficulty with smooth eye tracking, mild slowing of thought and speech, preserved strength.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Genetic testing shows CAG repeat >39 in HTT gene. Brain MRI demonstrates caudate atrophy.

puzzles.explanation: Early-stage Huntington's disease with choreiform movements, behavioral disturbance, and positive family history confirmed by genetic testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 55-year-old male with sudden onset severe upper arm pain and visible lump or swelling in the biceps region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Weakness with elbow flexion and supination reduces arm strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He felt a sudden pop in the upper arm while lifting a heavy box overhead at work three days ago. No prior shoulder surgery or rotator cuff history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked weakness with elbow flexion and supination strength testing (grade 2/5). Visible bulge or absence of normal biceps contour in upper arm on inspection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows absent tendon at shoulder. MRI confirms rupture with retracted muscle.

puzzles.explanation: Long head biceps rupture with classic Popeye deformity from maximal effort.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports significant pain in the knee with variable symptoms depending on activity level and time of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking improving with single-point cane support. Knee flexion progressing but extension lag persists; sit-to-stand transfers improving.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of severe knee osteoarthritis refractory to conservative management. Recent surgical intervention within the past month.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Well-healed incision with mild effusion. Active knee flexion approximately 90 degrees with 15-degree extension lag. Quadriceps weakness evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show proper prosthesis positioning with neutral limb alignment. No evidence of loosening.

puzzles.explanation: TKA rehabilitation requires early extension lag correction, progressive range of motion gains, quadriceps strengthening, functional mobility training, and edema management to achieve optimal prosthetic function and patient satisfaction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 3-year-old child with high fever, persistent cough, and rapid breathing for one week. No improvement with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child is breathless even at rest and cannot walk short distances without increased respiratory distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born at term with no perinatal complications. No prior hospital admissions. Currently unwell for four days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, respiratory rate is 62 breaths per minute with subcostal and intercostal recession. Oxygen saturation is 91% on room air. Auscultation reveals coarse crepitations and reduced air entry in the left lower lobe.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows left lower lobe consolidation with air bronchograms. Nasopharyngeal swab positive for respiratory syncytial virus.

puzzles.explanation: Pediatric pneumonia presentation with severe presentation and danger signs requiring hospitalization. Demonstrates how infection impacts children differently with higher respiratory rates and more dramatic clinical signs.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Increasing discharge from surgical site with visible gap opening at wound edges, beginning second post-operative week.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight or perform upper limb loading activities; prone positioning contraindicated due to wound location.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Major surgical procedure 10 days prior with initially unremarkable course. Risk factors include obesity, diabetes, poor nutrition, and immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Partial wound separation with serosanguineous drainage. Surrounding tissue induration with minimal erythema; patient systemically stable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows separation of fascial layers with fluid collection. Early abscess formation possible.

puzzles.explanation: Partial wound dehiscence without frank infection may be managed conservatively with meticulous wound care, negative pressure therapy, nutritional support, and delayed closure, or surgically with repeat closure depending on contamination risk and underlying structure exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young soccer player reports 'seeing stars' and brief confusion after heading the ball forcefully.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sat out game, reports feeling 'foggy' and having trouble remembering play sequences.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Second head impact to same region 3 weeks prior; reported worsening of fogginess and concentration issues since first incident despite return to light training.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: GCS 15, normal neurological exam, normal pupil response, mild headache, no focal deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head normal. ImPACT baseline neurocognitive testing shows decline from pre-season scores in processing speed and reaction time; score consistent with concussive injury.

puzzles.explanation: Grade 1-2 concussion with transient altered consciousness, post-concussive symptoms (fogginess, memory issues), and normal structural imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young professional with persistent groin discomfort interfering with gym workouts.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain triggered by compound movements like squats and lunges; relieved with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious onset over 6 months; no prior hip injury or surgery. Symptoms began after increasing deadlift volume at the gym.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive anterior hip impingement test; pain on flexion-internal rotation combined movement. Hip flexion ROM limited to 100° and internal rotation to 10° in flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: 3D CT imaging demonstrates combined cam and pincer morphology.

puzzles.explanation: Combined FAI types are common and may present with non-specific complaints initially, requiring detailed imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness. A 34-year-old occupational worker develops chest tightness, cough, and wheezing specifically during work shifts and weekends off symptom-free.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient is unable to perform sustained physical exertion; symptoms improve markedly with rest and are worse in the evening after activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has a childhood history of atopy and seasonal allergies. No prior respiratory diagnoses. Symptoms began 6 weeks ago and have progressively worsened. No recent infections or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, diffuse expiratory wheeze is audible on auscultation bilaterally. Peak expiratory flow rate is 52% of predicted. Accessory muscle use is present with mild tachycardia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and no consolidation. Spirometry demonstrates FEV1/FVC ratio of 0.62 with 18% bronchodilator reversibility. Fractional exhaled nitric oxide is elevated at 42 ppb.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma presentation with clear temporal relationship to workplace exposure and improvement with removal from exposure. Requires exposure history and documentation of work-relatedness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult office worker with lower back pain and shooting pain down the leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worse with prolonged sitting and forward bending; improves with lying down. Unable to bend to pick up light objects.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain started after lifting a heavy object; positive lower limb radiation below the knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive straight leg raise test, diminished ankle reflex. Weakness in foot dorsiflexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows posterolateral disc protrusion at L5-S1 level with nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar disc herniation presents as a classic occupational case with clear mechanical triggers and nerve root compression signs on examination and imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports profound fatigue unrelieved by rest, muscle pain, and impaired cognitive function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant post-exertional malaise with disproportionate symptom worsening after minor activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began gradually following a mild viral illness 14 months ago and have progressively worsened. She has no history of prior chronic fatigue, fibromyalgia, or mood disorder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Orthostatic intolerance on tilt-table testing, reduced cardiac output with minimal exertion, cervical lymphadenopathy, and absence of motor weakness or focal neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Low natural killer cell counts, elevated inflammatory markers, abnormal cytokine levels, brain imaging changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic fatigue syndrome shows post-viral onset with post-exertional malaise, cognitive dysfunction, and immunological abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 34-year-old warehouse worker with sudden leg pain following heavy lifting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with sitting, forward bending, and coughing. Partially relieved by lying on side with knees bent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two previous back pain episodes over 5 years without leg symptoms. Smoker with sedentary lifestyle outside work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive straight leg raise at 35 degrees reproducing leg pain. Reduced ankle jerk with weak great toe extension (L5 myotome) and sensory changes in L5 dermatome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows posterolateral disc herniation at L4/L5 compressing the left L5 nerve root.

puzzles.explanation: Classic L4/L5 disc herniation with L5 radiculopathy: dermatomal pain and paraesthesia, weak toe extension, positive SLR, and aggravation with flexion-loading activities.

clues.label: Complaint
