clues.text: Adult patient with acute knee pain after forced flexion or direct anterior tibial blow; minimal to moderate swelling over initial hours.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain with kneeling and deep squatting, symptoms worsen with stairs. Less instability sensation compared to ACL injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury from high-energy mechanism; patient often unaware of complete injury details. May report feeling or hearing a pop at time of injury, with rapid effusion development.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive posterior sag sign at rest, posterior drawer test positive with knee at 90 degrees. Quadriceps strength assessment important.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates PCL fiber disruption with increased T2 signal throughout ligament; proximal or distal avulsion may be present. Associated posterolateral corner injury or medial meniscal tear common.

puzzles.explanation: PCL injuries are often missed on initial assessment because they produce less obvious instability than ACL tears.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Older patient reports gradual-onset difficulty focusing on near objects.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty maintaining clear focus during sustained reading; symptoms worsen with dim lighting and small print sizes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset coincided with patient's mid-50s; she has no prior eye disease, trauma, or systemic comorbidities. Reading glasses were never needed before this.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Near-point accommodation reduced to 33 cm (normal for age ~25 cm). Distance visual acuity normal. Pupillary reflexes intact; no visual field defect.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cycloplegic refraction shows hyperopic shift relative to patient's prior prescription from 5 years ago; lens power insufficient for near-distance focusing.

puzzles.explanation: Presbyopia is age-related loss of accommodation affecting near vision, managed with reading glasses or bifocals.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with recurrent episodes of acute vertigo lasting 2-3 hours, accompanied by unilateral ear fullness and roaring tinnitus. The episodes began 18 months ago and have progressively worsened. Between attacks, she reports fluctuating low-frequency hearing loss and mild persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During acute episodes, she experiences severe nausea with vomiting and is unable to walk safely. She has noticed that exposure to loud noise and crowded environments tends to trigger or worsen symptom clusters. Her symptoms are unpredictable in timing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension is managed on medication. She reports that high salt intake and periods of stress seem to cluster her symptom episodes. She denies recent head injury or upper respiratory infection. No known family history of similar vestibular or hearing disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, during an acute episode, horizontal nystagmus is present with characteristic rotatory component. Romberg test shows significant instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver does not reproduce vertigo. Caloric testing demonstrates reduced vestibular response on the affected side. Audiometry shows sensorineural hearing loss pattern in low frequencies.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the temporal bones with high-resolution sequences shows asymmetric enlargement of the membranous labyrinth (endolymphatic hydrops) on the affected side. No mass at the cerebellopontine angle. No demyelinating lesions or other structural abnormalities.

puzzles.explanation: Ménière's Disease shows bilateral disease (affecting 15-30% of cases) with progressive hearing loss and vestibular dysfunction on both sides requiring comprehensive management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man presents with progressive low back and hip pain over 2 years, which has significantly impacted his mobility and independence. He reports increasing bone pain in the lumbar spine and proximal femur, particularly with weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He demonstrates a slightly widened gait and reports pain with prolonged standing. Walking distances have diminished from 2 miles to 200 meters. He experiences pain relief with rest but finds the pain reoccurs with minimal activity, restricting his previously active lifestyle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a family history of Paget's disease (father was affected). No recent trauma, but he notes gradual increase in shoe size and hat size over the past decade. Laboratory work shows markedly elevated alkaline phosphatase at 285 U/L (normal 30-120) and elevated urinary hydroxyproline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, the lumbar spine and proximal femurs demonstrate localized warmth indicating increased bone vascularity. Direct percussion over affected areas elicits pain. No neurological deficits currently present, though mild lower extremity weakness (4+/5) noted in hip flexors. Bone deformity palpable in lumbar region with mild anterior bowing of left femur.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the lumbar spine and pelvis show characteristic mixed lytic and sclerotic bone lesions with coarse trabecular pattern. The femoral necks and lumbar vertebrae demonstrate cortical thickening and increased bone density. Radionuclide bone scan reveals significantly increased uptake in multiple sites including lumbar spine, pelvis, and proximal femurs.

puzzles.explanation: Paget's disease of bone is a rare metabolic bone disease with progressive deformity and complication risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old office worker presents with eye discomfort and occasional blurred vision that has developed gradually over 3 months. She reports the symptoms worsen as her workday progresses and describes mild frontal headaches in the afternoons. Symptoms are worse on working days than weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Her symptoms are tightly correlated with continuous near-work activity, improving rapidly with breaks and distance viewing. She also reports significantly fewer symptoms during periods away from work, such as weekends and vacation. This pattern has been consistent and reproducible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Transition to full-time remote work 3 months ago coincided with symptom onset. She has noticed reduced blinking when concentrating on her screens. Recent optometric assessment was normal, and she denies any systemic autoimmune or rheumatological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visual acuity is 20/20 bilaterally at distance but becomes 20/40 at near distance (approximately 40 cm). Tear film break-up time is reduced at 4 seconds (normal >5 seconds), indicating aqueous tear deficiency. Ocular surface staining with lissamine green shows scattered punctate epithelial erosions. Accommodation amplitude measured at 10 diopters (expected 12-14 at age 32).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anterior segment optical coherence tomography demonstrates decreased tear meniscus height and thinning of the tear film layer. Meibography reveals mild gland dropout in the lower lid consistent with meibomian gland dysfunction from reduced blinking. No retinal pathology identified on dilated fundoscopy or OCT of optic disc and macula.

puzzles.explanation: Computer vision syndrome shows severe occupational eye strain with accommodation dysfunction and significant vision changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office manager presents with chronic lower back pain and neck stiffness that has developed over 8 months of desk work. She describes gradual onset of central low back discomfort and progressive cervical tightness without any acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain intensifies significantly after prolonged sitting (>2 hours) and improves markedly with movement, stretching, and postural correction. She reports morning stiffness that resolves within 30 minutes of activity. Standing and walking provide symptomatic relief. Pain does not radiate distally and is not aggravated by specific movements or neural tension tests.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with minimal breaks. Recent ergonomic assessment identified workplace risk factors contributing to sustained postural deviation. No history of significant spinal trauma, prior disc pathology, or neurological symptoms. General health unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, postural assessment reveals prominent forward head posture (approximately 3 cm anterior to plumb line) with thoracic kyphosis exaggeration and anterior pelvic tilt. Cervical and lumbar range of motion are full but slightly limited by muscle tightness. Lumbar flexion shows reversal of normal curve but no radicular pain. Straight leg raise 85 degrees bilaterally. Slump test negative. Palpation reveals sustained muscle tension in cervical paraspinals and lumbar erector spinae.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show preserved spinal alignment and disc heights without degenerative changes. MRI demonstrates normal disc signal, intact facet joints, normal canal diameter, and no neural compression. Imaging is structurally normal.

puzzles.explanation: Postural syndrome shows work-related postural pain in manual worker with core dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 27-year-old overhead throwing athlete (baseball pitcher) presents with progressive medial elbow pain over 6 weeks. The pain is localized to the medial aspect of the elbow and has progressively worsened with continued pitching activity despite attempting rest periods.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by throwing, particularly during the late cocking phase and deceleration phase of the pitch. He reports ability to perform other upper extremity activities without pain. Throwing velocity has decreased noticeably, and he cannot maintain control of pitches. Non-throwing activities cause minimal discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He increased his pitching volume significantly 2 months ago without adequate progression in training load. He reports no specific acute traumatic event but rather insidious onset with progressive symptoms. He played through mild pain initially, thinking it would resolve. No previous elbow surgery or significant injury to this extremity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness is elicited directly over the medial collateral ligament origin. The moving valgus stress test reproduces pain (also called dynamic valgus stress test). Milking maneuver is positive, reproducing sharp pain over the ligament. Manual valgus stress at 90 degrees elbow flexion demonstrates increased laxity compared to contralateral side with a soft end-feel. Grip strength is reduced on affected side. No nerve involvement signs on upper limb neurological testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the elbow reveals partial thickness tear of the anterior bundle of the ulnar collateral ligament with associated edema at the ligament origin. High signal intensity within the ligament substance indicates ligamentous disruption. Small joint effusion present. Flexor-pronator origin shows mild edema consistent with associated overuse strain. No osteochondral lesion identified.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic UCL insufficiency from cumulative overhead throwing overuse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman reports persistent hip region pain and difficulty with basic mobility tasks including walking and stairs. Onset was gradual over recent weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient uses a cane and demonstrates significant weakness with hip abduction tasks. She struggles particularly with activities requiring hip flexion beyond 90 degrees or weight-bearing through the affected leg.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent hip surgery via anterolateral approach 8 weeks ago for osteoarthritis. Completed standard rehabilitation protocol. No episodes of catching, locking, or sudden instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates significant hip flexion weakness (3/5) and hip abduction weakness (2+/5). Hip flexion range is 85 degrees limited by pain and stiffness. Hip abduction range is 25 degrees with weakness noted. Hip extension appears functional at 15 degrees. No apparent signs of hip instability with log-roll test or flexion-adduction test. Mild swelling present around anterior surgical site. Sensation intact to light touch throughout lower extremity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show appropriate component positioning with no signs of aseptic loosening, malalignment, or early failure. Imaging does not explain the ongoing weakness and functional loss.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows mid-term recovery (4 months) with good pain outcome but persistent hip weakness limiting functional progress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with a history of recurrent shoulder pain presents with acute worsening of his chronic shoulder discomfort following a fall onto his right shoulder 2 weeks ago. He reports constant aching pain in the lateral shoulder and difficulty with overhead activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: He has marked weakness with attempts to raise his arm overhead, which significantly limits reaching activities and self-care. Sleeping on the affected shoulder is impossible. Passive motion causes less pain than active motion. Pain is worse with lifting, pulling, and throwing activities. He avoids these activities due to weakness and sharp pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has a 2-year history of intermittent right shoulder pain from overuse related to his work as a carpenter. He had not sought formal treatment, managing with over-the-counter analgesics. The acute trauma 2 weeks ago appears to have converted his chronic tendinopathy into a symptomatic tear. He reports no prior shoulder surgery or significant trauma to this shoulder.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, abduction is weak and painful at 3/5. External rotation strength is reduced compared to internal rotation (which is relatively preserved). Drop arm test cannot be completed due to weakness. Supraspinatus tenderness is present on palpation. Apprehension test is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a full-thickness rotator cuff tear of the supraspinatus measuring 1.2 cm in anteroposterior dimension with retraction. Fatty infiltration is present in the supraspinatus muscle at stage 2. The infraspinatus is intact. Acromion is hypertrophic (curved type) contributing to subacromial space narrowing. Acromioclavicular joint osteoarthritis is mild.

puzzles.explanation: Full-thickness rotator cuff tear with acute drop arm sign and weakness on examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old man with a 12-year history of type 2 diabetes presents with progressive bilateral lower limb pain and sensory loss over 18 months. He describes a burning sensation in his feet and difficulty with fine sensory perception.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking distances are limited due to burning pain in his feet, and he reports difficulty with balance and proprioception, particularly in darkness. He has had episodes of stumbling. Nighttime pain significantly disrupts sleep quality. He avoids walking barefoot due to numbness and fear of injury. Standing tolerance is reduced to 15-20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Poor medication adherence has resulted in suboptimal glycemic control (HbA1c 9.2%) throughout his diabetes course. Associated comorbidities include hypertension and hyperlipidaemia. Notably, B12 level is normal and there is no history of chemotherapy, malignancy, or significant trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle reflexes are bilaterally diminished (1+/4). Vibratory sense is markedly reduced at both ankles (perceives only 2/8 seconds of tuning fork). Proprioception is impaired in toes (unable to detect direction of passive toe movement). Two-point discrimination is diminished in feet (distance >1 cm). Monofilament testing shows decreased sensation to 5.07 monofilament in distal lower extremities bilaterally. Motor strength is 4+/5 in foot dorsiflexors with distal lower extremity weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies demonstrate reduced conduction velocities and decreased amplitudes in both peroneal and tibial nerves consistent with demyelination. Distal latencies are prolonged. F-waves are abnormal or absent. Electromyography shows denervation potentials (fibrillations and positive sharp waves) in intrinsic foot muscles, confirming axonal degeneration. MRI of lumbar spine is normal without disc herniation or stenosis contributing to symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexopathy with acute painful onset, proximal weakness, and focal denervation pattern atypical for distal neuropathy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman reports persistent fatigue lasting 4 years that significantly impairs her ability to work and engage in daily activities. She notes that physical exertion, even mild, triggers worsening of symptoms that can last for days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She is now largely housebound and unable to sustain any consistent activity level. Even brief periods of light activity (walking to the mailbox, preparing a meal) result in disproportionate exhaustion lasting 24-48 hours, forcing her to rest significantly afterward.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Her symptoms began insidiously approximately 2 months after a presumed viral illness. Prior to illness, she was an active professional with full work capacity. She reports new-onset difficulties with concentration and short-term memory. She denies depression, major life stressors, or psychiatric illness. Comprehensive metabolic panel, thyroid function, and vitamin levels are normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, mental status is clear and oriented, though she demonstrates slowed processing and occasional difficulty with recall. No signs of joint inflammation or objective muscle weakness (5/5 strength throughout). Neurological examination is otherwise normal. Vital signs show no significant orthostatic hypotension. Cognitive testing reveals mild impairment in attention and processing speed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain is normal with no white matter abnormalities or structural lesions. Metabolic panel, CBC, thyroid, and autoimmune markers (ANA, RF, anti-CCP) are all normal. Sleep study demonstrates non-restorative sleep architecture without obstructive apnea.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic fatigue syndrome shows insidious onset with autonomic dysfunction and significant functional impairment without clear infectious trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old recreational soccer player presents with insidious-onset deep groin pain that began 3 months ago without any specific traumatic event. He describes sharp anterior hip pain with occasional clicking sensation within the hip joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are aggravated by prolonged sitting, particularly with hip flexion greater than 90 degrees, and by cutting and pivoting movements in soccer. He experiences pain with hip flexion-adduction activities. Walking and straight-line running are tolerable but aggravated by direction changes. Pain improves with rest and anti-inflammatory medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: He has no prior hip injuries or surgery. Training volume increased markedly 6 months ago with shorter recovery between matches. Denies any family history of joint disease. Generally fit with no other musculoskeletal complaints. No previous groin or hip symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior impingement test (FADIR - flexion, adduction, internal rotation) reproduces his groin pain. Hip flexion adduction test is positive. Log-roll test is negative. McCarthy test demonstrates reproduction of clicking and pain. Hip range of motion shows mild limitation in internal rotation (25 degrees compared to 35 degrees on contralateral side). Pain does not radiate down the leg. Neurological examination is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip with intra-articular gadolinium demonstrates an anterior-superior labral tear (ASLAP-type tear) at the 1-2 o'clock position. Labral fraying and signal intensity changes indicate labral fiber disruption. No hip joint effusion. The femoral and acetabular cartilage appears intact. No femoroacetabular impingement morphology. No occult fractures or osteonecrosis identified.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior hip labral tear associated with femoroacetabular impingement, presenting with groin pain, clicking, and positive impingement signs in a hypermobile individual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman reports progressive weakness and clumsiness affecting her left arm and leg over the past 6 months. Onset followed a minor respiratory infection. She describes episodes of tremor and difficulty with fine motor control. She is significantly distressed about her symptoms and reports high levels of anxiety.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She reports inability to use her left hand for fine motor tasks but demonstrates inconsistent functional use of the limb in natural settings. Her gait is slow and cautious, but the pattern is variable and non-organic. She reports improvement with distraction and inconsistency between reported symptoms and observed function during casual observation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She experienced significant life stressors approximately 6 months prior to symptom onset, including relationship dissolution and work-related stress. She reports high emotional distress and anxiety. No family history of neurological disease. She is a high-achiever with perfectionist traits. She has seen multiple specialists without definitive organic diagnosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates weakness that does not follow anatomical nerve or dermatome distribution, described as 'give-way' weakness that gives impression of effort limitation rather than true paralysis. Hoover sign is positive (contralateral hip extension weakness when attempting to lift affected limb). Gait is inconsistent and varies with attention. Romberg test shows excessive sway that normalizes with distraction. Strength grades vary between examinations.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI including diffusion weighted imaging is completely normal with no white matter lesions, acute infarcts, or structural pathology. No evidence of demyelinating disease. Cervical and thoracic spine MRI show normal cord signal and normal structural anatomy. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing including EMG and NCS is entirely normal. No evidence of organic neurological disease on imaging or electrophysiological studies.

puzzles.explanation: Functional seizures with atypical characteristics, inconsistency with organic seizure patterns, normal EEG, and clear psychological stressors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with progressive pain in the forefoot, specifically between the third and fourth toes, over the past 8 months. She describes sharp, burning pain and paresthesias that radiate into the toes, particularly when wearing tight footwear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is exacerbated by walking in heels or tight shoes and improves significantly with removal of footwear and massage. She reports difficulty with prolonged standing or walking more than 30 minutes. Pain is absent during rest with feet elevated. She has modified her shoe choices to loose-fitting, flat footwear to minimize symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has no prior history of foot trauma or surgery. She worked as a hairdresser for 25 years requiring prolonged standing, which may have contributed to the condition. She reports increasing pain over the past 8 months despite footwear modifications. No systemic disease or diabetes. No history of inflammatory arthropathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Worked as hairdresser for 25 years with prolonged standing. No prior foot trauma, surgery, or orthopedic intervention. No diabetes, inflammatory arthropathy, or peripheral neuropathy. Pain progressive despite conservative footwear modifications over 8 months, suggesting mechanical compression not responding to load reduction alone.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the forefoot demonstrates a hypoechoic nodular mass measuring 8 mm in the third intermetatarsal space, consistent with Morton's neuroma. The lesion is located plantarly between the metatarsal heads with compression of adjacent tissues. MRI shows a T2-hyperintense fusiform mass at the third intermetatarsal space with perineural fibrosis. No bony abnormalities or stress fractures identified.

puzzles.explanation: Morton's neuroma in the third web space with classic burning/numbness, positive Mulder's click, aggravated by narrow footwear, and confirmed on ultrasound >5mm.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old sedentary office worker presents with gradually worsening unilateral hip and buttock pain over 4 months, radiating down the posterolateral thigh. The pain is constant and has significantly impaired their ability to sit for prolonged periods at work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting, hip internal rotation movements, and driving; it eases with standing, walking, and hip external rotation. They must change positions frequently throughout the day and have started standing at their desk.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent trauma or falls reported. The patient has a sedentary lifestyle with minimal physical activity and denies regular stretching or conditioning. No bowel/bladder dysfunction or weight loss reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with the FAIR test (flexion-adduction-internal rotation) and Freiberg's sign (internal rotation of flexed hip). Hip external rotators are weak at 4/5, and palpation over the greater sciatic notch elicits tenderness. The SLR and Kernig's tests are negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip and pelvis shows no structural pathology, disc herniation, or facet arthrosis. However, hypertrophy of the piriformis muscle is noted with signal changes suggesting muscle strain, and mild compression of the sciatic nerve is visualized.

puzzles.explanation: Deep gluteal syndrome represents a common mimic of radiculopathy, requiring specialized provocation testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retired tradesperson presents with progressive bilateral lower back and buttock pain over 18 months, with radiation into both lower extremities when walking. Symptoms are reproducible and significantly limit walking distance and tolerance for standing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking more than 100 meters triggers pain and numbness in both legs, forcing the patient to sit and rest; symptoms resolve quickly with sitting or forward bending. The patient has adopted a flexed posture at the waist and can walk longer distances leaning on a shopping trolley.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Longstanding history of lower back pain since 1990s following manual work. No acute traumatic event precipitated this decline. Patient reports no bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or lower limb weakness. Sleep quality is disrupted due to nighttime pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral hip and ankle reflexes are diminished (1+). Sensation to light touch is reduced in a bilateral L5 and S1 distribution. Straight leg raise is full and painless. The patient demonstrates exaggerated lumbar lordosis with mild kyphotic correction when bending forward.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine reveals multilevel degenerative changes with significant central stenosis at L4-L5, characterized by ligamentum flavum hypertrophy, disc bulging, and facet joint arthrosis. The spinal canal diameter is severely narrowed, particularly at the level of maximum stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis presents with an insidious onset in a middle-aged patient with prominent stiffness and exercise limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with persistent pelvic pain and deep perineal discomfort over 3 years that has significantly impacted her quality of life, sexual function, and work productivity. Pain is constant but fluctuates in intensity throughout the day and menstrual cycle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman with persistent pelvic pain over 3 years. Pain is constant but variable in intensity, worsening with her menstrual cycle. Significant impact on daily activities and quality of life.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain is worse with prolonged sitting and sexual intercourse. Patient improves with position changes, lying down, and heat. Has significantly modified activities to avoid triggers.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpation of pelvic floor muscles reveals significant guarding and tenderness, particularly on the left side. Pain is reproduced with pelvic floor pressure and vaginal palpation of obturator internus. Straight leg raise and hip range of motion are unremarkable, but internal hip rotation is limited by discomfort.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound and MRI imaging show no ovarian cysts, fibroids, or endometrial pathology. No structural abnormalities of the bladder, uterus, or bowel are identified. Imaging is essentially normal, ruling out other organic pelvic pathology as the primary driver of symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome shows combined endometriosis and adhesions post-PID with associated IBS and dyspareunia in a young woman with multiple comorbidities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old office worker presents with unilateral right-sided neck pain and occipital headaches over 6 months, worsening with prolonged computer work. Pain is not radiating and has caused significant functional limitation in work capacity and neck mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting, sustained neck extension, and specific head movements (particularly right rotation and right side bending). The patient reports relief with frequent position changes, gentle stretching, and heat application. Symptoms fluctuate throughout the day with worse pain in the afternoon.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma or motor vehicle accident; symptoms developed gradually over months related to ergonomic factors. Patient reports no previous neck injury, no radiation into the arms, and no hand tingling or weakness. Sleep is disrupted by neck pain when rolling onto the right side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, right cervical rotation is restricted to 60 degrees compared to left side. Right lateral flexion provokes localized neck pain at the C4-C5 level. Palpation over the right cervical facet joints elicits marked tenderness. Upper extremity strength and reflexes are intact, and sensation is normal in all dermatomes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: On examination: right cervical rotation restricted to 60 degrees (left side 80+ degrees). Right lateral flexion reproduces localized neck pain at the C4-C5 level. Palpation directly over the right cervical facet joints at C4-C5 elicits marked tenderness. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation are intact in all dermatomes; no findings of nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical facet joint sprain is acute post-traumatic with mechanical provocation pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office worker presents with progressive lower back pain over 2 years. Pain is localized to the lower lumbar and sacroiliac region, worsened by prolonged sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain with sitting for more than 30 minutes and difficulty rising from a chair. Pain is relieved by lying down and walking. Forward bending is limited and uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No history of acute trauma or injury. Patient reports morning stiffness and tightness in hip flexors and lower back. Minimal exercise activity. No radicular symptoms or systemic features.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thomas test markedly positive. Hip abductor strength 4/5 bilaterally. Core stability poor: cannot maintain neutral spine in prone bridge. Lumbar extension painful and limited. Palpable anterior pelvic tilt with increased lumbar lordosis.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine shows no disc herniation, stenosis, or neural compromise. Mild degenerative changes are present but not sufficient to explain symptoms. Imaging does not reveal significant structural pathology; findings are consistent with muscular and postural dysfunction rather than structural disease.

puzzles.explanation: Lower crossed syndrome is occupational postural dysfunction with classic anterior pelvic tilt pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old recreational weightlifter presents with acute onset sharp anterior shoulder pain and an audible 'pop' during bench press. He reports immediate arm weakness and functional loss. Pain persists at rest and with movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot lift overhead or carry loads. All shoulder movement, particularly reaching across the body, reproduces anterior pain. Symptoms present even at rest but substantially worse with active use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of heavy weightlifting with progressive training intensity over recent years. No prior shoulder pathology. Injury occurred during a heavy loaded lift. No neck involvement or constitutional symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, anterior shoulder is tender with visible ecchymosis along proximal arm. Shoulder flexion strength is 3+/5. Speed's test and O'Brien's test reproduce anterior shoulder pain. Rotator cuff strength testing is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the shoulder demonstrates complete rupture of the long head of biceps tendon at the intertubercular groove with significant proximal retraction of approximately 5 cm. There is associated hemorrhage and edema in the bicipital groove. The rotator cuff tendons are intact without tear or signal abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Long head biceps rupture complicating rotator cuff repair or tears.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman presents with a 6-month history of progressive bilateral symmetrical distal lower extremity pain, numbness, and burning sensation starting in her toes. Symptoms began approximately 4 weeks into a chemotherapy regimen and have steadily worsened.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports significant functional limitation with walking and balance, particularly in low-light environments. She experiences increased symptoms with prolonged standing or walking and finds relief with sitting and lower extremity elevation. She reports difficulty with fine motor tasks such as buttoning clothes and writing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports no diabetes, significant alcohol use, or family history of hereditary neuropathy. She denies recent infections or medication changes aside from chemotherapy. No prior chemotherapy exposure.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral lower extremity sensation is diminished distally in a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced to the ankle bilaterally. Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally. Motor strength in lower extremities remains intact at 5/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies and electromyography demonstrate a sensory-predominant axonal polyneuropathy with reduced sensory nerve action potentials bilaterally in the sural and superficial peroneal nerves. No demyelinating features are present. MRI of the lumbar spine is normal, ruling out structural causes.

puzzles.explanation: Taxane-induced peripheral neuropathy with typical timing, distribution, and axonal pattern correlating with chemotherapy exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old retired teacher presents with painless progressive weakness of the right shoulder over 12 months, with inability to actively raise her arm overhead. She initially attributed symptoms to aging but sought evaluation when function significantly deteriorated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She cannot perform overhead tasks such as reaching into cabinets or combing her hair. Attempting to abduct her shoulder against gravity results in immediate collapse. She can perform passive range of motion comfortably but cannot actively lift her arm from her lap. Non-rotator cuff activities are relatively preserved with good hand grip strength.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She denies any acute shoulder injury or trauma. She did have a remote history of shoulder impingement symptoms 8 years ago that improved with conservative treatment. She has not undergone any shoulder surgery. She reports long-standing use of corticosteroid inhalers for COPD (15 years). She attributes her weakness to aging and declining activity level.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates severe weakness of shoulder abduction (1/5) and external rotation (0/5). The drop arm test is dramatically positive with inability to maintain any arm elevation. Infraspinatus atrophy is marked and visible bilaterally with greater atrophy on right (affected) side. Supraspinatus atrophy is also present. Strength testing demonstrates preserved hand grip (5/5) and elbow strength (5/5). Passive shoulder motion is pain-free and full.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the right shoulder reveals a large full-thickness rotator cuff tear involving both supraspinatus and infraspinatus tendons measuring 2.8 cm x 1.5 cm with significant retraction. Severe fatty infiltration of both muscles indicating chronic degenerative change (Goutallier grade 3-4). Supraspinatus muscle atrophy is advanced. No significant arthritic changes. Acromion is a hooked morphology.

puzzles.explanation: Rotator cuff tear complicating adhesive capsulitis, unmasked as motion improves.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old woman with known osteoporosis fell at home and was unable to get up. She has severe pain in the hip and groin region with immediate onset. She was found on the floor 2 hours later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate any weight on the left leg. Pain is constant and severe; even passive hip movements (flexion, rotation) are acutely painful. She is bed-bound and requires full assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has had osteoporosis for 8 years but reports poor adherence to therapy. She lives independently and ambulated with a walker at baseline. No head injury, confusion, or loss of consciousness reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the left lower extremity is shortened and externally rotated compared to the right. Left hip is markedly tender, and any attempt at movement (flexion, internal/external rotation) reproduces severe pain. Hip flexors and extensors are 0/5 due to pain inhibition. Sensation and pulses are intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays of the left hip reveal a displaced intracapsular femoral neck fracture. CT imaging confirms a subcapital fracture with approximately 90-degree angulation. There is significant osteopenia evident on imaging. Associated soft tissue swelling is noted in the hip region.

puzzles.explanation: Femoral neck fracture complications in very elderly with severe osteoporosis have poor prognosis requiring careful management decisions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old girl is referred to physiotherapy following screening detection of spinal curvature at school. She reports no pain and has no functional complaints, but her mother notes she appears to have unequal shoulder heights and breast heights when viewed from behind.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports no activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty with normal daily activities, sports, or school. She has no difficulty with physical education and participates in volleyball without symptoms. Posture appears asymmetrical with shoulder drop on one side and truncal asymmetry evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient denies any activity limitations, pain, or breathing difficulty even with volleyball. Truncal asymmetry is visually evident; patient and parents have noticed progressive postural changes over the past 6–12 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Adam's forward bend test reveals asymmetrical rib prominence on the right side with thoracic rib hump measuring approximately 1.5 cm. Shoulder heights are unequal with right shoulder higher than left. Skin examination is normal without hairy patches, cafe-au-lait spots, or dimpling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal X-rays demonstrate a right thoracic spinal curvature measuring approximately 35 degrees Cobb angle. The curve is primarily thoracic with apex at T7-T8. Vertebral heights are normal, and there is no kyphosis, vertebral rotation, or bony abnormalities suggesting secondary causes.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic adolescent scoliosis is the most common type, often detected during growth spurt, requiring monitoring for curve progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of involuntary urine leakage with coughing, sneezing, and laughing that has progressively worsened and is now affecting her social and professional life. She reports light-to-moderate urine loss during these exertional events, particularly when activities are unexpected.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Incontinence is reliably triggered by physical exertion including coughing, sneezing, jumping, and exercise activity. Walking long distances may precipitate leakage if the bladder is full. The patient reports relief with pelvic floor muscle contraction before coughing and with regular pelvic floor muscle training exercises.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports she can manage symptoms by contracting pelvic floor muscles proactively (before coughing/sneezing) and has noticed improvement with dedicated pelvic floor muscle training over the past 6 months. She has not sought other treatments (pessary, medications, or injections) to date.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pelvic floor muscle strength is graded 3/5 using Oxford scale, with reduced endurance and poor voluntary contraction quality. Cough test with full bladder reproduces leakage. Abdominal examination is soft without masses. Neurological examination is normal. Urinalysis is negative for infection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic ultrasound during Valsalva maneuver demonstrates urethral hypermobility with funneling of the bladder neck and descent of the proximal urethra of approximately 1.5 cm. Post-void residual is normal at 15 mL. No pelvic organ prolapse or other structural abnormalities are identified.

puzzles.explanation: Stress Urinary Incontinence shows pure stress mechanism with exertion-triggered leakage in a multiparous woman without overactive bladder symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old manual laborer presents with acute onset lower back and leg pain following a lifting injury at work 2 weeks ago. Pain radiates down the right leg with associated numbness and tingling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly worse with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Walking and extension-based movements provide relief. Patient has stopped heavy lifting and adopted a lumbar support belt.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports a 3-week history of progressive worsening symptoms after the acute lifting incident. He has a prior history of lower back pain 5 years ago that resolved with conservative care. He denies bowel/bladder dysfunction, saddle anesthesia, or progressive neurological decline. He reports sleeping poorly due to leg pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, straight leg raise on the right is limited to 40 degrees due to leg pain. Right ankle dorsiflexion strength is 4/5 with normal hip flexor strength. Sensation to light touch is reduced in the right L5 distribution (lateral leg and dorsum of foot). Right knee reflex is normal but ankle reflex is diminished at 1+.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine demonstrates a right-sided disc herniation at L5-S1 level with extrusion of nuclear material. There is compression of the right L5 nerve root with associated nerve root edema. The herniated material is in the axillary zone, just lateral to the spinal canal. No cauda equina syndrome is evident.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy is the textbook presentation with classic dermatomal pain and positive neurological findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old recreational runner presents with progressive lower leg pain over 6 weeks that has forced her to reduce running frequency and distance. She reports morning stiffness and tightness in the lower leg that improves with activity, and localized swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by running, particularly on hard or uneven surfaces, and by impact activities. The patient reports pain during the first 10-15 minutes of running that improves during the run, then worsens again after activity. Walking causes mild discomfort, and the patient has modified her training to non-impact activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recently increased her running volume by 50% over the past 8 weeks as she trained for a half-marathon. She reports a change to a new running shoe 3 months ago. No acute trauma, no previous lower leg injuries. She denies systemic symptoms, fevers, or weight loss. She runs 4-5 days per week.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, localized tenderness is present along the posteromedial tibia in the middle third of the leg, spanning approximately 5 cm. There is mild swelling and erythema over the tender area. Palpation reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion and plantarflexion are full and painless. Lower extremity alignment shows mild pronation at the foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals cortical stress reaction at the posteromedial tibia in the middle third with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema. Tibialis posterior and soleus fascia show inflammation. No fracture line is visible, consistent with early-stage stress injury rather than frank fracture or other pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Shin splints shows typical medial tibial stress syndrome from running with periosteal inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old with chronic productive cough and dyspnea on exertion. Progressive respiratory symptoms with frequent infections. Functional capacity has declined over the past year.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 16-year-old with a one-year history of progressive dyspnea on exertion and chronic productive cough. Recently experiencing fatigue and recurrent infections requiring treatment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Diagnosed with genetic pulmonary condition in early childhood. History of recurrent respiratory tract infections treated with multiple antibiotic courses. Pancreatic insufficiency managed with enzyme supplementation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Genetic pulmonary condition first diagnosed in early childhood. Multiple courses of antibiotics for recurrent respiratory infections. No pancreatic or gastrointestinal issues reported.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates hyperinflation with bronchial wall thickening and bronchiectatic changes predominantly in upper lobes. Evidence of bronchial secretion impaction with localized air trapping.

puzzles.explanation: Late-diagnosed CF with advanced lung disease and early signs of pulmonary complications. Demonstrates variable age of presentation and progressive decline if untreated early.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 8-year-old with lower extremity weakness and loss of sensation below the mid-back. Patient uses wheelchair for community mobility but has limitations with transfers and positional changes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 8-year-old with chronic lower limb weakness and reduced sensation. Uses wheelchair for distance but family reports increasing difficulty with positional transfers and balance activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient manages self-care with assistance but has poor trunk control in sitting. Cannot participate in playground activities; bladder and bowel management requires scheduled catheterization and bowel routine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Detected prenatally; emergency spinal closure within 48 hours of birth. Subsequent surgeries for progressive deformity. VP shunt placed for hydrocephalus. Multiple surgical episodes across childhood.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of spine demonstrates segmental spinal cord abnormality with partial agenesis at thoracic level. Progressive spinal deformity with 45-degree scoliotic curve noted on X-ray. Lateral recess stenosis from ossified spinal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Tethered spinal cord is a late complication of spina bifida repair causing progressive neurological deterioration requiring surgical untethering.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 47-year-old woman with progressive swelling of the right upper extremity following cancer treatment. Notes heaviness and tightness in arm and hand with clothing becoming too tight over weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including gripping and keyboard work. Unable to wear rings or watch on affected arm. Experiences increased swelling after prolonged use of arm or in warm weather. Sleep disrupted due to uncomfortable arm positioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer treated with mastectomy and axillary lymph node dissection 18 months ago. Completed adjuvant chemotherapy and radiation to chest wall. No known family history of lymphatic disorders. Recent cellulitis episode of affected arm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm circumference exceeds left by 3.5 cm at forearm; 2.8 cm at wrist. Pitting edema present in dorsum of hand and forearm. Skin thickened with loss of normal contours; palpable nodularity in subcutaneous tissue. Stemmer sign positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates absent lymph uptake in right axilla with delayed transit in upper extremity. Ultrasound confirms honeycomb pattern of subcutaneous edema with echogenic dermal thickening. Absent or hypoplastic deep lymph nodes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Primary lymphedema with filariasis component, common in tropical regions. Bilateral presentation distinguishes from secondary causes. Long-standing disease with significant fibrotic changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old competitive athlete with 3 weeks of progressive shoulder blade pain. Notices the shoulder blade on one side looks different when training, and overhead activities feel uncomfortable.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform push-ups, bench press, or throwing activities without exacerbation of scapular pain. Shoulder flexion and horizontal adduction movements reproduce symptoms. Fatigue and heaviness in shoulder girdle after minimal activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during intense strength training involving heavy bench pressing 3 weeks ago. No prior shoulder injuries. Progressive weakness noted with each training session. Denied neurological symptoms or neck pain referral.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked scapular winging with medial border prominence; winging increases with wall push-up. Weakness of scapular protraction on manual testing. Inability to maintain scapular position during forward arm raise.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in serratus anterior with positive sharp waves and fibrillations. Serratus anterior muscle shows altered signal intensity on MRI. Nerve conduction studies of long thoracic nerve show evidence of axonal injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic serratus anterior paralysis with chronic scapular winging and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old with shoulder pain and stiffness for 6 weeks following a sports-related overhead injury. Pain worsens with movement and at night. Currently attending physiotherapy with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reach overhead or out to side beyond 45 degrees. Sleeping on affected shoulder not tolerable. Avoids pushing/pulling activities. Pain with dressing and grooming tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during overhead throwing sport. Underwent surgical repair 6 weeks ago. Patient reports good adherence to post-operative restrictions and early physiotherapy. No prior shoulder pathology or surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant discrepancy between passive (110° flexion) and active ROM (60° flexion, 45° abduction). Marked muscle guarding and apprehension. Pain-limited movement. Deltoid strength difficult to isolate due to guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative ultrasound demonstrates successful repair integrity with no evidence of re-tear, gap formation, or fluid collection. Findings exclude major structural failure and support mechanistic cause of persistent stiffness as adhesions or inadequate post-operative mobilization rather than surgical complication.

puzzles.explanation: Post-SLAP repair in throwing athletes requires careful progression of upper extremity strengthening, sport-specific training, and gradual return to throwing with structured interval throwing program to prevent recurrence.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old office worker with frequent bilateral headaches described as pressing/squeezing around the head. Headaches occur most days, lasting hours to full day, without complete resolution between episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen with sustained computer work and poor posture; improve with brief breaks and neck stretching. Stress and fatigue are clear triggers. No nausea or light sensitivity, but neck stiffness accompanies headache onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Headache pattern started approximately 2 years ago coinciding with job change requiring prolonged desk work. Denies head trauma, neurological symptoms, or medication overuse. Previous episodes responded well to rest and over-the-counter analgesics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals significant tenderness in bilateral upper trapezius and suboccipital muscles. Forward head posture noted with chin jutting during cervical flexion. Cervical range of motion preserved but reproduction of symptoms with sustained flexion. No focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of normal cervical lordosis consistent with postural dysfunction. MRI excludes structural pathology, mass, or intracranial abnormality. No evidence of cervical spondylosis or foraminal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Tension-type headache shows occupational postural trigger with forward head posture and cervical-associated findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old male fighter with acute hand injury sustained during boxing training 2 weeks ago. Localized pain and swelling at knuckle level with visible deformity of hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 24-year-old male with acute hand pain and swelling sustained during contact sport training 2 weeks ago. Localized symptoms at knuckle level with visible hand deformity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct punch impact to knuckle during sparring session. Initial swelling managed at home with ice and elevation. No prior hand injuries or fractures. Training halted immediately post-injury; minimal physiotherapy initiated.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: No prior hand fractures or injuries. Initial management with ice and elevation only; no imaging obtained acutely. Patient remains concerned about hand alignment and residual prominence at knuckle.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hand demonstrates fifth metacarpal neck fracture with angulation of 35 degrees and minimal displacement. Metacarpal head intact without intra-articular extension. No associated carpal bone involvement or soft tissue ossification.

puzzles.explanation: Boxer's fracture shows healed fifth metacarpal fracture with malunion and chronic functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with a 5-year history of chronic pain in the pelvic region. She reports dyspareunia and urinary frequency but denies fever, weight loss, or systemic symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She avoids prolonged standing and sitting due to symptom exacerbation. She reports difficulty with normal bowel and bladder function. Symptoms ease with rest in a supine position with hips flexed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a history of hysterectomy 8 years ago without resolution of pain. She has had multiple consultations with gynecologists and urologists with no clear diagnosis. She denies fever, weight loss, or malignancy risk factors. She reports significant psychological distress related to chronic pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscles are hypertonic and tender on palpation. Hip abduction and internal rotation produce discomfort at end-range. Lower limb reflexes and motor strength are intact, but sensory testing reveals decreased light touch sensation in a dermatomal pattern corresponding to S2-S4.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the pelvis demonstrates no visible masses, adhesions, or structural abnormalities. The bladder, uterus (absent post-hysterectomy), and rectum appear normal. Imaging findings are reassuring and do not identify any obvious pathology explaining the patient's chronic pain.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Pelvic Pain Syndrome shows complex pain syndrome with history of multiple pelvic surgeries, probable adhesions, central sensitization, and significant psychological impact.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 19-year-old student with persistent headaches, dizziness, and significant difficulty concentrating. Reports fatigue that interferes with daily tasks. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged visual tasks or bright environments. Vertigo triggered specifically by rapid head movements. Gait unsteady, particularly on uneven surfaces or when turning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Loss of consciousness for approximately 3 minutes at scene. Admitted to hospital and required ICU-level monitoring in acute phase. No mechanical ventilation needed. Previously no significant medical or neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Dynamic gait assessment reveals unsteady balance with increased arm swing for compensation. Positive Romberg test with 20-second stance instability. Dix-Hallpike maneuver triggers vertigo; Fukuda test shows significant deviation. Cranial nerves II-XII intact; no focal neurological deficits.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Follow-up MRI demonstrates resolving contusions in frontal lobes with minimal residual subdural fluid. Microhemorrhages visible on susceptibility-weighted imaging in white matter tracts. Cerebrospinal fluid spaces normal; no mass effect or midline shift.

puzzles.explanation: Epidural hematoma in elderly anticoagulated patient with classic lucid interval followed by rapid deterioration from expanding hemorrhage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling localized to the front of the kneecap. Visible fullness anterior to patellar tendon with noticeable increase in size over several days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 45-year-old with acute-onset knee swelling over the past week. Reports visible fullness in front of the knee that has progressively worsened. Denies significant trauma or mechanical symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: New job requiring frequent kneeling positions on hard flooring for 8-10 hours daily. Symptoms began after three weeks of work. Denies recent fall or direct knee trauma. No previous knee problems or inflammatory arthritis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable, fluctuant swelling anterior to patella measuring approximately 4 cm in transverse diameter. Skin erythema and slight warmth over bursa; no fever. Pain with deep palpation of bursa. Knee joint motion unrestricted; meniscal and ligamentous tests normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates simple fluid collection anterior to patella without debris or septations. Bursa measures 1.2 cm at greatest depth. Patellar tendon and underlying joint space sonographically normal. No bursal wall enhancement on post-contrast imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Prepatellar bursitis develops from repetitive kneeling or direct trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old reports persistent low back pain 8 months after a work-related strain injury. Pain level has remained high despite gradual functional improvement and medical clearance to gradually resume normal activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids bending, lifting, and sustained sitting despite medical clearance for light activity. Catastrophizes about pain with minimal provocation. Self-imposed restrictions on activities far exceed physiological limitations. Delayed recovery despite gradual symptom improvement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial lumbar strain from work injury 8 months ago. Received passive therapies exclusively; no progressive exercise rehabilitation. Multiple provider visits with varying diagnoses heightened anxiety. Family members reinforce avoidance behaviors; overprotective support system present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Objective findings minimal and inconsistent: straight leg raise limited by guarding but improves markedly when patient is distracted during conversation. Able to flex lumbar spine 60 degrees when attention diverted, but reports severe pain with intentional forward bending. Fear-avoidance beliefs questionnaire indicates elevated fear-related cognitions with score in severe range (67/96).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows minimal degenerative changes with small central disc protrusion at L4-L5 without cord compression; findings inconsistent with reported disability level. Prior imaging reviewed; changes have remained stable. No imaging evidence of progressive structural pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Kinesiophobia shows severe trauma-related fear-avoidance with PTSD features limiting function across multiple activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational runner with progressive pain in the Achilles tendon region. Reports morning stiffness and pain that improves with activity, then worsens toward evening and after intense running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running distances limited to less than 3 km before pain becomes limiting. Uphill running and speed work significantly aggravate symptoms. Walking on stairs, particularly downstairs, causes focal Achilles pain. Stiffness pronounced after sedentary periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running volume over past 8 weeks; added hills and tempo sessions without gradual progression. No prior significant Achilles problems. Previous left ankle sprain 5 years ago. Tight calves noted since increased training. Age-appropriate general health; no inflammatory conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness on palpation of Achilles tendon at 3-4 cm proximal to insertion, with palpable nodularity. Calf strength 5/5 bilaterally but pain with resisted plantarflexion against resistance. Single leg calf raise limited to 8 repetitions; pain increases progressively.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates thickening of Achilles tendon at midportion with hypoechoic regions suggesting intratendinous degeneration. Doppler flow reveals increased neovascularity within the tendon. Retrocalcaneal bursitis noted on imaging with fluid accumulation.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Achilles tendinopathy may show structural degenerative changes resistant to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old with localized lateral elbow pain following repetitive gripping activities. Pain is sharp and well-localized over the lateral elbow prominence with progressive worsening over 6 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping, lifting, and twisting activities reproduce pain. Difficulty carrying objects with the affected arm; weak grip strength noted. Symptoms aggravated by pronation of forearm under load. Pain often radiates down lateral forearm toward wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Began after increased repetitive work involving tool use and forceful gripping. No prior elbow injuries; no rheumatological history. Works as tradesperson requiring repetitive wrist and forearm activity. Symptoms gradually worsened despite continued activity on affected arm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Point tenderness over lateral epicondyle with pain reproduction on palpation. Mill's test and Cozen's test both positive eliciting lateral elbow pain. Grip strength reduced 15% compared to contralateral side. Wrist extension against resistance reproduces symptoms; no neurological deficits present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound reveals hypoechoic thickening of common extensor tendon origin at lateral epicondyle with heterogeneous echotexture. Mild surrounding soft tissue edema noted. Power Doppler demonstrates neovascularity within the tendon. No calcifications or cortical irregularities.

puzzles.explanation: Classic lateral epicondylitis with gradual onset from overuse, typical in middle-aged individuals with repetitive arm use.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old recreational runner presents with localized foot pain of gradual onset over the past 3 weeks. The pain is activity-dependent and worsens with prolonged weight-bearing. This presentation could suggest several overuse injuries or acute traumatic conditions affecting the midfoot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by running and walking activities, particularly on hard surfaces. The patient reports relief with complete rest and ice application. Progressive functional decline with each week of attempted training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in running mileage by 40% over four weeks without adequate recovery. No acute traumatic event recalled. Patient is female with history of irregular menstrual cycles and reports low dietary calcium intake.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, focal point tenderness at the second or third metatarsal shaft with reproducible pain on applied load-bearing. Single-leg stance and hopping produce immediate pain. Swelling is minimal and localized to the affected metatarsal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays demonstrate a linear fracture line through the metatarsal shaft with early periosteal reaction at the margins. MRI shows bone marrow edema surrounding the fracture site with characteristic stress reaction pattern in the trabecular bone.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsal stress fracture shows recurrent multiple metatarsal stress fractures with underlying energy deficiency.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents with acute onset elbow pain and swelling after a fall on an outstretched hand while rollerblading. Pain is laterally localized and worsens with movement. The mechanism involved falling from standing height onto an extended arm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports inability to supinate the forearm without marked pain and functional loss. Pronation is relatively preserved but limited. Weight-bearing through the upper extremity in standing push position is not tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior elbow injuries or instability. No inflammatory history. Impact occurred at the lateral aspect of the elbow during the fall. Immediate swelling and inability to continue activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable point tenderness over the radial head with reactive swelling. Pain is elicited with supination against resistance. Varus and valgus stress tests are negative; however, rotational movements of the forearm produce significant discomfort.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging reveals a fracture through the radial head with minimal displacement of the fracture fragment. The fracture line extends through the articular surface of the radiocapitellar joint. Associated soft tissue swelling is present without evidence of significant comminution.

puzzles.explanation: Radial head fracture with mechanical symptoms from fracture fragment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old patient reports a 6-month history of persistent, overwhelming tiredness that is disproportionate to physical activity level and not relieved by rest. Daily activities that were previously manageable now require significant effort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Work capacity has declined from full-time employment to part-time, with frequent required rest days. Even light household tasks such as cooking and laundry result in exhaustion lasting several hours. Social and recreational activities have been substantially curtailed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent chemotherapy 4 months ago for a newly diagnosed systemic illness. Although fatigue was expected during treatment, it has persisted beyond the typical recovery window. Sleep quality remains disrupted with early morning awakening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On assessment, resting heart rate is elevated at 88 bpm. No objective weakness is found on manual muscle testing, which is normal throughout. Cardiovascular and respiratory responses to light exertion are disproportionately elevated compared to normal activity levels.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Laboratory analysis shows persistent anemia (hemoglobin 10.2 g/dL) and elevated inflammatory markers (CRP, ESR). Thyroid function studies are normal. Imaging surveillance shows no evidence of disease recurrence.

puzzles.explanation: Cancer-related fatigue shows treatment-related profound fatigue with systemic effects during active cancer therapy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old with several years of progressive shoulder pain and stiffness. Pain worsens with overhead activities and is notably stiff in the mornings. Gradual onset over many years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching for items on shelves is significantly limited and painful. Swimming, a formerly enjoyed activity, now produces pain during both backstroke and freestyle. Dressing activities, particularly putting on a shirt, are increasingly difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Remote shoulder dislocation treated conservatively 15 years prior. Multiple episodes of subacromial impingement over the past decade. Thirty years of occupational overhead work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, active external rotation is limited to 45 degrees with pain at end-range. Painful arc is present between 70-120 degrees of abduction. Crepitus is palpable within the glenohumeral joint during movement. Supraspinatus and infraspinatus strength testing shows weakness secondary to pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing at the glenohumeral joint with osteophyte formation along the humeral head margins. MRI confirms hyaline cartilage thinning and small focal chondral defects. Bone marrow edema is present at the articular surfaces.

puzzles.explanation: Primary glenohumeral osteoarthritis with progressive degenerative changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old basketball player presents acutely with ankle pain and swelling after a sudden cutting movement during a game. The injury occurred approximately 2 hours ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports inability to bear weight on the affected side. Attempted ambulation produces severe pain. Running, jumping, and any lateral weight shift are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: This ankle was previously injured 8 months ago with similar mechanism. That prior sprain was treated with rest and taping but did not receive formal physiotherapy rehabilitation. Patient reports ankle 'giving way' episodes twice in the intervening months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the anterior drawer test is positive with anterior displacement of the talus. Inversion stress testing reproduces pain and excessive ankle motion. Palpable tenderness is present over the anterior talofibular ligament. Proprioceptive deficits are apparent on single-leg stance testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals grade II tear of the anterior talofibular ligament with partial disruption of the calcaneofibular ligament. Edema surrounds both ligaments. Small syndesmotic ligament inflammation is visible at the tibiofibular joint.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral ankle sprains are common inversion injuries affecting the anterior talofibular and calcaneofibular ligaments.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old avid golfer presents with insidious-onset groin pain that has progressively worsened over 6 months. Pain is localized to the medial hip and inner thigh region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The golf swing, particularly the follow-through phase, reproduces pain consistently. Walking for distances exceeding 15 minutes triggers discomfort. Internally rotating the hip, such as crossing the leg while sitting, immediately provokes sharp groin pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 30-year golf history with chronic poor swing mechanics emphasizing deep hip rotation. Recently completed extended tournament schedule over 4 weeks with minimal rest. No acute traumatic event. Progressive activity-related presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The FABER test reproduces pain in the ipsilateral groin. Internal hip rotation in flexion is painful and limited to 20 degrees. Hip adductor strength testing shows pain-limited weakness at 4/5. Palpation of the adductor longus origin is tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals adductor longus tendinopathic changes with mild partial tearing at the pubic symphysis insertion. Concurrent mild hip joint cartilage thinning and edema at pubic symphysis noted.

puzzles.explanation: Golfer's hip shows chronic femoroacetabular impingement with labral involvement limiting golf performance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old with a recent knee injury presents with persistent joint pain and swelling that has not improved with conservative management over the past 3 weeks. Pain is localized to the knee and worsens with activity. Some loss of motion is noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking distances greater than 10 minutes increases pain and swelling. Stairs, particularly descending, are difficult and require rail support. Sitting with the knee flexed beyond 90 degrees produces discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient sustained a medial meniscal injury requiring surgical intervention 3 weeks ago. Prior to injury, patient was training at a competitive level with high-impact demands. Recovery is slower than anticipated at this stage.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, reactive joint effusion is palpable in the suprapatellar pouch. Knee flexion is limited to 110 degrees secondary to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength is 4/5 with visible atrophy. McMurray's test is now negative; the medial joint line tenderness has resolved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative imaging demonstrates successful surgical resection with evidence of reactive inflammatory changes within the joint. No evidence of iatrogenic ligament injury or articular cartilage involvement. Structural integrity of remaining knee anatomy is preserved.

puzzles.explanation: Complex meniscectomy with malalignment presents recovery challenges. Treatment requires intensive proprioceptive training, neuromuscular control retraining, potential consideration of alignment correction, and careful load management to prevent osteoarthritic progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with heel pain that is worst upon rising in the morning and taking the first steps, then gradually improves with activity before returning at end of day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Activities requiring prolonged standing or walking on hard surfaces significantly exacerbate pain. Patient notices barefoot walking and light footwear worsen symptoms; pain is minimal when wearing supportive shoes with cushioning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain began insidiously 4 months ago without specific precipitating injury. Patient recently increased exercise volume and has BMI of 32. Works a job requiring standing for 6-8 hours daily. Wears predominantly flat casual shoes without arch support.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, discrete tenderness is elicited at the medial calcaneal tubercle along the plantar fascia insertion. Pain is reproduced with passive dorsiflexion of the foot combined with toe extension. Calf tightness limits ankle dorsiflexion to 5 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging demonstrates fascia thickening to 4.2 mm (normal <3 mm) at the medial insertion. Hypoechoic appearance indicates inflammatory change. No heel spur is visible on radiographs, ruling out the osseous pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis results from mechanical overload and inflammation of the plantar fascia insertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old manual laborer presents 4 weeks after a thermal injury to the hand and forearm. Pain and functional loss are significant. Wound care is ongoing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip or perform fine motor tasks. Self-care activities including bathing and dressing are severely limited. Sleep is disrupted by pain and frequent dressing protocol requirements.

clues.label: History
