clues.text: Long smoking history with continued active smoking despite cessation attempts. Recurrent chest infections managed with antibiotics over recent years. Previous occupational dust exposure. No known cardiac disease. Recent unintentional weight loss noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with flattened diaphragms and increased anteroposterior diameter. Peripheral bronchial wall thickening visible. HRCT demonstrates centrilobular emphysema predominantly in upper lobes with reduced lung parenchyma. Small airways disease pattern evident.

puzzles.explanation: Severe COPD (GOLD 3) with characteristic progressive dyspnoea, smoking history, hyperinflation, airflow limitation on spirometry, and significant exercise limitation requiring pulmonary rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old female reports unilateral headaches that began after starting a new office-based role. Pain worsens with certain neck movements and sustained postures. Analgesic medications provide minimal relief.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen during prolonged computer work and morning stiffness. Patient reports difficulty with work tasks requiring sustained posture or repeated neck turns to the right. Symptoms partially resolve with periodic breaks and postural changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month history of recurrent episodes following increased screen time and poor ergonomics. No history of head trauma, though she notes chronic forward head posture. No photophobia or phonophobia. Pain began after sustained office work transitions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On cervical examination, restricted right cervical rotation (60 degrees; normal >80 degrees) and extension provokes familiar headache. Positive cervical flexion-rotation test reproducing unilateral frontal pain. Palpation reveals asymmetrical muscle tension in right upper trapezius and cervical paraspinals.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine MRI reveals mild degenerative changes at C3-C4 and C4-C5 levels with facet joint hypertrophy. No significant nerve root compression. Straightening of normal cervical lordosis noted. Multilevel spondylotic changes most prominent at C3-C5 corresponding to referred pain distribution.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache is occupational in origin with clear postural and tension components.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old female with recent whiplash injury reports upper chest and shoulder pain with associated upper extremity neurological symptoms. Pain is worse with certain neck postures and limits functional activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead activities and reaching aggravate symptoms dramatically. Sustained neck extension and contralateral side-bending reproducibly worsen pain and tingling in the arm. Symptoms improve with arm elevation and neck flexion positions. Sleeping on the affected side increases nocturnal symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Motor vehicle accident 3 months ago with hyperextension-hyperflexion mechanism. Symptoms began gradually over weeks and have worsened despite ongoing conservative management. Recent flare correlates with increased work activity and positional demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Elevated first rib palpable on affected side. Bilateral scalene muscle tension and tenderness. Positive Roos test: upper extremity claudication and colour change within 2 minutes, consistent with vascular compression.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical imaging excludes spine pathology. CT demonstrates narrowed interscalene space with hypertrophic scalenes contributing to neurovascular bundle compression at first rib level.

puzzles.explanation: First rib dysfunction is a rare condition affecting thoracic outlet with serious vascular compromise risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old female presents with progressive bilateral upper extremity pain, numbness, and a sensation of heaviness in the hands. Symptoms began insidiously over several months without clear trauma or precipitant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot sustain overhead activities or maintain elevated arm positions for more than a few minutes. Symptoms worsen with tasks requiring arms held away from body, such as carrying or reaching. Sleep is disrupted by symptom flares with certain sleeping positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month progressive course without clear precipitant, though patient reports history of thoracic outlet symptoms with exercise. Recent workplace ergonomic changes and increased computer use. No prior surgical intervention. Symptoms initially postural but now present at rest in some positions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adson test and Roos test reproducibly recreate symptoms bilaterally. Hand intrinsic weakness noted; thenar and hypothenar muscle bulk diminished. Supraclavicular palpation elicits tenderness and Tinel sign bilaterally. Pulses are equal and symmetric at rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR angiography demonstrates reduced arterial caliber through thoracic outlet with compression of subclavian artery between anterior and middle scalene muscles. Color Doppler ultrasound confirms flow abnormality with positional changes. Cervical ribs absent but muscular hypertrophy contributes to narrowed interscalene space.

puzzles.explanation: Work-related neurogenic TOS from poor ergonomics and postural dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old overhead athlete reports insidious onset of deep shoulder pain with clicking and catching sensations over the past 8 months. Pain is greatest with throwing activities and worsens progressively with continued participation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Throwing reproduces sharp deep pain with a catching sensation that forces cessation of activity. Late-cocking phase of throwing causes particular symptom exacerbation. Nighttime symptoms limit sleep comfort in certain positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Eight-month history following increased training volume in throwing sport. No specific traumatic event preceding onset. Previous right shoulder impingement syndrome. Progressive worsening despite rest periods and anti-inflammatory medication management.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive O'Brien's test producing deep shoulder pain consistent with patient's complaint. Anterior slide test and crank test in abduction/external rotation reproduce pain. Apprehension test positive in 90-90 position, suggesting anterior-superior instability pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MR arthrography reveals posterior superior labral tear extending from 10 o'clock to 2 o'clock position with associated irregular articular surface. Fluid tracking into the tear confirms disruption. Subtle associated undersurface rotator cuff fraying noted. Anteroinferior labrum appears intact.

puzzles.explanation: Bankart lesion from anteroinferior shoulder dislocation with labral detachment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 4-year-old child presents with developmental delay affecting gross motor milestones and cognitive function. Parents report hypotonia and reduced strength limiting activities compared to age-matched peers.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 4-year-old child presents with global developmental delay. Parents report the child is slower than peers in reaching motor milestones, appears weaker, and tires easily with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Child demonstrates a wide-based gait and cannot hop or jump. Stair climbing requires upper limb support and rail-holding. Fine motor tasks are notably clumsy; pencil grasp is immature and feeding is messy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Condition identified in early infancy. Cardiac and orthopedic complications are known to be associated. No significant neurological events since birth. Attends early intervention services.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Positive prone head lift test (unable to clear forehead from mat). Severe ligamentous laxity noted: thumb-to-forearm touchable, ankle dorsiflexion 40°+. Knee valgus noted bilaterally even at rest. Vertical sagittal plane control poor during supported standing.

puzzles.explanation: Down syndrome frequently presents with atlantoaxial instability and global hypotonia affecting motor function and requiring careful cervical monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old runner reports insidious onset of deep anterior shin pain that began during training progression. Pain is activity-related and localized to the lower anterolateral leg, worse with running and relieving with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Onset of pain occurs during running, classically after 20 minutes of sustained activity. Sharp, deep pain in the shin prevents continued running and requires activity modification. Pain does not occur with walking at normal pace. Symptoms worsening with increased training volume despite attempts to rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Twelve-week progressive history correlating with increased distance running and addition of high-impact drills. Recent changes to training surface from track to asphalt. No previous lower extremity injuries. Training load increased 40% over 4 weeks preceding symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness elicited over anterior-medial tibia at the junction of proximal and middle thirds. Mild swelling noted over the painful region. Positive hop test reproducing familiar pain. Pain reproduced with resisted dorsiflexion. Percussion over tibia elicits focal pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tibial radiographs appear radiographically normal in early stages. MRI reveals characteristic findings including bone marrow edema on T2 sequence localized to the anterior tibia in the symptomatic region. STIR imaging shows hyperintense signal reflecting inflammatory response. No complete fracture line identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial stress fracture shows medial tibial involvement in poorly conditioned individual with rapid training progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old female reports a painless bulge in the posterior knee that became noticeable after knee swelling and pain. The mass does not limit function significantly but causes subjective awareness and occasional discomfort with certain knee positions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Bulge becomes more apparent with knee extension and activity; the mass reduces somewhat with knee flexion and rest. Prolonged walking increases prominence. Certain knee positions occasionally provoke mild aching in the popliteal region. No mechanical symptoms or true locking noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two-year history of gradual enlargement following prior knee effusion related to osteoarthritis. No acute traumatic event preceding cyst appearance. Bilateral mild knee osteoarthritis evident on imaging. Intermittent knee swelling management with activity modification and NSAIDs.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, a palpable firm mass noted in the popliteal fossa, best appreciated with knee extended. No warmth or erythema overlying the mass. Popliteal mass transilluminates, suggesting cystic fluid-filled nature. Knee effusion palpable. Active flexion and extension pain-free without mechanical restriction.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms anechoic fluid collection in popliteal fossa measuring 3.5 cm with no internal echoes. Direct communication demonstrated between cyst and knee joint capsule, confirming synovial origin. MRI shows T2 hyperintense cyst with intact walls, no hemorrhage. Associated knee joint effusion and mild degenerative changes noted.

puzzles.explanation: Baker's cysts are common and usually develop secondary to underlying knee joint pathology.

puzzles.answer: Avascular Necrosis of the Femoral Head

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old presents with progressive hip pain localized to the groin and lateral hip region. Pain is worse with weight-bearing and certain hip positions, with no recalled traumatic injury. The insidious onset over several weeks suggests multiple possible hip pathologies including osteoarthritis, labral pathology, or inflammatory conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty with weight-bearing activities and ambulation, particularly ascending stairs and transitioning from sitting to standing. Pain is aggravated by hip flexion beyond 90 degrees and internal rotation, while pain is eased with rest and hip abduction positioning. Walking distance has progressively decreased over the past month.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a significant history of corticosteroid use for 2 years secondary to chronic respiratory disease. Additionally, there is past history of excessive alcohol consumption. These risk factors combined with the progressive nature of symptoms over 3 months increase suspicion for a vascular compromise pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hip flexion is limited to 90 degrees with pain onset, and internal rotation is significantly restricted to 15 degrees. The FABER test reproduces groin pain, and the log roll test elicits lateral hip and groin pain. Hip strength is 4/5 in hip abductors and flexors bilaterally, with pain-limited range of motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: On examination, internal rotation is severely limited to 10–15 degrees with guarding and pain. Hip flexion and abduction are limited to 85–90 degrees. Hip strength is 4/5 in abductors and flexors, though motor deficit appears pain-limited rather than truly neurogenic. FABER test reproduces groin pain. Notably, external rotation and extension remain relatively preserved compared to internal rotation.

puzzles.explanation: Late-stage AVN often presents with established femoral head deformity requiring surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office worker presents with a 3-month history of dull, aching pain localised to the upper trapezius and levator scapulae bilaterally. No history of trauma or significant injury. Pain is constant but fluctuates throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports pain is aggravated by prolonged sitting at a desk with poor posture, particularly with forward head positioning. Neck rotation and lateral flexion toward the affected side reproduce pain. Symptoms ease with heat application, massage, and neck stretching. The patient notes morning stiffness lasting 15-20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient attributes symptom onset to recent intensive project work with increased stress. Reports previous episodes of neck pain that resolved with rest and therapy 2-3 years ago. No significant medical history, no rheumatological or autoimmune conditions. Current sleep disrupted; wakes with stiffness that improves with movement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, firm myofascial trigger points are identified in the upper trapezius that reproduce referred pain to the posterior head and temporal region when pressed. Active cervical range of motion is within normal limits but reproduces local muscle tenderness at end-ranges. The Levine test for thoracic outlet syndrome is negative, ruling out neural involvement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine unremarkable for degenerative changes, disc herniation, or neural compression. Ultrasound of trapezius: normal muscle echotexture, no focal lesion, normal vascularity. Normal findings support clinical diagnosis of primary muscle pain disorder without structural substrate.

puzzles.explanation: Myofascial pain syndrome presents with classic trigger point referral patterns, localized to specific muscles with reproducible findings on examination.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old carpenter presents with a 2-month history of inner elbow pain that developed insidiously without trauma. Pain worsens with gripping and repetitive hand activities. No prior similar episodes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot sustain grip-based activities or perform forceful wrist flexion. Pain is particularly triggered by combined wrist flexion with pronation movements. Symptoms improve with activity modification and rest, but return with repetitive gripping tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works as a carpenter and reports recent increase in overhead work and repetitive gripping tasks over the past 3 months. There is no history of direct trauma to the elbow. The patient has not sought prior treatment for musculoskeletal conditions. Symptoms began gradually after intensive project work in winter months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is maximal directly over the medial epicondyle. The Golfer's elbow test, performed with wrist flexion against resistance, reproduces medial elbow pain. Wrist flexor strength is diminished at 4/5 compared to the contralateral side. Elbow flexion and extension are preserved, but pronation with wrist flexion is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the medial elbow demonstrates heterogeneous echotexture and thickening of the flexor carpi radialis and palmaris longus tendon origins. There is mild hypoechoic change within the tendons consistent with tendinopathy. Power Doppler imaging shows increased vascularity at the medial epicondyle consistent with chronic inflammatory response.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational presentation of medial epicondylitis from new ergonomic stressors in office setting.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman presents with shoulder pain and progressive stiffness affecting the glenohumeral joint. The pain is localized deeply in the shoulder joint and is constant in nature, worsening at night. The 4-month gradual onset could suggest adhesive capsulitis, rotator cuff tear, or glenohumeral osteoarthritis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 58-year-old woman reports shoulder pain that started 4 months ago and has worsened gradually. The pain is deep and constant, worse at night. She notices her shoulder feels increasingly stiff and restricted in movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient cannot reach overhead or behind her back to fasten a bra. Forward flexion is possible but abduction and external rotation are severely restricted. Passive stretching provides temporary relief, but active movement is markedly limited.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Six months ago the patient sustained a mild rotator cuff strain and was advised to immobilize the shoulder for several weeks. After the immobilization was discontinued, progressive stiffness and pain developed over the following months despite no further injury. No history of trauma, inflammatory disease, or diabetes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the shoulder demonstrates thickening and contracture of the glenohumeral joint capsule, particularly visible on axial and coronal sequences. T2-weighted imaging shows patchy edema within the rotator cuff muscles due to disuse. No rotator cuff tears or labral pathology is identified. MR arthrography confirms reduced joint volume secondary to capsular inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis with progressive stiffness and loss of motion in all planes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 78-year-old woman presents with generalized muscle weakness and loss of functional strength affecting multiple muscle groups. The patient reports progressive difficulty with ambulation and basic activities of daily living over the past 2 years. The insidious onset and diffuse distribution could suggest sarcopenia, myositis, neurological disease, or systemic metabolic derangement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports difficulty rising from a chair without upper extremity support, climbing stairs, and walking long distances. Standing balance is compromised, and the patient has experienced several near-falls. Fine motor tasks such as opening jars and buttoning buttons are increasingly difficult. The patient describes feelings of fatigue that limit daily participation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty rising from a chair without armrest support, climbing stairs, and walking long distances. Standing balance is compromised with several near-falls. She reports generalized fatigue that limits participation in sustained activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, grip strength is measured at 18 kilograms bilaterally, which is below the 10th percentile for age and sex. Knee extension strength is 3+/5 bilaterally, and hip flexor strength is 3/5. Gait speed is reduced at 0.8 meters per second. The Short Physical Performance Battery score is 4, indicating high fall risk and poor functional mobility.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan shows low muscle mass with appendicular skeletal muscle index of 5.8 kilograms per meter squared, below the threshold for sarcopenia diagnosis. Computed tomography of the thigh shows marked intramuscular fat infiltration and reduced cross-sectional muscle area. MRI confirms widespread myosteatosis and atrophy of bilateral quadriceps and hip flexor muscles.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sarcopenia involves progressive muscle loss and weakness from reduced physical activity and anabolic resistance in older adults.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 70-year-old woman with established osteoarthritis presents with chronic bilateral lower limb pain, heaviness, and progressive functional decline over 2 years. She reports walking has become increasingly difficult, and she experiences numbness in both feet that worsens with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Standing still is worse than walking, and walking backwards or with hip flexion (leaning on a walker) improves symptoms. The patient reports increased pain during extension-based movements and relief with flexion-based positioning. She has reduced daily activity significantly and requires assistive devices.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known history of lumbar spondylosis diagnosed 10 years ago. Patient reports no recent trauma, falls, or acute exacerbations. She denies incontinence, retention, or bowel changes. She is on treatment for hypertension but is otherwise systemically well.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity strength is 4+/5 in hip flexors and ankle dorsiflexors. Patellar reflexes are diminished bilaterally (1+). Sensory testing reveals decreased sensation in both feet in an L5-S1 distribution. The lumbar spine shows marked stiffness with minimal forward bending capability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT imaging of the lumbar spine demonstrates severe multilevel degenerative stenosis involving L3-L4, L4-L5, and L5-S1 levels. There is marked narrowing of the spinal canal, lateral recess stenosis, and facet-mediated stenosis. Disc bulging and osteophyte formation are prominent contributors.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spinal stenosis is an atypical presentation in an elderly patient with confounding comorbidities and poor symptom-imaging correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old man with a 20-year history of poorly controlled hypertension (BP 160-180 mmHg) and diabetes presents with acute-onset dizziness and imbalance. He reports vertigo with nausea and one-sided facial numbness. Symptom onset was sudden this morning without warning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot stand or walk without assistance. Double vision occurs intermittently. Right-sided coordinated movements are impaired, particularly fine motor tasks. Nausea is provoked by head movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior transient ischemic attack 5 years ago. Currently on aspirin monotherapy without additional antiplatelet or anticoagulation cover. No recent illness or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the patient demonstrates left-sided facial weakness with drooping of the left mouth corner and asymmetric smile. Dysdiadochokinesia is present on right-sided rapid alternating movements. Horizontal nystagmus is observed on rightward gaze. The Romberg test shows significant postural instability. Cranial nerve examination shows miosis and ptosis on the left, consistent with Horner's syndrome.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in the left brainstem at the level of the pons, measuring 8 millimeters by 6 millimeters. The lesion involves the dorsomedial pontine tegmentum. Fluid-attenuated inversion recovery sequences confirm acute ischemic stroke. Magnetic resonance angiography of the intracranial vasculature shows no significant stenosis or occlusion.

puzzles.explanation: Pontine stroke causing crossed syndrome with ipsilateral eye movement abnormality and contralateral motor hemiplegia.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old recreational runner presents with a 3-month history of gradual-onset medial ankle pain. The dull ache worsens with activity and improves with rest. No recent ankle injury recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking, stair climbing, and running aggravate pain. Patient reports difficulty maintaining arch support during prolonged standing. Arch support and rest provide relief. Jumping provokes sharp medial ankle pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient is a recreational runner who recently increased training volume by 30% over the past 2 months without adequate progressive loading. There is no history of ankle trauma or prior ankle instability. The patient notes that symptoms correlate with increased running mileage. Body mass index is 28, contributing to increased lower extremity loading during activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the posterior tibial tendon approximately 4 centimeters proximal to the medial malleolus. The single-leg heel raise test reveals mild difficulty with heel elevation on the affected side, with ability to complete 10 repetitions compared to 15 on the unaffected side. The arch shows subtle flattening with weight-bearing compared to the contralateral foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the posterior tibial tendon demonstrates mild hypoechoic signal within the tendon substance, suggesting early tendinopathy without significant fiber disruption. The tendon measures 6.2 millimeters in anteroposterior diameter, mildly enlarged compared to contralateral side at 5.8 millimeters. Power Doppler shows mild increased vascularity at the tendon surface consistent with early inflammatory response.

puzzles.explanation: Stage 1 PTTD involves tendon inflammation with preserved foot structure and function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old runner presents with lateral knee pain that develops during running activities. The pain is localized to the lateral knee joint line and describes a sharp, stabbing quality. The 4-week onset during increased training volume could represent iliotibial band syndrome, lateral meniscal tear, lateral ligament sprain, or patellofemoral pain syndrome.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly aggravated when running more than 3 kilometers, particularly with downhill running. The patient reports pain with knee flexion between 20-30 degrees during the running gait cycle. Pain is eased with rest, ice, and foam rolling of the lateral thigh. Walking at normal pace does not provoke symptoms, but stairs require modification.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient increased running volume from 15 kilometers per week to 35 kilometers per week over 4 weeks while training for a marathon. A recent change in running surface to concrete from park trails preceded symptom onset. The patient reports running in worn-out shoes with poor cushioning. No history of prior knee injury or lower extremity biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The Ober test reveals hip adduction limited to 15 degrees on the affected side compared to 25 degrees contralaterally, indicating iliotibial band tightness. Hip abductor strength is 4/5 on the affected side versus 5/5 on the contralateral side. Noble compression test is positive. Knee effusion and ligamentous laxity are absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates thickening of the iliotibial band measuring 4 millimeters compared to normal 2-3 millimeters. T2-weighted imaging shows edema adjacent to the lateral femoral condyle at the region of band friction. No meniscal tears, ligamentous injuries, or cartilage defects are identified. The vastus lateralis muscle shows normal signal intensity without fatty changes.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ITBS indicates systemic muscular imbalances extending beyond local IT band tightness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman fell from standing height while getting out of bed 2 hours ago. She reports severe pain in the hip and groin region and is unable to place any weight on the affected leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The affected limb is held in external rotation and slight flexion at rest. Any attempt to internally rotate or abduct the hip triggers severe pain. She cannot perform transfers or ambulate without maximum assistance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a history of osteoporosis diagnosed 15 years ago with poor medication compliance. She takes calcium and vitamin D supplementation but reports limited physical activity due to age. The fall occurred during a simple transfer at home with no direct trauma to the hip region. She lives alone and could not call for help for 2 hours.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the affected limb is held in external rotation at approximately 45 degrees and slight hip flexion of 10-15 degrees. The limb appears 2 centimeters shorter than the contralateral side. Palpation reveals crepitus in the hip region and severe pain with any movement. Sensation and pulses in the lower extremity are intact. No skin tenting or open wound is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the pelvis show a comminuted intertrochanteric fracture with the fracture line extending from the greater trochanter to the lesser trochanter. The fracture extends into the medial cortex, classified as an unstable intertrochanteric fracture. Bone density appears osteoporotic on radiographs. CT imaging confirms no hip dislocation and assesses fracture displacement suitable for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric hip fractures are common in elderly and typically require surgical fixation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old woman presents with difficulty and pain during vaginal penetration, including both sexual intercourse and gynecological examination. The patient describes an involuntary muscular response that prevents penetration. The onset 2 years ago could suggest vaginismus, vulvodynia, endometriosis, or pelvic floor myofascial pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 31-year-old woman reports difficulty with vaginal penetration during sexual intercourse for the past 2 years. She describes an involuntary muscular tightening that prevents entry despite her desire to proceed. Pain accompanies any penetration attempt. No prior trauma or significant life events coincided with onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports no history of sexual trauma or abuse. She grew up in a conservative religious environment with limited sex education. She denies use of hormonal contraceptives and reports no prior gynecological surgeries. Menarche was at age 13 with regular menstrual cycles. General health is good with no other medical conditions or chronic pain syndromes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, visual inspection of the vulva and introitus appears normal with no visible lesions, redness, or atrophy. However, when attempting to insert a single finger at the introitus, involuntary circumvaginal muscle contraction is palpated, preventing insertion despite adequate lubrication. The pelvic floor muscles are notably tense and hypertonic. Internal examination cannot be completed due to protective muscle guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transperineal ultrasound demonstrates increased muscle thickness in the bulbocavernosus and superficial transverse perineal muscles, consistent with hypertonic pelvic floor musculature. Increased echogenicity in the pelvic floor muscles suggests chronically contracted state. Pelvic MRI rules out structural abnormalities including endometriosis, fibroids, or anatomical variants affecting the vaginal canal.

puzzles.explanation: Vaginismus is primary vaginismus with lifelong involuntary pelvic floor muscle contraction preventing any penetration, associated with psychological factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old football player presents with acute-onset severe pain over the anterolateral hip following direct shoulder-to-hip contact during a tackle. Pain is localized and well-demarcated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is unable to return to play and has severe limitation with hip flexion and trunk movements. Walking is difficult with a pronounced limp, and the patient favors the unaffected side. Pain is exacerbated by activities requiring hip flexion and lateral trunk flexion away from the injury. Running and kicking are impossible due to pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism: direct shoulder-to-hip contact during tackle. No prior hip injuries. No history of bone fragility or medical conditions affecting bone quality.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, significant tenderness and swelling are present over the iliac crest, particularly at the attachment site of the abdominal musculature. Ecchymosis is developing over the area. Hip flexion produces pain at the iliac attachment and anterior hip region. The anterior superior iliac spine and anterior inferior iliac spine are palpated and found to be intact without step-off or displacement. Hip strength testing is limited by pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the pelvis show no evidence of fracture through the iliac crest or acetabulum. CT imaging of the pelvis reveals soft tissue swelling over the anterolateral hip with blood products within the muscles at the iliac attachment. No occult fracture is identified. Bone integrity is maintained with normal cortical thickness.

puzzles.explanation: Hip pointer shows typical iliac crest contusion from direct contact with localized bruising and swelling.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old recreational soccer player presents with acute-onset lateral knee pain after a twisting injury to the lower limb. The patient felt the knee 'give way' during the mechanism and experienced immediate, severe pain. Onset is acute, mechanism is non-contact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient is severely limited with weight-bearing and has a sense of instability with any dynamic movement or single-leg stance. Immediate swelling suggests significant ligamentous or meniscal involvement. Pain is lateral and worsens with any rotational or cutting movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a prior history of an ankle sprain on the contralateral side 6 months ago. There is no prior knee injury. The soccer injury occurred when the patient landed on the outside of the foot with the knee in slight flexion, forcing the knee into inversion and external rotation. The patient continued playing briefly before severe pain forced him to stop.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is elicited along the lateral collateral ligament from the fibular head distally. The varus stress test at 30 degrees of knee flexion shows increased laxity compared to the unaffected side, graded as 1+ opening. The anterior drawer test is negative, and Lachman's test is negative, making anterior cruciate ligament tear less likely. Mild joint effusion is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates a partial tear of the lateral collateral ligament with interruption of approximately 50% of the fiber bundle at the level of the fibular head. T2-weighted imaging shows edema and hemorrhage within and around the ligament. The anterior cruciate ligament and menisci appear intact. Soft tissue swelling is present in the lateral knee compartment.

puzzles.explanation: Posterolateral injuries often involve LCL plus other structures and present with complex instability patterns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A neonate with bilateral foot deformity noted at birth. Parent reports inability to passively correct foot position to neutral. Family history significant for similar presentation in maternal grandfather.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Feeding and sleeping unaffected. Able to move hips and knees freely. Parents concerned about future weight-bearing and whether foot position will prevent normal gait development.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Full-term vaginal delivery at 39 weeks. No maternal infections, medications, or diabetes during pregnancy. No other congenital anomalies. Foot deformity noted immediately at birth by delivery team.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral feet demonstrate equinus deformity with inversion and adduction of the forefoot. Pirani score measures 6/6, indicating moderate deformity. Passive dorsiflexion limited to 5 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anteroposterior and lateral foot radiographs demonstrate talar-navicular uncovering. Talocalcaneal angle measures 15 degrees (normal >20 degrees). Tibiocalcaneal angle shows equinus positioning of 45 degrees.

puzzles.explanation: Congenital clubfoot is a fixed foot deformity requiring early treatment with stretching and casting to correct and prevent gait dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old recreational runner reports gradual onset of medial foot and ankle pain over 6 weeks. Pain worse with morning stiffness and gradually improves with activity. Denies prior significant ankle injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain increases significantly after running, particularly when going uphill. Walking on uneven surfaces elicits discomfort. Foot appears to pronate excessively during gait, and patient reports difficulty with single-leg stance activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recently increased running frequency from 3 to 5 days weekly. History of mild pes planus bilaterally. Takes no anti-inflammatory medications. No history of systemic inflammatory conditions or previous ankle surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, point tenderness elicited along the posterior tibial tendon just posterior and inferior to the medial malleolus. Single-leg heel raise test demonstrates weakness and inability to maintain inversion at midfoot. Mild swelling noted in the medial ankle region with positive tenosynovitis sign.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals hypoechoic thickening of the posterior tibial tendon measuring 8mm in cross-sectional diameter (normal <5mm). Fluid collection evident within the tendon sheath. Power Doppler demonstrates increased neovascularization within and around the tendon.

puzzles.explanation: Tibialis posterior tendinopathy results in progressive foot dysfunction and arch collapse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old accountant reports gradual progressive imbalance over 2 years. Wife notes increased stumbling and difficulties with coordination. Patient reports trouble with fine motor tasks such as writing and buttoning clothes.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports unsteady gait that worsens on uneven terrain or in dim lighting. Requires rail support for stairs. Reaches for objects but overshoots the target consistently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began insidiously without preceding infection or trauma. No significant past medical history. Father had similar symptoms starting in late 40s. Patient denies alcohol use. No medications with neurotoxic potential. Denies sensory symptoms or weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tandem stance maintained for only 2 seconds with eyes open, impossible with eyes closed. Finger-nose testing demonstrates consistent overshooting bilaterally. Rapid alternating hand pronation-supination becomes irregular and slows after 2-3 cycles.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows selective atrophy of the cerebellum, particularly the midline vermis. Scattered white matter signal changes in the deep periventricular regions. Genetic testing reveals a pathogenic trinucleotide repeat expansion in the SCA region.

puzzles.explanation: Hereditary spinocerebellar ataxia with progressive incoordination, characteristic cerebellar signs, and confirmed genetic mutation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with recurrent episodes of severe vertigo lasting 20 minutes to 3 hours. Associated with left-sided hearing loss, tinnitus described as low-frequency roaring, and left ear fullness sensation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Episodes are completely unpredictable and incapacitating—patient must lie down during attacks. Severe nausea and vomiting accompany most episodes. Avoids driving due to fear of attacks. Between episodes, reports only mild imbalance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: First episode 18 months ago. Frequency has remained stable at 2-3 attacks monthly despite rigorous sodium restriction and diuretic trial over 6 months. No recent head trauma, no ototoxic medications, no prior vertigo before this illness began.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: At rest, no spontaneous nystagmus. Dix-Hallpike and Romberg maneuvers normal. Caloric irrigation shows reduced ice water response on left (40% reduction compared to right). Weber test: sound lateralizes to left ear (bone conduction intact). Audiometry shows low-frequency air-bone gap on left side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Gadolinium-enhanced MRI demonstrates enhancement of the left endolymphatic sac and duct, consistent with endolymphatic hydrops. High-resolution imaging shows increased endolymphatic volume occupying >50% of the cochlear and vestibular spaces. Pure-tone audiometry shows low-frequency sensorineural hearing loss pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Meniere's disease requires comprehensive management including medical therapy, dietary modification, balance training between episodes, psychological support for anxiety and unpredictability, and monitoring for progressive hearing loss and functional decline.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe calf pain during activity, accompanied by an audible 'pop'. Rapid onset of swelling and inability to bear weight on the affected leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to plantar flex foot or heel walk. Attempted jogging elicits severe calf pain. Swelling progressed over 24 hours with prominent bruising visible on the medial ankle and foot sole. Calf tightness severely restricts ankle dorsiflexion range.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recreational athlete with no prior calf problems. No warning signs before injury. No relevant medical history or current medications. Relatively infrequent training schedule (twice weekly).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable defect detected in the gastrocnemius-soleus complex at the musculotendinous junction. Positive Thompson test with absent plantar flexion response on calf squeeze. Severe pain on passive ankle dorsiflexion. Swelling and bruising extending to medial ankle with ecchymosis tracking down to sole.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates complete tear of the medial gastrocnemius muscle with muscle fiber retraction. Hemorrhagic collection noted within the muscular compartment measuring 4 x 6 cm. MRI confirms soleus muscle involvement with T2 hyperintense edema throughout the muscle belly and proximal musculotendinous junction.

puzzles.explanation: Tennis leg shows acute medial gastrocnemius muscle tear from explosive pushing movement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old soccer player with chronic ankle pain that developed gradually over 8 months. Denies recent injury, though had minor ankle sprains several years ago. Pain is constant but varies with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Kicking activities consistently trigger sharp anterior ankle pain. Pain worsens with plantarflexion combined with inversion. Walking on uneven terrain elicits discomfort. Stiffness occurs after periods of inactivity, gradually improving with gentle movement. Running speed significantly affected.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-level athlete with progressive symptoms and no single injury event. Training volume is substantial (3-4 sessions weekly). Has not undergone ankle surgery previously. No systemic disease or inflammatory indicators reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, palpable tenderness at the anterior tibiofibular interval. Plantarflexion-inversion combined movement reproduces pain sharply. Anterior ankle drawer test negative. Anterior impingement test positive with pain reproduction. Ankle plantarflexion range 50 degrees with restriction felt anteriorly.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals anterior osteophyte formation at the talonavicular joint margin measuring 4mm. Hypertrophic soft tissue nodule present anterior to the capsule with T2 hyperintense signal. Anterior joint capsule demonstrates thickening and folding consistent with synovitis. Plain radiographs confirm subtle talonavicular joint space osteophytes.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior ankle impingement results from osteophyte formation typically after ankle sprains or repetitive inversion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old female soccer player reports bilateral anterior knee pain present for 4 months. Pain worsens gradually throughout the day and after sports practice. Denies specific injury or trauma; pain developed insidiously.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain aggravated significantly by running and jumping activities. Ascending and descending stairs elicits bilateral anterior knee pain. Prolonged sitting with knees bent increases discomfort. Patient reports knee stiffness after practice that resolves with activity. Reduced training tolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms coincided with increased training frequency and intensity at start of competitive season. Growth spurt noted over past 18 months. No prior knee injuries or surgeries. Denies morning pain or night symptoms. Currently active in competitive soccer and training 5 days weekly.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symptoms coincided with competitive season intensification and documented growth spurt over 18 months. No prior knee injuries. Notably, pain is activity-dependent with no morning symptoms or nocturnal awakening; resolves with rest.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bilateral hip abductor weakness (4/5) with positive single-leg squat test showing dynamic knee valgus. Special testing reveals patellar tilt sign positive bilaterally. Q-angle 16° (elevated for paediatric age). Palpable tenderness over medial patellar facet bilaterally. VMO activation deficit on manual muscle testing.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent patellofemoral pain requires comprehensive biomechanical assessment and correction of kinetic chain dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old gentleman with acute onset of severe dizziness and ataxia following sudden excruciating occipital headache. Unable to stand or walk independently since symptom onset 6 hours prior. Reports associated nausea and vomiting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 71-year-old gentleman with acute onset severe dizziness and inability to stand or walk. Sudden occipital headache this morning, accompanied by nausea and vomiting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severely limited mobility — cannot stand or walk without maximal assistance. Speech is slurred and difficult to understand. Rapid, coordinated movements of the hands are severely impaired.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, dysmetria profound on finger-nose testing with severe overshooting bilaterally. Severe gait ataxia evident requiring maximal assistance for any standing attempt. Positive Romberg test with inability to maintain stance even with eyes open. Hypotonia noted in upper extremities. Nystagmus present with lateral gaze, horizontally directed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Profound dysmetria on finger-nose testing with severe bilateral overshooting. Marked gait ataxia requiring maximal assistance. Maintains abnormal balance even with eyes open (positive Romberg). Horizontal nystagmus evident on lateral gaze.

puzzles.explanation: Hereditary cerebellar ataxia with progressive incoordination, characteristic MRI findings of cerebellar atrophy, and positive genetic testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational skier reports medial knee pain that began during a fall while skiing and has persisted for 2 weeks. Pain is present during walking and worsens with side-to-side movements. Swelling has been consistent since the injury. The presentation could encompass various knee ligament or meniscal injuries.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational skier reports medial-sided knee pain that began during a fall 2 weeks ago. Pain worsens with side-to-side movements and walking. Swelling has been present since injury onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports pain with weight-bearing activities when the foot is externally rotated. Downstairs negotiation is limited. Normal walking is managed but feels unstable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the valgus stress test is positive at 30 degrees knee flexion with increased medial joint opening. Palpation reveals tenderness along the medial joint capsule and ligament. Passive knee extension reproduces pain. Anterior drawer test is negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Valgus stress test at 30 degrees knee flexion demonstrates increased medial joint opening. Medial joint line tenderness is present. Anterior drawer test is negative.

puzzles.explanation: Isolated MCL sprains are relatively common and often occur with valgus stress mechanisms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old post-operative patient (Day 2 following abdominal surgery) reports shallow breathing and reduced exercise tolerance. Complains of mild dyspnea on minimal exertion. Mild dry cough present but non-productive.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to ambulate more than 5 metres without dyspnea and desaturation. Splinting behavior evident during coughing. Baseline oxygen saturation 96% on room air at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Surgical procedure was open partial colectomy under general anesthesia with 90-minute operative time. Pre-operative history significant for 40 pack-year smoking history, though quit 5 years ago. Mild COPD with baseline FEV1/FVC ratio 65%. Pain adequately controlled with multimodal analgesia including epidural catheter.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced breath sounds throughout right lower lobe with stony dullness to percussion. Respiratory rate elevated at 22 breaths per minute. Oxygen saturation 92% on room air. No clubbing, cyanosis, or peripheral edema. Chest wall pain on deep palpation limits voluntary deep inspiration.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates wedge-shaped radiodensity at right lung base with obscuration of right heart border. No mediastinal shift or pleural effusion. Remainder of lung fields clear. Findings consistent with lobar collapse.

puzzles.explanation: Post-operative atelectasis from pain-limited breathing and mucus retention. Common complication responsive to incentive spirometry, pain management, and bronchial hygiene.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old woman reports episodic vertigo occurring 2-4 times monthly without clear triggers. Episodes last 30 minutes to 4 hours. Associated with frontal headache and motion sensitivity during attacks. Denies hearing loss or tinnitus.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During episodes, lying still in quiet dark room is essential. Visual motion and head movement exacerbate dizziness severely. Bright lights or screens worsen symptoms. Nausea accompanies most attacks though vomiting occurs in only 30%. Complete symptom resolution between episodes with normal balance and coordination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Personal history of migraine headaches since age 18, typically responding to triptans. Mother and maternal grandmother both with migraines. Symptoms began 18 months ago, initially attributed to inner ear disease. Audiometric testing normal. Caloric irrigation normal bilaterally. No recent vestibular testing abnormalities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination during asymptomatic period, gait normal and balance unimpaired. Nystagmus not observed. Dix-Hallpike maneuver produces no vertigo or nystagmus. Weber and Rinne testing normal. Cranial nerves intact. Head impulse test normal. No spontaneous nystagmus on straight-ahead gaze.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI with and without gadolinium demonstrates no acute abnormality or demyelinating lesions. No mass effect or structural pathology identified. White matter signal intensity normal. Cerebellopontine angle clear bilaterally. No evidence of temporal bone or inner ear abnormality. Caloric testing from vestibular laboratory confirms symmetrical responses.

puzzles.explanation: Vestibular Migraine shows the classic association of migraine with vertigo/dizziness in a young female with visual aura and photophobia, normal objective testing between episodes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old teacher with a 3-month history of progressive medial ankle and arch discomfort. Pain develops gradually throughout the day and is particularly bothersome at end of workday. Denies acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged standing aggravates symptoms significantly. Stair climbing and walking downhill cause sharp pain along the medial foot. Patient notes arch feels weak and unstable, particularly on walking surfaces that lack support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupation requires standing 6+ hours daily. History of chronic ankle sprains (5+ episodes over 15 years) with subsequent lateral ligament laxity. Early signs of hypertension managed with lifestyle modification. No diabetes or systemic inflammatory disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible midfoot collapse and valgus heel positioning evident during standing. Positive too-many-toes sign observed on visual inspection. Posterior tibial strength graded 3+/5 with reproduction of pain during muscle testing. Medial ankle swelling present with tenderness along tendon course.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI imaging demonstrates edema and partial tearing within the mid-portion of the posterior tibial tendon. Tendon diameter measures 7.5mm with signal changes indicating intratendinous mucoid degeneration. Mild lateral ankle joint space narrowing suggesting chronic instability pattern.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced posterior tibial tendon dysfunction produces chronic foot deformity requiring significant intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 5-year-old with congenital lower extremity weakness and sensory changes. Parents report difficulty with continence management.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Non-ambulatory, uses wheelchair for all mobility. Requires contact guard for transfers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prenatal diagnosis of spinal defect with early surgical repair. Known hydrocephalus managed with shunt. History of recurrent infections.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Complete paraplegia with grade 0/5 motor strength bilaterally in lower extremities. Sensory level corresponds to L3. Significant hip and knee flexion contractures.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spinal MRI demonstrates segmental spinal cord aplasia with lipoma extending from dural sac. Ventricular enlargement from hydrocephalus evident on brain MRI. Spinal X-ray shows kyphotic deformity at surgical site.

puzzles.explanation: Spina bifida is a neural tube defect with incomplete closure causing paraplegia, sensory loss, and often hydrocephalus requiring urgent surgical repair.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old gentleman with sudden onset difficulty seeing to one side. Reports words appear cut off when reading. Wife noticed he seems confused this morning. No eye pain. Onset was acute, while sitting at breakfast.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot safely navigate his home environment. Repeatedly walks into furniture and doorframes on the right side. Wife reports he seemed confused about which room he was in earlier. Denies awareness of any visual problem—attributes difficulty to 'not paying attention.'

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of atrial fibrillation (anticoagulated with warfarin, INR 2.1). Hypertension managed with lisinopril. Prior stroke 3 years ago affecting right motor cortex (minor residual weakness). Denies recent falls or head trauma. Symptom onset this morning with acute awareness of vision loss.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual field confrontation demonstrates right-sided field loss equally in both eyes. Pupils reactive, eye movements normal. Unable to recall examiner's name or the date. Mild increase in right knee reflexes. No focal motor weakness on standard strength testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Acute MRI with DWI sequences shows restricted diffusion in the left posterior circulation distribution. Left occipital cortex and medial temporal lobe involved. Calcarine fissure region includes acute signal changes. Mild involvement of left thalamic region on T2 imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Acute PCA stroke affecting occipital lobe with homonymous visual field loss and possible prosopagnosia from temporal lobe involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-year-old child with significant developmental delay across motor and cognitive domains. Parents report persistent weakness and difficulty with balance-dependent activities. Known genetic syndrome diagnosed prenatally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child requires handrail support for stairs and adult assistance for mobility. Cannot tolerate sustained activity due to rapid fatigue. Unable to perform single-leg balance tasks or execute coordinated running.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Developmental trajectory significantly delayed: did not walk until 20 months. Born with known chromosomal abnormality. Prior cardiac assessment documented. Currently enrolled in multimodal rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable generalized ligament laxity with passive ROM notably increased. Knee joints demonstrate hyperextension. Core stability severely compromised. Lower extremity strength graded 3/5. Postural control absent without external support.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine imaging reveals evidence of cervical myelopathy risk with atlantoaxial subluxation noted on flexion-extension films. Spinal cord appears normal. Skeletal findings consistent with generalized connective tissue laxity. Scoliosis screening reveals mild curvature progression over time.

puzzles.explanation: Down syndrome is a chromosomal disorder (trisomy 21) with characteristic facial features, intellectual disability, and often cardiac abnormalities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old university student reports wrist pain and swelling after a fall on outstretched hand while playing basketball 3 days ago. Pain is localized to the thumb-side of the wrist. Despite mild swelling, patient reports being able to use the hand for most daily tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to grip or squeeze without discomfort. Gripping activities and return to basketball are not tolerated. Wrist extension range of motion mildly reduced but does not severely limit daily function. Radial-sided (thumb-side) swelling persists on examination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy mechanism with fall from height of 6 feet. Wrist extended at impact. Initial swelling subsided somewhat but residual discomfort persists. Anxiety regarding healing timeline and return to sport. Denies prior wrist injuries. Right-hand dominant; injury on dominant side.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness elicited at the radial-sided scaphoid tubercle. Positive scaphoid compression test reproducing wrist pain. Radial-sided wrist swelling palpated. Pain with ulnar deviation combined with wrist extension. Range of motion slightly limited in extension (50 degrees) and radial deviation (15 degrees). Neurovascular status normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial plain radiographs (AP, lateral, and scaphoid views) appear normal without obvious fracture line. CT imaging (High-resolution thin-section) demonstrates non-displaced fracture of the scaphoid waist with minimal separation (<2mm). MRI confirms T2 hyperintense signal through the fracture line with surrounding bone marrow edema. No ligamentous injury visualized.

puzzles.explanation: Acute scaphoid fracture from typical FOOSH injury with normal initial X-rays requiring advanced imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive swimmer presents with insidious-onset lower back pain localized to one side of the midline. Pain worsens with backward arching or hyperextension movements. The pain is sharp and increases with high-intensity training or sprinting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences pain with butterfly stroke and backstroke more than freestyle. Pain intensifies with training progression and return to competitive intensity. Prolonged sitting and forward bending are tolerated without significant symptoms. Pain is worst at end-of-day after training.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has trained competitively for 5 years with high training volume and repetitive spinal extension loading. Pain onset occurred 8 weeks ago coinciding with increased training intensity. No traumatic event or acute injury. Family history is notable for low back pain in both parents.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral lumbar pain is reproduced with single-leg hyperextension test on the affected side. Lumbar extension combined with rotation toward the painful side increases symptoms. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle tenderness at the level of pain. Lower extremity neurological examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT scan demonstrates a unilateral pars interarticularis defect at the L5 level with intact vertebral body and disc. SPECT imaging shows increased uptake at the symptomatic level, indicating active bone stress. Plain radiographs may appear normal; advanced imaging is required for definitive visualization.

puzzles.explanation: Spondylolysis is the classic young athlete presentation with stress fracture mechanism from repetitive hyperextension movements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old male reports acute onset bilateral leg pain and progressive weakness over 48 hours. He has noticed recent loss of bladder control and numbness in the perineal area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than short distances; stairs are impossible. Reports severe difficulty lifting feet off ground when walking. Symptoms worsen significantly within 10 minutes of lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long history of low back pain with known disc disease. Acute worsening triggered by occupational heavy lifting 2 days ago. Currently unable to voluntarily move feet or toes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise test reproduces radicular pain bilaterally. Lower extremity myotomes show grade 2-3 strength in ankle dorsiflexors and plantarflexors. Anal sphincter tone is diminished on rectal examination. Patellar and Achilles reflexes are absent bilaterally; perianal sensory testing shows loss of sensation in S2-S5 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a large central disc herniation at L4-L5 level causing severe compression of multiple nerve roots in the spinal canal. There is abnormal signal change within the cord and loss of fluid around the compressed nerves.

puzzles.explanation: Cauda equina syndrome is the textbook surgical emergency with classic red flag symptoms requiring immediate imaging and intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old patient presents with progressive difficulty lifting the left foot during walking over the past 2 weeks. The foot feels heavy and unresponsive, with no pain. Gait has become increasingly awkward, and the patient has begun tripping.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports tripping frequently and catching the left toe when walking, particularly on uneven surfaces. Climbing stairs is significantly impaired; the patient cannot dorsiflex the ankle against gravity. Running and sports are impossible. The patient describes walking with a high-stepping gait to compensate for the dorsiflexion weakness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was acute with no preceding trauma or injury. The patient recalls a pressure point or direct blow to the lateral knee area approximately 10 days before symptom onset. Recent prolonged sitting with leg crossed at the knee and tight band-aid or wrap around the upper fibular region preceded the symptoms by several days.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle dorsiflexion strength is 0-1/5 on the left; plantarflexion and inversion are intact at 5/5. Great toe extension is severely weakened. The patient demonstrates sensory loss over the dorsum of the foot and first web space between the great toe and second toe. Tapping over the fibular head reproduces symptoms, suggesting nerve entrapment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the left knee and proximal tibia shows external compression of the common peroneal nerve at the fibular head level. T2-weighted imaging reveals increased signal within the nerve distal to the compression point, indicating axonal swelling and denervation changes. No structural lesion or tumor identified.

puzzles.explanation: Common peroneal nerve compression palsy from prolonged pressure with foot drop, sensory loss in nerve distribution, and conduction block on EMG.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old office administrator reports bilateral neck and shoulder tension with occipital headaches occurring 3-4 times weekly. The discomfort is described as a dull ache with occasional sharp shooting sensations, worsening as the workday progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms triggered specifically by sustained computer work and forward head posturing. Patient avoids prolonged desk tasks but tolerates short activity bursts. Morning stiffness present but improves with movement. No neurological symptoms reported.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 15-year history of sedentary desk work with recent job transition increasing daily screen time to 6+ hours. Recent workplace ergonomic changes (monitor repositioning downward). No prior neck injury, no radicular history. Stress levels elevated in past 3 months coinciding with symptom onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical rotation limited bilaterally with headache reproduction at end-range; flexion/extension restricted approximately 20 degrees. Upper trapezius demonstrates marked hypertonia with trigger points; levator scapulae tender on palpation. Scapular positioning: elevated and protracted bilaterally. Neurological testing: normal reflexes and sensation throughout upper limbs; no dermatomal deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays: reversal of normal lordosis with anterior head position (approximately 3 cm deviation from neutral alignment). MRI: mild degenerative disc bulging at C4-C5 and C5-C6 without neural foraminal stenosis or cord compression. No acute structural pathology. Soft tissue signal shows myofascial thickening in bilateral upper trapezius consistent with chronic muscular tension.

puzzles.explanation: Neck tension shows chronic work-related neck pain with psychological stress component and severe postural deviation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old male with a desk-based job presents with insidious-onset lower back and hip discomfort that has gradually worsened over the past year. He reports difficulty maintaining an upright posture and finds that his symptoms worsen as the day progresses.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered and amplified by prolonged static positions, particularly sitting at desks or in cars. Activities requiring floor-level work or rising from low surfaces provoke significant discomfort. Standing and walking provide relief. Patient notes postural 'corrections' ease symptoms within minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset was insidious without trauma. The patient has maintained a sedentary lifestyle for a decade with minimal structured exercise. No history of previous back injury, no progressive neurological symptoms, and no constitutional red flags. Current medications and comorbidities are unremarkable.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, bilateral hip flexor tightness is evident with positive Thomas test. Hip abductor weakness is noted at 4+/5 bilaterally. Lumbar erector spinae and rectus femoris are tight; abdominal core strength is poor. Pelvic tilt is anterior with excessive lumbar lordosis. Neurological examination is entirely normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar spine shows minimal degenerative changes without disc herniation, stenosis, or facet arthrosis of clinical significance. The spinal cord and nerve roots are not compressed. Imaging is essentially normal and does not identify structural pathology; findings support a muscular and postural etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Lower crossed syndrome occurs in athletes with training-related muscular imbalance and movement dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old male reports chronic low back pain with intermittent pain radiating into the left lower leg. Symptoms began 5 months ago and have worsened despite initial post-operative improvement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient demonstrates severe limitations with bending forward; even slight flexion beyond 15 degrees triggers back pain. Sit-to-stand transfers are slow and guarded with pain reproduction. Walking tolerance is reduced to 10-15 minutes before requiring rest. The patient reports difficulty with rotation movements and cannot lift objects heavier than 10 pounds.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient underwent fusion surgery for spondylolisthesis grade 2 with spinal stenosis. Prior to surgery, 3 years of conservative management had failed. Post-operatively, the patient had initial improvement for 3 months, followed by recurrent pain. Current medications include opioids; the patient is 4 weeks into physical therapy with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar flexion limited to 20 degrees; forward bending reproduction test triggers left lower extremity radicular pain at approximately L5 distribution. Straight leg raise positive on left at 60 degrees reproducing radicular symptoms. Hip flexor strength reduced to 4/5 bilaterally. Palpation over surgical scar elicits localized tenderness without warmth or swelling.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative CT imaging shows successful fusion with solid bony bridging across the L4-L5 segment. Hardware positioning is appropriate without subsidence. However, MRI reveals adjacent segment degeneration at L3-L4 with left-sided foraminal stenosis and nerve root contact. T2-weighted imaging shows disc desiccation and early stenotic changes at the level above the fusion.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Spinal Fusion shows mid-term recovery (6 months) after two-level fusion with gradual functional improvement and early fusion evidence on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 4-year-old child presents with persistent head tilt to the right with marked cervical asymmetry. The parents report the child has always held the head in this position since infancy, though range has gradually decreased. No acute trauma or injury history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates difficulty looking directly forward or to the left; the head consistently returns to right lateral flexion. Forward head movement is severely limited, and the child compensates by rotating the entire trunk. Attempts to passively correct the head position cause the child to return immediately to the left-tilted posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The abnormal head posture was first noted at 6 weeks of age and has remained static. There is no history of birth trauma, intrauterine constraint, or neuromuscular disorder. The child was not confined to any particular position during infancy. Recent evaluation rules out vestibular dysfunction and vision deficits.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Abnormal posture first noted at 6 weeks of age and has remained unchanged. Parents deny birth trauma, intrauterine constraint, or confinement during infancy. No prior neuromuscular diagnosis or ophthalmologic problems identified.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine ultrasound shows the right sternocleidomastoid muscle with thickened, echogenic appearance consistent with fibrosis. MRI of the neck rules out intraspinal pathology, vascular malformations, or structural abnormalities. The cervical vertebrae demonstrate normal alignment without dysplasia or asymmetry affecting cervical positioning.

puzzles.explanation: Acquired acute torticollis from infection or inflammation responds to anti-inflammatory treatment and muscle relaxation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old distance runner reports posterior knee pain for 6 weeks. Pain is sharp and burning, worsens with running, and has progressively limited training volume. No acute injury event.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running downhill significantly aggravates the pain; the patient reports immediate discomfort when initiating downhill running on trails. High-speed activities and sudden deceleration reproducibly provoke sharp posterior knee pain. Walking is generally tolerated; however, descending stairs with full body weight loading triggers symptoms. Prolonged sitting with knees flexed beyond 90 degrees causes mild discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training error: 40% mileage increase over recent weeks with shift to hilly terrain and tempo running. Bilateral posterior knee pain noted, right > left. No prior knee pathology relevant to current complaint. Previous patellar tendinitis fully resolved 3 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted knee flexion with external rotation at 30° reproduces pain. Pain elicited with combined resisted ankle plantarflexion and knee flexion. Posterior knee joint line tender to palpation. Minimal effusion; soft tissue thickening palpable over popliteal tendon insertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the bilateral knees demonstrates thickening and signal hyperintensity within the popliteus tendon at its femoral origin, measuring approximately 7mm in cross-sectional diameter compared to normal 3-4mm. Fluid signal is noted in the popliteal sheath, indicating reactive tenosynovitis. T2-weighted imaging shows increased signal within the muscle belly compatible with myotendinous strain.

puzzles.explanation: Popliteal tendinopathy presents as posterolateral knee pain, commonly seen in distance runners.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old soccer player experienced a non-contact pivoting injury during match play 3 weeks ago with immediate onset of acute knee pain. The player reports persistent swelling, functional instability, and apprehension during weight-bearing activities, preventing return to sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The knee gives way unpredictably during lateral movements and pivoting maneuvers; the player demonstrates a guarded gait. The patient reports significant pain and instability when weight shifts from the injured leg or during rapid direction changes. Ascending and descending stairs are performed slowly with hand rail support. The player cannot tolerate running or cutting movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with planted foot and external rotation at the knee; the player felt a pop and immediate pain. The player was unable to continue playing and experienced rapid knee swelling within 2 hours. No prior knee injuries. The player reports difficulty with proprioception and describes feeling unable to trust the knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lachman test at 30 degrees of knee flexion demonstrates markedly increased anterior tibial translation with soft end-feel, graded as 2-3/3 for positive anterior cruciate ligament insufficiency. Anterior drawer test is positive at 90 degrees. Pivot shift test reproduces apprehension and dynamic instability with subluxation and reduction. Moderate knee effusion is palpable; pain-free range of motion is limited to 0-80 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates disruption of the anterior cruciate ligament fibers with diffuse increased signal throughout the substance of the ligament, consistent with acute complete tear. T2-weighted imaging shows bone bruising at the lateral femoral condyle and posterolateral tibial plateau, confirming the mechanism of injury. No meniscal tear is identified.

puzzles.explanation: ACL complete tear (Grade 3) shows acute traumatic mechanism with pivot shift and complete ligament disruption on MRI.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 31-year-old athlete presents with acute severe pain at the base of the great toe and swelling following a sudden forced backward bending of the toe during sport. Weight-bearing is immediately compromised.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot participate in sport and ambulates with significant pain avoidance. Push-off activities and toe-off during gait are severely limited. Functional activities requiring toe dorsiflexion or plantarflexion force are impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred with a forceful dorsiflexion load through the first ray when the player's planted foot was struck by an opponent, forcing the great toe into maximum extension. Immediate pain and swelling developed within minutes. The player reported a popping sensation at the time of injury. No prior first ray pathology or injury history documented.

clues.label: Examination
