clues.text: Acute trauma with fall or direct blow to the chest. Pain onset is immediate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness over the rib is elicited. Pain is reproduced with chest compression or rib palpation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may demonstrate fracture line, though many rib fractures are not visualized initially. Chest CT demonstrates fracture and excludes pulmonary injury.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fracture has chronic sequelae with persistent pain despite imaging healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with repetitive occupational activity presents with pain in the dorsal forearm. Pain is worse with gripping and wrist motion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by repetitive wrist extension and resisted finger extension. Patient reports visible swelling in the proximal forearm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms onset insidious over weeks. Patient is a rower/rock climber [or specific activity]. No prior forearm injury. No systemic disease or inflammatory history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the proximal dorsal forearm where extensors carpi radialis longus and brevis cross over extensor carpi radialis and extensor digitorum communis. Crepitus may be appreciated with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates tendon sheath inflammation and tenosynovitis at the intersection area. MRI shows inflammation and swelling.

puzzles.explanation: Intersection syndrome from intense gripping activities with characteristic location and crepitus finding.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Infant presents with weakness and flaccidity of one arm noticed immediately after delivery. Caregiver reports the arm hangs limp at the side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant unable to lift the affected arm against gravity. No protective movement or grasp response on affected side. Cries asymmetrically during distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring significant traction. Weakness noted in the delivery room. Parents report arm has remained weak over first 2 weeks with no improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Arm held in adduction and internal rotation (waiter's tip posture). Weakness predominantly in shoulder abduction and external rotation. Biceps and deltoid strength severely reduced. Wrist and finger movements relatively preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography documents denervation changes in affected muscles. MRI may show nerve root avulsion if severe.

puzzles.explanation: Erb's palsy is injury to the upper brachial plexus (C5-C6) from birth trauma, resulting in shoulder and arm weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute stroke involving anterior cerebral artery territory presents with acute neurological deficit. The patient has unilateral lower extremity weakness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient presents with acute onset of leg weakness and difficulty moving one side of the body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to walk independently. Severe difficulty with lower extremity control on one side affecting balance and mobility.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sudden onset during rest. History of hypertension and smoking. Symptoms began this morning with no prior similar episodes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lower extremity strength 3/5 on affected side with preserved upper extremity strength. Hyperreflexia and extensor plantar response noted. Mini-cog score 18/30 with mild confusion.

puzzles.explanation: Acute ACA stroke with lower extremity-predominant weakness, personality disturbance, and medial hemisphere involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged patient presents with central wrist pain and progressive loss of grip strength. Pain develops insidiously in the wrist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform sustained gripping (e.g., opening jars, holding tools). Fine motor tasks like writing become progressively difficult. Symptoms worsen with repeated wrist loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual, progressive onset over weeks to months. No acute injury, though patient reports past wrist trauma (fracture or ligament injury) years prior. Symptoms do not improve with rest alone.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness elicited over scaphoid-lunate joint. Wrist extension and radial deviation are restricted. Positive scaphoid shift test. Grip strength markedly reduced compared to contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate lunate flattening and fragmentation. MRI shows lunate necrosis and signal changes. Sequential imaging documents disease progression.

puzzles.explanation: Kienbock's disease with classic progressive presentation and imaging findings of avascular necrosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old software developer presents with numbness and tingling in the hand that worsened over 6 weeks. Symptoms are triggered by prolonged desk work. Pain is present in the forearm and hand.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with fine motor tasks and weakness in hand pinching. Resisted forearm movement in one direction reproduces forearm pain. Rest provides temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 8-10 hours daily at a desk with forearm resting on a padded armrest in pronation. He denies neck pain or shoulder involvement. Symptoms began insidiously without trauma. No history of elbow fracture or subluxation. The patient is right-hand dominant with symptoms on the right forearm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weakness demonstrated in thumb-down pronation grip (4/5 strength). Tinel's sign is positive in the proximal forearm between two muscle bodies. Median nerve distribution sensory testing shows mild diminishment.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the forearm demonstrates hypoechoic swelling of the median nerve as it passes between the two heads of the pronator teres muscle. Ultrasound shows nerve compression without mass effect or fracture. Cross-sectional area of the median nerve at this level is increased compared to the contralateral side.

puzzles.explanation: Pronator syndrome with median nerve compression at pronator teres origin, distinguished from CTS by location.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old male office worker presents with episodic unilateral headaches located in the temporal and occipital regions bilaterally asymmetrical. Symptoms correlate temporally with neck stiffness and worsen throughout the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Headache consistently provokes with prolonged sitting and neck extension movements. Relief noted when lying supine or with ice application. Certain neck postures reproducibly trigger pain within minutes. Neck stretching and movement paradoxically worsens the headache initially.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Three-year history of intermittent symptoms, initially triggered by a motor vehicle accident with whiplash mechanism. Progressive worsening associated with sedentary work environment. Reports no visual changes, hearing loss, or vestibular symptoms. Stress management minimally affects pain pattern.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical range of motion significantly restricted in extension (40 degrees; normal >50 degrees) and right rotation (55 degrees; normal >80 degrees). Extension immediately reproduces familiar occipital-temporal headache. Positive flexion-rotation test with pain reproduction. Cervical paraspinal muscles palpably tight and tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays demonstrate loss of cervical lordosis with subtle C4-C5 disc bulging. MRI shows C4-C5 and C5-C6 uncovertebral joint arthrosis with mild cervical spondylosis. Facet joint changes at C3-C4 level on the right correlate with restricted ipsilateral rotation and headache reproduction.

puzzles.explanation: Cervicogenic headache is the classic mechanical presentation with clear cervical-headache relationship and mechanical provocation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from carpal tunnel release surgery reports ongoing hand symptoms or recurrent symptoms. The patient is in the post-operative phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation may persist with ongoing hand dysfunction. The patient may demonstrate improved or persistent symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Carpal tunnel release surgery was performed weeks to months prior. Recovery is ongoing with variable symptom improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, hand sensation and strength are assessed to document improvement from pre-operative baseline. Scar tenderness may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging may include electrophysiology studies to document improvement in median nerve conduction if symptoms persist.

puzzles.explanation: Severe or chronic carpal tunnel cases may have prolonged recovery. This presentation requires aggressive scar management, sensory reeducation, and extended strengthening to achieve functional recovery of thenar muscles.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with acute anterior thigh pain that developed during activity. Pain is localised to the anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or activities requiring hip flexion and knee extension. The patient demonstrates functional limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset during or immediately after athletic activity. The patient may have inadequate warm-up or prior thigh injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable in the rectus femoris. Resisted knee extension or hip flexion reproduces pain. Swelling may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates muscle fiber disruption or hematoma within the rectus femoris. MRI characterises the tear location and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic rectus femoris strain may develop from repetitive sport movements and prior injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young to middle-aged adult presents with acute unilateral facial weakness. The patient reports facial droop and asymmetry that developed over the course of hours to days.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional impairment includes inability to close the eye completely on the affected side, difficulty with oral hygiene, and loss of facial symmetry during smiling or facial movements. Water escapes from the mouth during drinking due to lip weakness.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset was acute and unilateral with no prior warning. Recent upper respiratory tract infection or possible exposure history may be present. The patient denies prior facial weakness episodes and has no significant neurological history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral lower facial weakness is more pronounced than upper facial involvement, with incomplete eye closure on the affected side. Tear production may be reduced. Hyperacusis and altered taste are possible. Facial grimace reproduces characteristic asymmetry.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography demonstrates denervation changes consistent with acute peripheral nerve injury. Imaging of the head (CT or MRI) is unremarkable, ruling out central causes, space-occupying lesions, or brainstem pathology affecting the facial nucleus.

puzzles.explanation: Classic idiopathic Bell's palsy with acute unilateral facial nerve paralysis affecting forehead and orbicularis oris, suggesting upper motor neuron involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with acute neck trauma presents with neck pain and stiffness. The injury resulted from acceleration-deceleration mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with neck movement. The patient demonstrates guarding and protective postures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Motor vehicle accident or similar mechanism causing rapid neck acceleration-deceleration. Immediate onset of symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited by pain. Tenderness is present in cervical musculature. Neurological examination is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of cervical spine may be normal or show straightening. MRI may demonstrate soft tissue injury or disc herniation.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash associated disorder is acute post-traumatic injury from classic motor vehicle mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young baseball pitcher presents with medial elbow pain. The pain develops during throwing activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by throwing, particularly with high-velocity throwing. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with throwing sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athlete engaged in overhead throwing sports. Symptoms develop with increased throwing volume or intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, medial elbow tenderness is present over the medial epicondyle. Valgus stress test reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may demonstrate medial epicondyle avulsion or fragmentation. MRI characterises soft tissue involvement and ligamentous injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Little League elbow may progress to collateral ligament injury requiring surgical intervention and extended rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from amputation presents with significant functional loss requiring rehabilitation. The patient requires prosthetic fitting and training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with loss of limb function. The patient is learning to ambulate with prosthesis.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Amputation performed for limb loss from trauma, infection, or vascular disease. The patient is in early post-operative rehabilitation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, residual limb healing and shaping is assessed. Phantom pain may be present. Balance and gait training documents progress.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs of residual limb document healing. Advanced imaging monitors soft tissue changes with prosthetic adaptation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-amputation rehabilitation requires holistic management of phantom pain, stump conditioning and shaping, upper extremity strengthening for prosthetic ambulation, and psychological support during adjustment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with neck pain that radiates into one arm. The pain has developed gradually or acutely following minor trauma or activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is reproducibly triggered by certain neck movements, particularly extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient reports functional limitation with arm activities and difficulty maintaining posture.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of neck pain may be present. Symptoms began spontaneously or after minor trauma. The patient may have prior history of cervical spine problems. Symptoms are activity-dependent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck movement is limited, particularly extension and ipsilateral side-bending. Upper extremity weakness is demonstrated in a dermatomal distribution. Diminished reflexes may be present. Spurling's test reproduces radicular pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging of cervical spine demonstrates disc herniation at the affected level with nerve root compression and narrowing of the neural foramen. The herniated material displaces neural structures.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical disc herniation is the classic presentation with acute nerve root compression and clear imaging correlation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged adult presents with progressive weakness in the hands affecting fine motor tasks. The patient reports difficulty with activities including writing and buttoning clothes over the past several months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional decline is evident with impaired grip strength, difficulty with coordinated movements, and progressive inability to perform occupational tasks. The patient demonstrates a characteristic pattern of weakness that spreads to involve multiple limb regions over time.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began focally in one region and gradually spread to involve other body areas. The patient reports no sensory changes, preserved cognition, and no significant trauma or infection history. Progressive nature over 6-12 months noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, visible muscle fasciculations are present in affected regions, with measurable atrophy. Motor strength demonstrates asymmetric weakness pattern. Hyperreflexia is noted in hyperexcitable muscle groups. Cranial nerve examination may reveal mild tongue atrophy or fasciculations.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography (EMG) demonstrates acute denervation with fibrillations and positive sharp waves in affected muscles, consistent with anterior horn cell loss. Needle EMG confirms motor unit action potential changes. MRI of brain and spine excludes structural lesions or spinal cord compression.

puzzles.explanation: Sporadic ALS with spinal-onset presentation showing upper and lower motor neuron signs, fasciculations, and denervation on EMG.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old accountant presents with progressive lower back pain and leg weakness that worsens with extended periods of standing or walking. The patient reports a forward-leaning posture is more comfortable than standing upright. Symptoms began 18 months ago and have gradually deteriorated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking forward uphill is tolerated better than walking backward or on flat surfaces. Prolonged standing without support causes severe pain and cramping in both legs. Sitting down or leaning forward significantly relieves symptoms within minutes. The patient can walk further when bent forward, using a shopping cart for support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had a 20-year history of intermittent lower back pain managed conservatively. No history of prior spinal surgery. Recent fall from a ladder with direct blow to the lumbar region occurred 18 months ago, coinciding with symptom onset. He denies bowel or bladder changes. Family history of osteoporosis is noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Forward spinal flexion reproduces pain, but lumbar extension is limited and painful. Straight leg raise testing is negative. Neurological examination reveals normal strength and reflexes bilaterally. Palpation over the spinous process at L4-L5 elicits tenderness. Standing extension loading test increases symptoms.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-rays show anterior slipping of L4 on L5 measuring approximately 15-20% of the vertebral body width, with associated degenerative changes. MRI confirms anterior spondylolisthesis at L4-L5 with canal narrowing and bilateral foraminal stenosis. No instability on flexion-extension radiographs. Disc signal is mildly decreased at the slipped level.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis is the classic case with clear mechanical symptoms and definitive imaging showing vertebral slip.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with knee injury presents with pain on the lateral knee. The injury occurred with varus stress or inversion mechanism.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation is present with difficulty with walking and activities causing knee pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute injury mechanism documented. The patient may have prior ankle or knee injury history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lateral knee tenderness is present. Varus stress test demonstrates excessive varus motion indicating ligamentous injury.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates lateral collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.

puzzles.explanation: LCL injuries are less common than MCL but can occur with inversion and varus mechanisms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman reports progressive pain and swelling in her hand joints, particularly affecting the distal interphalangeal joints, with morning stiffness lasting 20 minutes. Pain is worse in the dominant hand and has progressed over several years.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Fine motor activities such as writing, sewing, and opening jars are significantly limited by hand pain and weakness. Carrying objects with a firm grip worsens symptoms. Hand elevation and ice provide temporary relief; prolonged use causes pain flare-ups.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has had repetitive hand use occupationally (seamstress) for 40 years and experienced a distal interphalangeal joint injury at age 35. She has no systemic rheumatologic symptoms, fevers, or rashes. Family history significant for osteoarthritis in the paternal line.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable osteophytes (Heberden nodes) at the distal interphalangeal joints of fingers 2-5 bilaterally. Hand grip strength reduced to 18 kg on right (age-expected 25-30 kg). Finger ROM limited at distal joints, with crepitus appreciated. No synovitis or joint warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show joint space narrowing primarily at distal interphalangeal joints with prominent marginal osteophytes and subchondral bone sclerosis. No evidence of erosions, periostitis, or soft tissue calcifications. Metacarpophalangeal joints spared.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoarthritis is localized to one or two joints with activity-related pain pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with high-energy ankle trauma presents with acute ankle pain and swelling. The injury is often from a fall from height or motor vehicle accident.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to weightbear. The patient demonstrates ankle deformity and significant swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy trauma is documented with direct axial load to the ankle. Associated injuries may be present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, ankle deformity is evident with severe swelling. The ankle is extremely painful with any movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate pilon fracture with comminution of the tibial plafond. CT imaging characterises the fracture pattern and is essential for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pilon fractures are devastating injuries with high complication rates requiring urgent surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old woman presents with acute-onset paralysis of the right leg that began suddenly during an emotionally stressful period at work 4 weeks ago. She is unable to walk independently and uses a wheelchair for mobility. She reports no sensory symptoms below the waist.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She demonstrates inability to bear weight on the right lower extremity, yet exhibits inconsistent functional use of the limb during natural activities and when unaware of observation. Her gait pattern when attempting to walk does not fit typical neurological disease patterns. She shows dramatic relief of symptoms during emotionally supportive interactions but sudden worsening during stressful encounters.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She experienced significant workplace conflict and interpersonal stress coinciding with symptom onset. She has a history of anxiety symptoms and perfectionist personality traits. She has seen multiple neurologists without definitive diagnosis of organic neurological disease. Prior psychiatric history includes adjustment disorder 3 years ago treated with psychotherapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, she demonstrates give-way weakness of right hip and knee with sudden collapse of effort when testing lower extremity strength. Hoover sign is positive indicating functional deficit rather than true paralysis. Gait when mobilizing without attention shows relatively preserved motor function compared to formal strength testing. Sensory examination is completely normal throughout. Reflexes are symmetric and normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spine MRI including cervical, thoracic, and lumbar regions is entirely normal without spinal cord lesions, compression, or structural abnormality. Brain MRI is normal with no white matter abnormalities, acute stroke, or demyelinating lesions. Routine laboratory studies including CBC and comprehensive metabolic panel are normal. Comprehensive neurophysiological testing is entirely normal without evidence of neuromuscular disease.

puzzles.explanation: Functional neurological disorder with non-organic motor signs, inconsistent findings on examination, and clear psychosocial precipitant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with running activity presents with progressive metatarsal pain. Pain develops in the ball of the foot.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running and impact activities. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with weightbearing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training volume or intensity coincides with symptom onset. Female athletes with amenorrhea are at increased risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal tenderness is present over the affected metatarsal. Pain is reproduced with single-leg hopping.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show callus formation at chronic stage. MRI demonstrates bone stress with periosteal reaction and bone marrow edema.

puzzles.explanation: Metatarsal stress fracture shows classic stress fracture from repetitive impact in runners or marchers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old female reports jaw pain localized to the joint in front of the ear that has progressively worsened over 4 months. She describes clicking or popping sounds with opening the mouth and occasional jaw locking that interferes with eating. Pain is dull and achy, worsening toward the end of the day.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking exacerbate pain and clicking significantly. The patient reports difficulty eating harder foods and now avoids them. Yawning reproduces the locking sensation. Resting the jaw and applying ice provide moderate relief. She clenches her teeth during stressful situations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a high-stress occupation with recent significant life changes. She works in a fast-paced environment and admits to daytime jaw clenching and nighttime teeth grinding. Dental history includes recent orthodontic treatment completed 2 years ago. No history of jaw trauma or prior TMJ disorders.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation over the temporomandibular joint bilaterally reveals tenderness on the affected side. Mouth opening measures 38 mm with deviation toward the right side. Clicking is audible during opening and closing of the mouth. Palpation of the masseter and temporalis muscles reveals significant myofascial trigger points with referred pain pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows displacement of the articular disc anteriorly with possible reduction during opening movements, indicating disc displacement with reduction. Mild joint space narrowing is present. Signal intensity changes in the joint fluid suggest mild inflammatory changes. No evidence of osteoarthritis or significant degenerative changes on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents as chronic degenerative TMJ osteoarthritis with limited conservative response and structural joint changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with fall on outstretched hand (FOOSH) with wrist flexion presents with acute wrist pain and visible deformity. The deformity is volar (palmar).

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Severe functional limitation is present with inability to use the hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall on outstretched hand mechanism with wrist in flexion documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, volar wrist deformity is visible. Significant swelling and tenderness are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate distal radius fracture with volar (palmar) angulation and displacement characteristic of Smith's fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Smith's fracture with volar displacement from fall on flexed wrist.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A recreational runner presents with pain and stiffness at the back of the lower leg. Pain is localised to the Achilles region and develops with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically triggered by running and activities involving plantarflexion. The patient experiences morning stiffness that improves with activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. The patient may have prior history of Achilles pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable along the Achilles tendon. Crepitus may be appreciated with tendon movement. Resisted plantarflexion reproduces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates Achilles tendon thickening, hypoechoic changes, or partial tear. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation confirming tendinopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Achilles tendinopathy develops from cumulative microtrauma and is common in runners and athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old former tennis player presents with progressively worsening shoulder pain for 8 months, particularly when lifting the arm overhead. Pain is located over the top of the shoulder and radiates down the lateral arm. Sudden acute flare-ups occur with minor overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Overhead reaching activities, particularly reaching behind the head, provoke sharp pain and weakness. Carrying groceries with the affected arm is no longer possible due to pain and weakness. Lying on the affected shoulder is uncomfortable. Rest and ice provide temporary relief, but symptoms return quickly with activity resumption.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient played competitive tennis for 30 years, retiring 10 years ago. She reports a fall on the outstretched hand 8 months ago that preceded current symptoms. Past medical history includes hypertension controlled with medication and mild osteoarthritis in other joints. She denies previous shoulder surgery or dislocations.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Abduction of the arm reproduces sharp pain, particularly between 60-120 degrees. Supraspinatus strength testing shows weakness with painful arc. Positive Hawkins test and Neer's impingement sign are elicited. Infraspinatus strength is 5/5. Palpation reveals tenderness over the supraspinatus insertion on the humeral head.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain film X-ray of the shoulder demonstrates a focal rounded density over the supraspinatus tendon insertion area. CT imaging shows a calcification measuring 12 mm with a heterogeneous appearance located within the supraspinatus tendon. The calcification is located 8 mm lateral to the humeral head articular surface margin.

puzzles.explanation: Acute calcification of supraspinatus with severe inflammatory response.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male presents with acute onset inability to move his affected leg after undergoing hip replacement surgery. He cannot walk and cannot lift his leg off the bed. The anterior thigh is noticeably swollen and tender.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform active hip flexion or knee extension. Sensory loss noted over the anterior thigh and medial lower leg in dermatomal pattern. Non-weight bearing and requires assistance with all mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred intraoperatively during total hip arthroplasty when the femoral nerve was stretched or contused during retraction for surgical access. Post-operative imaging revealed significant hematoma in the psoas and iliacus region. No prior neurological deficits. The patient underwent urgent hematoma evacuation 24 hours after surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength 0/5 with no visible iliopsoas contraction. Knee extension absent. Patellar reflex absent on affected side. Sensory loss to light touch in L2-L4 distribution. Complete paralysis of quadriceps and hip adductors.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip and proximal femur shows large hematoma in the iliopsoas and psoas major extending along the femoral nerve course. Nerve signal is interrupted at the level of the hematoma. Post-hematoma evacuation imaging shows partial reduction in swelling but persistent nerve compression. Nerve edema is visible extending distally.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical femoral nerve injury with acute quadriceps paralysis, loss of knee extension, and imaging evidence of nerve compression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old retired educator experiences chronic heel and arch discomfort extending across the sole of the foot. Morning pain is severe, necessitating 15-20 minutes of slow movement before tolerance improves. This chronic presentation affecting weight-bearing structures requires differentiation from multiple structural and systemic conditions.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: New or increased walking beyond 2 kilometers triggers escalation of pain for the following 24-48 hours. Elevated heels provide some relief. Running is not tolerated; even brisk walking produces discomfort within 10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 18 months ago following increased daily walking activity post-retirement. Patient has type 2 diabetes controlled by metformin with BMI 29. Previously experienced plantar fasciitis 10 years ago that resolved with conservative treatment within 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, pain is reproduced with palpation along the medial longitudinal arch. Windlass test produces marked pain with dorsiflexion of the hallux. Ankle plantarflexion range is normal; however, dorsiflexion is limited to 8 degrees bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals chronic thickening of the plantar fascia measuring 5.1 mm at the insertion point with heterogeneous signal intensity. Small calcaneal heel spur is visible. Marrow signal intensity at the calcaneal tuberosity is mildly altered, consistent with chronic inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Plantar fasciitis athlete shows chronic refractory heel pain with calcification and significant functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old diabetic woman reports gradual progressive weakness in her right leg over the past 3 months without preceding injury. She describes the leg feeling heavy and numb.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She reports severe difficulty with stairs and standing from a chair. Her walking tolerance has dropped significantly. She notes night-time thigh pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 15-year history of poorly controlled Type 2 diabetes with recent HbA1c of 9.2%. She reports unintentional weight loss of 12 pounds over the past 3 months. No history of leg trauma or prior neurological disorders. Recent imaging ruled out lumbar pathology. Vascular history is significant for peripheral vascular disease.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength is 2/5 with minimal antigravity power. Knee extension is similarly weak. Patellar reflex is diminished on the affected side. Sensory loss follows L2-L4 distribution.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the lumbar plexus and proximal femur shows signal abnormality within the psoas and iliacus muscles consistent with denervation edema. The femoral nerve appears enlarged proximally with T2 hyperintense signal consistent with inflammation. No focal mass or compressive lesion. Findings are consistent with diabetic femoral neuropathy.

puzzles.explanation: Diabetic lumbosacral radiculoplexopathy affecting femoral nerve with proximal leg weakness and motor loss in femoral distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An adolescent athlete presents with pain at the tibial tuberosity below the kneecap. Pain develops during or after physical activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: An adolescent athlete reports anterior knee pain with activity. Pain is localised below the kneecap and worsens with running and jumping.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain is reproduced with resisted knee extension and explosive movements. Functional impact includes difficulty with stairs, squatting, and returning to full sport participation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, swelling and tenderness are present at the tibial tuberosity. Pain is reproduced with knee extension against resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs may show tibial tuberosity enlargement and fragmentation. MRI demonstrates tibial tuberosity inflammation and soft tissue involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Residual OSD effects may persist after apophyseal closure due to bony irregularities and scar tissue.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports persistent fatigue and shortness of breath with minimal exertion. Symptoms began after a prolonged period off work due to acute illness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping for breath and becomes exhausted after light household tasks. Walking >100m triggers symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior illness, hospitalization, or prolonged immobility is documented. The patient has been inactive for weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Heart rate and blood pressure rise disproportionately with minimal activity. Resting heart rate elevated at 95 bpm. Six-minute walk test shows marked reduction in distance with dyspnoea at low workload.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cardiopulmonary exercise testing shows VO2 max 40% below age-predicted values. Echocardiography and pulmonary function tests are normal; no structural cardiac or lung disease detected.

puzzles.explanation: Post-illness deconditioning involves reduced exercise tolerance and function after immobilization, reversible with rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient with history of allergies or asthma presents with recurrent episodes of wheezing, dyspnoea, and chest tightness. Symptoms are variable.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports recurrent episodes of breathlessness, chest tightness, and audible breathing sounds. Symptoms are intermittent and variable in frequency and severity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to sustain aerobic activities without symptom onset. Symptoms typically resolve with rest or cessation of activity, though some persist for hours post-exertion.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symptoms worse during high pollen seasons and after exposure to cold air or specific allergens. Nocturnal coughing occasionally reported. No fever or recent infection preceding onset.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry demonstrates obstructive pattern with bronchial hyperresponsiveness. Response to bronchodilators documents reversibility.

puzzles.explanation: Classic pediatric asthma with allergen triggers, family history, and reversible airway obstruction confirmed by spirometry. Typical presentation responsive to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete presents with pain in the groin region or lower abdominal region. Pain develops with athletic activity and resolves with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is triggered by running, kicking, or twisting movements. The patient demonstrates functional limitation with sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in training intensity or volume coincides with symptom onset. Sport involving rapid acceleration and deceleration is typical.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resisted hip adduction reproduces pain. Palpation over adductor insertion at pubis is tender. Pain does NOT reproduce with hip flexion or knee extension against resistance, and pubic palpation does not elicit tenderness further laterally toward the ASIS.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates adductor muscle strain or rectus femoris tear near the pubic symphysis. MRI characterises muscle injury and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Athletic pubalgia requires high index of suspicion and may require surgical evaluation after failed conservative care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 25-year-old patient presents with recurrent shoulder pain and a sense of the shoulder 'slipping' or feeling unstable. Symptoms are triggered by overhead activities and throwing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead press or throwing activities without fear of shoulder giving way. Symptoms occur in multiple arm positions, not just one.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient often has generalized ligamentous laxity. Symptoms develop with activity or may occur spontaneously.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension sign positive in multiple planes. Sulcus sign present (inferior instability). Relocation test positive. Laxity testing reveals increased humeral head translation in anteroinferior and posteroinferior directions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates anteroinferior and posteroinferior labral tears with enlarged capsule. Humeral head subluxation evident in multiple positions on dynamic sequences.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability secondary to connective tissue disorder with severe manifestations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 56-year-old recreational jogger presents with progressive pain and swelling on the medial ankle that began insidiously 8 months ago without preceding acute injury. The foot is progressively losing its arch and turning outward. Walking on uneven surfaces has become increasingly difficult, and the patient describes a sensation of instability.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Running is no longer tolerated; the patient can only walk short distances before ankle pain and swelling worsen. Walking on uneven terrain aggravates symptoms more than flat surfaces. Prolonged standing increases pain and swelling significantly. The patient reports the foot 'giving way' occasionally with walking. Rest, ice, and elevation provide only temporary relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 20-year history of sporadic ankle sprains, with no specific traumatic event preceding current symptoms. He runs 4 times per week on concrete surfaces. No surgical history. Past medical history includes hypertension and hyperlipidemia. He reports progressive loss of endurance over the past year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant foot pronation is observed during gait, with increased medial longitudinal arch collapse. A positive single-leg stance test shows loss of balance on the affected foot. Resisted inversion strength is 4/5 on the affected side. Palpation reveals swelling and tenderness along the posterior tibial tendon insertion. Navicular drop test measures 15 mm compared to 5 mm on the unaffected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the ankle shows thickening and partial-thickness tear of the posterior tibial tendon at the medial malleolar region, measuring 8 mm in width. Surrounding soft tissue edema is present. The spring ligament shows partial disruption. Hindfoot alignment demonstrates 8-degree valgus deformity with talonavicular uncoverage on coronal imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced Stage 2 PTTD often requires surgical consideration for symptomatic management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive shortness of breath with recurrent respiratory infections since childhood. Chronic productive cough is present.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk distances without stopping for breath and has difficulty climbing stairs. Daily activities are increasingly limited by fatigue.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-standing pancreatic insufficiency with fat malabsorption. Family history of early-onset lung disease. Recent increase in sputum volume and colour change.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, decreased oxygen saturation with exertion. Fine crackles may be heard. Finger clubbing is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT demonstrates bronchiectasis and bronchial wall thickening. Pulmonary function tests show restrictive and obstructive patterns.

puzzles.explanation: Classic early-onset CF with systemic manifestations including malabsorption, bronchiectasis, and multiorgan involvement. Definitive diagnosis via sweat chloride test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Child with progressive difficulty keeping up with peers during play and self-care tasks. Parents report stiffness that seems worse after rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently or maintain sitting without support. Gait is asymmetrical with one leg stiffer than the other.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Difficult labour with low Apgar scores. Developmental milestones delayed from infancy. Symptoms have remained stable over the past 3 years with no deterioration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Modified Ashworth Scale 2+ in bilateral hip adductors and knee extensors. Clonus present at ankles bilaterally. Babinski sign positive. Weakness in antigravity positions.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain shows periventricular white matter loss with ventriculomegaly, consistent with hypoxic-ischaemic injury in the perinatal period.

puzzles.explanation: Ataxic CP features cerebellar dysfunction leading to poor balance, coordination, and fine motor control.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old competitive soccer player reports acute onset anterior hip pain that started 10 days ago during a match. Pain is sharp and localized to the anterior hip region. The patient felt something give while sprinting and was unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hip flexion against resistance reproduces sharp pain at the injury site. Activities requiring hip flexion with extension of the knee, such as kicking or sprinting, are particularly painful. The patient walks with a stiff gait and leans backward to minimize hip flexion. Ice, rest, and anti-inflammatory medications provide moderate relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Two-week period of increased training intensity in preparation for playoffs. Adequate warm-up performed before the injury match. No prior hip injuries, surgery, or inflammatory history. Competes at high frequency (4-6 times weekly).

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion strength reduced to 4/5 with pain on resistance. Palpation tender just below the inguinal ligament. Visible bruising in anterior hip region. Hip extension range of motion appears slightly limited on the affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the hip demonstrates acute partial-thickness tear of the iliopsoas muscle at the myotendinous junction approximately 2 cm distal to the inguinal ligament. Muscle edema and hemorrhage surround the tear site. No muscle retraction. Underlying hip joint and bony anatomy appear normal. Findings consistent with acute muscle strain grade 2.

puzzles.explanation: Hip flexor strains occur from explosive hip flexion movements, especially in sprinters and kickers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old with low back discomfort that developed gradually over 3 months. Pain is dull and aching, worse with certain activities and better with rest.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are reproducibly aggravated by prolonged sitting and forward bending, but improved with lying down and gentle movement. The patient has difficulty maintaining work-related postures but can walk without limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No clear precipitating injury. Patient reports occupational stress and has been sedentary for the past 18 months. This is the first episode of significant back pain.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Active lumbar flexion and extension achieved with mild discomfort; no restriction in ROM. Palpation reveals bilateral paraspinal muscle tightness, most prominent in lower lumbar segments. No neurological deficits detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine MRI is unremarkable: no structural pathology, no disc herniation, no facet hypertrophy, no evidence of spondylosis or stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain is occupational in nature with clear biomechanical and postural contributing factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute lateral trunk pain following an overhead throwing activity. The pain is sharp and well-localized to one side of the trunk, onset immediate during sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient experiences severe pain with trunk rotation toward the affected side and with lateral flexion away from the side. Activities requiring core stability such as throwing, twisting, and lunging reproduce the pain sharply. Walking and rest positions provide relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during competitive recreational baseball without prior warning. The patient had not warmed up adequately before the throwing activity. No previous history of similar injuries; overall good health with regular exercise routine.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The injury occurred during overhead throwing in cold, without preceding warm-up. No prior episodes of trunk strain or neurological history. Patient denies midline/spinal symptoms and reports no radiation of pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the lateral trunk musculature shows focal hypoechoic changes within the external oblique muscle consistent with acute muscle strain. No fluid collection or significant hemorrhage noted. The muscle fibers appear partially disrupted in longitudinal view.

puzzles.explanation: Oblique strain shows acute external or internal oblique muscle injury from rotational mechanism during sport.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man presents with deep, aching shoulder pain that developed insidiously 5 months ago. Pain is present at rest and notably worse at night, disrupting sleep. The discomfort is localized to the shoulder joint itself rather than radiating distally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports marked limitation in shoulder external rotation and overhead elevation, making it impossible to reach behind the back or reach high shelves. Internal rotation is relatively preserved. Throwing and overhead sporting activities are no longer possible. Pain is partially relieved with heat and gentle therapeutic exercise within available range.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Seven months prior to symptom onset, the patient suffered a myocardial infarction and was advised to minimize shoulder activity during cardiac recovery. Stiffness and pain gradually developed in the weeks following resumption of normal activity. He has type 2 diabetes mellitus diagnosed 8 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, shoulder abduction is limited to 85 degrees and external rotation is severely restricted to 15 degrees with hard end-feel. Forward flexion is limited to 120 degrees. Passive range of motion is similarly restricted in all planes. Rotator cuff strength testing shows weakness attributable to pain inhibition rather than true muscle loss. Scapular mechanics are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates capsular thickening and synovial inflammation with reduced joint space volume. Coronal and axial sequences show circumferential capsular fibrosis particularly affecting the anterior and posterior capsule. Rotator cuff muscles appear normal without fatty infiltration. No full-thickness tears are present. Findings are consistent with chronic adhesive capsulitis.

puzzles.explanation: Atypical presentation: young male with post-surgical secondary adhesive capsulitis. Unlike typical primary frozen shoulder, this developed after arthroscopy, showing that immobilisation and psychological factors can trigger the condition even without the usual risk factors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old office worker presents with 8 weeks of chronic shoulder pain. Pain is described as a deep ache with occasional sharp catching sensations. Overhead activities aggravate symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate sustained arm elevation or reaching overhead. Activities requiring repetitive mouse use and awkward shoulder posturing at work significantly worsen pain. Symptoms resolve completely when arm is at rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain developed insidiously over 8 weeks without specific injury. The patient reports increased shoulder demands from recent work changes requiring frequent computer mouse use and awkward posturing. History includes poor shoulder muscle conditioning and rounded shoulder posture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, Neer impingement test and Hawkins test are both positive, reproducing the patient's pain. Supraspinatus strength is 4/5 with painful weakness noted. Posterior shoulder tightness is evident with cross-body adduction stretch. Scapular dyskinesis is observed during active arm elevation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder MRI demonstrates thickening of the supraspinatus tendon with mild signal hyperintensity consistent with early tendinopathy. Subacromial space is narrowed to 7mm. Acromial morphology shows a Type II hooked acromion contributing to space compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Subacromial pain syndrome in a swimmer with training overload, characteristic painful arc, positive impingement signs, and scapular dysfunction contributing to the impingement mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A middle-aged to older adult presents with unilateral neck pain that is mechanical in nature. Pain is localised to one side of the posterior neck region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by neck extension and ipsilateral rotation. The patient demonstrates asymmetric movement restriction. Symptoms are activity-dependent and relieved by rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of motor vehicle accident or whiplash injury 2–5 years prior, with intermittent recurrent episodes of unilateral posterior neck pain. No radicular symptoms or neurological complaints reported. Pain pattern is episodic; flare-ups triggered by sustained postures or repetitive rotation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Neck extension combined with ipsilateral side-bending and rotation reproduces pain at the affected level. Unilateral posterior neck tenderness localised to facet region (just lateral to midline). Cervical rotation ROM restricted ipsilaterally by 20–30%. Upper extremity strength, reflexes, and sensation intact; no dermatomal deficit or lower limb signs. Negative neural tension tests.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates facet joint hypertrophy and joint space narrowing at the affected level. MRI may show degenerative changes including osteophytes and capsular inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical facet joint sprain is chronic with recurrent exacerbations from residual instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with a history of hypertension presents with progressive shortness of breath and fatigue over the past 3 months. Symptoms worsen with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot climb stairs without stopping. Cannot walk more than 50 metres on flat ground before becoming severely breathless. Nocturnal symptoms affecting sleep.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of cardiac disease including prior MI, hypertension, or cardiomyopathy is present. Progressive functional decline is documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, signs of heart failure including peripheral oedema, elevated JVP, or orthopnoea are evident. Vital sign abnormalities may be present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Echocardiography demonstrates reduced ejection fraction or diastolic dysfunction. Imaging characterises cardiac systolic and diastolic function.

puzzles.explanation: Classic systolic heart failure with fluid overload. Reduced ejection fraction on echocardiography, signs of congestion, and elevated natriuretic peptide confirm diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An older adult presents with acute severe hip pain following a fall. Unable to move the affected leg.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient held leg rigidly in a fixed posture and resists any movement of the hip. Severe pain with any attempt at mobility.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of osteoporosis. Fall occurred from standing height. No prior hip pain or dysfunction.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the lower extremity is held in external rotation and abduction. Significant swelling is evident over the hip.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show a fracture line between the greater and lesser trochanters, extending from the lateral femoral cortex medially across the femoral neck base.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric fractures often require surgical fixation with plates or intramedullary nailing due to fracture pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old footballer experienced sudden onset knee pain during a match. Describes a 'popping' sensation and immediate swelling on the inner knee after a twisting tackle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot weight-bear fully on the affected leg. Pain is worse with pivoting movements and side-stepping, but forward walking is tolerated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during contact sport when another player's shoulder drove into the patient's lateral knee while the foot was planted. No prior knee surgery, but patient reports previous ankle sprains.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Grade 2 medial joint line tenderness. Anterior drawer test is negative. Lachman test is negative. Mild effusion present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Magnetic resonance imaging demonstrates medial collateral ligament tear with characterisation of tear severity and associated injuries.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic MCL insufficiency from repeated injuries results in functional instability and laxity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retiree presents with progressive mechanical low back pain that has developed gradually over several years. The pain is typically localized to the low lumbar region and is worsened in the morning and by end-of-day fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms are exacerbated by sustained postures, particularly prolonged sitting and standing, with the patient reporting need for frequent position changes. Walking and gentle movement provide temporary relief. Bending activities are limited due to pain rather than mechanical restriction.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient reports a 40-year history of occupational heavy lifting as a construction worker. Previous episodes of acute low back pain have occurred over the past 15 years with increasing frequency. Multiple risk factors include smoking history, obesity, and sedentary lifestyle post-retirement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, lumbar flexion is limited to 40 degrees due to pain, with loss of normal lumbar lordosis. Palpation reveals paraspinal muscle spasm. Facet joint loading tests elicit localized pain. Straight leg raise is normal to 75 degrees bilaterally. Lower extremity strength remains 5/5 throughout.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine MRI shows multiple levels of disc degeneration with decreased disc height, particularly at L4-L5 and L5-S1. Signal loss within discs is noted on T2-weighted images. Osteophytes are present at degenerated segments without significant central canal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative disc disease is age-related degeneration with chronic mechanical symptoms correlating with imaging changes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient presents with acute onset of neck pain and upper back discomfort following a motor vehicle accident. Onset was immediate.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain radiating toward the shoulder and upper limb. Symptoms worsen with sustained postures and improve with rest and support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago. Patient reports no loss of consciousness. No prior neck problems. Symptoms have plateaued; initial severe pain has subsided partially but stiffness persists.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical flexion limited to 20°; rotation and lateral flexion also restricted symmetrically. Palpable muscle spasm in upper trapezius and paraspinal muscles. Cervical myelopathy signs absent. Upper limb strength and reflexes intact bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain cervical radiographs show loss of normal lordosis and no acute fracture or instability. MRI demonstrates mild muscle edema in paraspinal muscles without disc herniation, cord compression, or canal stenosis.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash associated disorder is severe with high-impact mechanism and complicating neurological involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old male presents with acute-onset severe pain in the right foot that woke him overnight. The affected joint is swollen, red, and warm. Pain is throbbing and intense, significantly limiting mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and is confined to bed rest. Even light pressure or passive movement of the foot causes severe pain. No other joints are affected.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a 10-year history of hyperuricemia and had been non-compliant with urate-lowering therapy. Recent dietary indulgences included a heavy seafood dinner and alcohol consumption. He reports prior similar episodes that resolved over 1-2 weeks without treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint examination shows marked swelling with severe restriction of dorsiflexion. Passive motion triggers acute pain. Exquisite tenderness at the first MTP joint line. No other joints show signs of inflammation. Core temperature is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the first MTP joint demonstrates hypoechoic joint effusion with synovial thickening and mild hyperemia on Doppler imaging. X-ray imaging shows no acute fracture or significant degenerative changes. Serum uric acid level is elevated at 8.2 mg/dL.

puzzles.explanation: Gout shows chronic tophaceous gout with polyarticular involvement in an older patient with medical comorbidities (heart failure, renal disease) and diuretic use.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old woman presents with progressive pain and swelling in her forefoot, particularly affecting the great toe joint region. She first noticed a bony prominence on the medial side of her foot in her early 30s, which has steadily worsened over the past decade.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is aggravated by walking in fitted shoes and high heels, with symptoms improving when shoes are removed. The patient has difficulty fitting into standard shoe sizes and has compensated by wearing increasingly wide shoes. Running and prolonged standing significantly worsen symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strong maternal family history of progressive foot deformities. Patient wore high heels occupationally for 15+ years. No preceding trauma or acute injury. Progressive worsening despite activity modification attempts.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weight-bearing assessment reveals lateral deviation of the great toe with a prominent bony bump on the medial forefoot. The first metatarsophalangeal joint shows tenderness to palpation, mild effusion, and restricted extension range. Intermetatarsal spacing appears widened on visual inspection.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing anteroposterior foot radiographs demonstrate lateral deviation of the hallux with intermetatarsal angle of 12 degrees and hallux valgus angle of 28 degrees. The medial eminence is prominent. Early degenerative changes are noted at the first MTP joint.

puzzles.explanation: Symptomatic hallux valgus may require conservative management or surgical correction when significantly limiting function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An infant presents with neck asymmetry and limited neck motion. The head demonstrates positional preference.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant unable to turn head fully to one side and prefers to look over the same shoulder. Difficulty tracking objects across midline.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The condition is congenital, often with history of difficult delivery or positioning. Early intervention is important for outcomes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, unilateral sternocleidomastoid tightness and restriction of neck rotation are demonstrated. A mass may be palpable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates focal thickening and fibrosis of the sternocleidomastoid muscle, confirming muscle-based pathology rather than vertebral or neurological cause.

puzzles.explanation: Untreated congenital torticollis leads to secondary plagiocephaly and facial asymmetry requiring more intensive intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with anterior knee pain triggered by jumping and running. Pain is activity-dependent and reproducible.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specifically localised to the patellar region and worsens with resisted knee extension movements. Aggravated by eccentric loading during landing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptom onset correlates with increased training volume or intensity. Recent changes in training are often contributory.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is palpable at the patellar tendon insertion. Pain is reproduced with resisted knee extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates patellar tendon thickening and hypoechoic changes. MRI shows intrasubstance signal changes and inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Patellar tendinopathy is common in explosive athletes due to repetitive high loading.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports progressive pain across multiple joints with episodes of instability and swelling. Symptoms have been lifelong with variable severity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to participate in contact sports or high-impact activities. Experiences recurrent sprains and dislocations, particularly at ankles, shoulders, and fingers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient notes mother and two siblings with similar lifelong joint problems. Early childhood marked by unusual flexibility; no discrete injury triggering onset. Prior physiotherapy and orthopaedic assessment without definitive diagnosis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Beighton score 7/9 (hyperextension of knees and elbows, thumb opposition, forward trunk flexion). Generalised ligamentous laxity with no focal instability pattern. Strength and neurological examination intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of shoulder: no structural labral tear or rotator cuff pathology despite recurrent instability episodes. Spine imaging normal. Genetic testing confirms haploinsufficiency affecting collagen architecture, consistent with benign joint hypermobility syndrome.

puzzles.explanation: Generalised joint hypermobility is the benign hypermobility syndrome with pain developing in young adulthood.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient presents with acute onset neck pain and stiffness following a motor vehicle collision. Injury occurred several hours ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to tolerate sustained postures. Pain worsens with combined movements (rotation with extension). Has not returned to driving or desk work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No loss of consciousness at time of injury. No significant head trauma or facial injury. Patient reports feeling 'whipped' backward then forward. Symptoms have persisted and worsened over 48 hours despite initial apparent minimal injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cervical ROM limited in all planes by pain and muscle guarding. Tenderness diffuse across upper trapezius, levator scapulae, and paraspinals. Headache elicited with neck extension. Upper limb reflexes normal; no dermatomal sensory loss. No signs of radiculopathy.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of cervical spine: normal alignment, no fracture or dislocation. MRI cervical spine: signal changes and edema in cervical musculature and ligaments consistent with soft tissue injury; no cord signal change or significant nerve root compression.

puzzles.explanation: Whiplash associated disorder is a chronic post-traumatic condition with ongoing symptoms and psychological overlay.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient recovering from recent surgery or traumatic injury develops severe pain and swelling in the affected area, with fever and feeling systemically unwell.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Rapid progression of local swelling prevents normal movement and use of affected limb. Systemic symptoms (fever, malaise, tachycardia) develop within hours to days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent surgery or penetrating trauma 24–72 hours prior. Patient reports pain and swelling progressing faster than expected for normal post-operative healing or wound infection.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, rapidly spreading erythema and swelling are evident with severe pain out of proportion. Skin changes including bullae may develop.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates extensive fascial inflammation and fluid tracking along tissue planes. Imaging helps define surgical extent.

puzzles.explanation: Post-necrotizing fasciitis rehabilitation requires intensive interdisciplinary care including wound management, plastic reconstruction coordination, deconditioning reversal, pain control, and significant psychological support for this life-altering condition.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with shoulder pain and swelling, worse with overhead activities. Multiple shoulder pathologies should be considered.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead reaching or repetitive arm activities without pain. Pain is worst during abduction movements; rests arm in protective position.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent increase in overhead work over past 2–3 weeks. History of chronic shoulder strain and possible rotator cuff involvement. Onset acute rather than insidious.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, focal swelling is evident over the deltoid region. Shoulder abduction reproduces pain. Neer's test is positive, and direct palpation elicits focal tenderness. Shoulder range of motion is restricted in abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows fluid collection within the subdeltoid bursal space. Bursa is distended; no rotator cuff tear visible. Findings consistent with bursal inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Subdeltoid bursitis with fluid distension and associated overhead activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 71-year-old woman on multiple antihypertensive medications experiences lightheadedness and near-syncope upon standing from bed in the morning. Frequent episodes of vertigo sensation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate prolonged standing; symptoms worsen as day progresses. Able to perform seated tasks without limitation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension controlled with amlodipine and lisinopril. Recently diagnosed diabetes on metformin. Previous episode of acute dehydration from gastroenteritis 1 week ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Blood pressure supine 148/90 versus standing 118/74. Postural drop of 30 mmHg systolic documented. Carotid pulses intact bilaterally. No orthostatic tachycardia noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Continuous cardiac monitoring during position change shows absence of reflex tachycardia despite systolic pressure decline. Suggests autonomic dysfunction or medication-induced orthostasis rather than hypovolemia.

puzzles.explanation: Autonomic postural hypotension from neuropathy involves impaired blood pressure compensation with position change.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old administrative professional working 8 hours daily at a computer workstation presents with bilateral forearm and wrist aching. Symptoms began insidiously 8 weeks ago with gradual onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged typing and mouse use, easing with rest breaks. Functional impact includes reduced work tolerance and awkward posture adoption to minimize discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No history of acute traumatic injury. Desk height reduction 8 weeks ago coincided with symptom onset. No prior similar episodes. Patient denies evening swelling or night symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Multiple tender points throughout flexor and extensor forearm musculature bilaterally. No positive Phalen or Tinel sign. Grip strength reduced but symmetrical. Forearm pronation/supination mildly limited.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG and nerve conduction studies normal bilaterally. Ultrasound imaging shows no tendon inflammation, nerve thickening, or structural abnormality. MRI of cervical spine unremarkable—no root compression or myelopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational overuse syndrome shows repetitive strain from occupational demand with forearm muscle involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old competitive distance runner reports posterolateral knee pain that began 3 weeks ago. Sharp, localized discomfort near the knee joint line on the outside.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Worsens significantly downhill running and high-speed sprinting. Subsides with walking and rest. Unable to complete training sessions. Pain on eccentric hamstring loading.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased weekly running distance by 40% four weeks prior to symptom onset. No history of acute hamstring tear. Had lateral knee pain from IT band syndrome two years ago, now resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over biceps femoris insertion at fibular head, exacerbated with knee flexion against resistance. Palpable tendon thickening at insertion site. Strength testing normal. Pain reproduced with resisted knee flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps femoris tendon thickening and hypoechogenicity at fibular insertion. MRI confirms tendon inflammation without full-thickness tear. Bursa involvement possible.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps femoris tendinopathy affects the short head insertion, common in sprinters and strength athletes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 64-year-old woman with a 15-year history of progressive foot deformity. Now presenting with pain in the forefoot, particularly over a bony prominence on one toe. Discomfort worsened by footwear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to wear narrow shoes or heels. Reports altered gait pattern and avoidance of prolonged walking. Pain prevents normal shoe selection.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong hallux valgus and crowding in foot structure contributed to hammer toe development. Inflammatory arthropathy absent. Daughters have similar foot structure. No acute trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fixed deformity at proximal interphalangeal joint (plantarflexion position non-reducible). Metatarsophalangeal joint shows flexion contracture with dorsal prominence. Prominent callus and corn formation overlying the bony prominence.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs confirm fixed plantarflexion deformity at the proximal interphalangeal joint with metatarsophalangeal dorsal subluxation. Early degenerative changes visible at the proximal interphalangeal joint articulation.

puzzles.explanation: Hammer toe is a structural deformity causing cosmetic and functional problems.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old ballet dancer reports anterior hip discomfort with groin tenderness onset 2 weeks ago. Pain worse with activities involving repeated hip flexion and turning movements. Some radiation into the upper anterior thigh.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate sustained hip flexion or repetitive flexion-based movements. Stair climbing and prolonged sitting provoke symptoms. Activities requiring hip extension after flexion are particularly uncomfortable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive ballet training 4 hours daily with recent choreography changes requiring greater hip flexibility demands. No prior hip pathology. Pain began 2 weeks after increasing flexibility training intensity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion ROM limited to 110 degrees (pain-limited). Thomas test positive suggesting hip flexor tightness. Palpation of anterior hip/groin elicits focal tenderness. Pain reproduced with combined hip extension and internal rotation (FABER modification).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound demonstrates bursa effusion anterior to hip joint. MRI confirms iliopsoas bursitis without labral pathology. Iliopsoas tendon visualized normally on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Iliopsoas bursitis presents as deep hip or groin pain from bursal inflammation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 60-year-old with several years of progressive neck pain and intermittent tingling in both arms. Pain worsens with certain neck movements. Recently noticed increasing clumsiness with fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient struggles with writing and buttoning clothes. Difficulty gripping objects with consistent force. Gait has become slightly unsteady over past 6 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Decades of desk work and poor posture. Previous imaging 10 years ago showed disc bulging. No major trauma. Symptoms stable until 18 months ago when progression accelerated. No previous neck surgery.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, neck range of motion is limited in multiple planes. Upper extremity strength testing reveals deficits consistent with myelopathy. Pathological reflexes (hyperreflexia, Babinski sign) may be elicited.

clues.label: Imaging
