clues.text: MRI demonstrates multiple level disc degeneration with osteophytes and spinal cord compression. The spinal canal is narrowed. Cord signal changes may indicate myelopathic compromise. Imaging confirms cervical spondylosis with myelopathy.

puzzles.explanation: Cervical spondylosis is age-related degenerative disease with local neck symptoms and minimal radicular features.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 3-month postpartum woman reports difficulty with functional activities and core stability. Notices her abdomen looks different and bulges when performing certain movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to return to pre-pregnancy exercise routines. Bulging worsens with carrying baby and stair climbing. Notably improves with external abdominal bracing/splinting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Normal pregnancy and vaginal delivery 3 months ago. Pre-pregnancy fitness level good with strong core baseline. Postpartum exercise initiated without clearance or modification for diastasis.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline separation palpable, approximately 2.5 fingers width at umbilicus. Tissue laxity evident. Abdominal contraction causes lateral bulging of rectus muscles. Fascia integrity intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging shows diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal separation 2.8 cm. Linea alba appears attenuated. No hernia. Dynamic assessment shows separation persists with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows chronic wide separation in a multiparous woman with multiple pregnancies and weight gain, with possible herniation and very poor core function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old healthcare worker reports recurrent episodes of chest tightness and breathlessness occurring several times weekly. Episodes are accompanied by anxiety and sense of panic.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During episodes, patient experiences dizziness, tingling in hands and around mouth, and a feeling of losing control. Symptoms rapidly resolve when patient slows breathing deliberately. Avoidance behavior is developing—patient limits public outings due to fear of episodes occurring.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known anxiety disorder for 3 years, previously managed with CBT alone. Recently started sertraline 6 weeks ago following increase in workplace demands and workload. No prior respiratory or cardiac diagnoses. Patient reports episodes are triggered reliably by stress or crowded environments.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: During controlled hyperventilation challenge, patient reproduces symptoms of paresthesia and lightheadedness. Arterial blood gas shows respiratory alkalosis (pH 7.48, low pCO2). Cardiac exam normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Capnography shows end-tidal CO2 below 30 mmHg during symptoms, confirming hyperventilation-induced hypocapnia. ECG normal excluding cardiac cause. Spirometry normal pulmonary function.

puzzles.explanation: Anxiety-induced hyperventilation with characteristic respiratory alkalosis confirmed by ABG. Psychogenic etiology evidenced by symptom reproduction with voluntary hyperventilation and normal organic workup.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old office worker with sedentary job presents with chronic lower back pain of 6 months duration. Mild to moderate pain (4/10 at baseline) with periodic exacerbations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged sitting, improves with movement and standing. Functional limitations include difficulty with prolonged work postures and household activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset without specific traumatic event. Repetitive strain from workstation setup. Mild obesity (BMI 28). Previous episode of low back pain 3 years ago, self-resolved.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar segmental mobility unremarkable. Hip flexor tightness bilaterally. Core strength 4/5. Straight leg raise negative. No neurological deficits or radicular signs. Palpation of lumbar paraspinals reveals mild muscular guarding without acute tenderness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show mild degenerative disc disease at L4-L5 and L5-S1. MRI demonstrates small disc bulging without neural compromise. No significant pathology explaining symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: Non-specific low back pain is the most common presentation: acute mechanical pain without serious structural disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 58-year-old man with hypertension and heavy smoking history. Sudden onset right-sided weakness and facial asymmetry while at work. Symptoms began 2 hours ago. Now in acute care.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot walk independently. Speech difficult to understand. Trouble understanding spoken words. Cognitive changes noted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hypertension poorly controlled at admission (BP 180/110). Smokes 20 cigarettes daily. No prior neurological events. Symptoms reached maximum severity within minutes of onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left-sided facial droop noted. Right pronator drift present. Increased reflexes (3+) in right arm and leg. Babinski sign elicited on right. Visual field defect on right side bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Acute CT brain shows hypodensity in left MCA territory involving motor cortex and corona radiata. Diffusion-weighted MRI confirms acute ischemic stroke. Angiography shows MCA occlusion.

puzzles.explanation: Right MCA stroke presenting with contralateral hemiparesis, UMN signs, and facial droop, secondary to atrial fibrillation with cardioembolic source.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with a painful ulcer on the lower leg that has been present for 4 weeks. She reports long-standing swelling and gradual skin discolouration in the same area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is significantly worse when the leg hangs down and improves with elevation. The ulcer produces moderate exudate and requires frequent dressing changes, limiting her walking tolerance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She reports a prior episode of deep vein thrombosis in the right leg 15 years ago. Her current lower leg edema has been progressive and unresponsive to compression therapy attempts over the past several years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Medial malleolar region ulcer with depth 0.5 cm, irregular borders, beefy red base. Surrounding skin with lipodermatosclerosis and hemosiderin deposition. Pitting edema bilateral legs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Duplex ultrasound shows superficial and deep venous insufficiency with valve incompetence in great saphenous vein. No acute DVT. Ankle-brachial index 0.95, confirming adequate arterial perfusion.

puzzles.explanation: Classic venous ulcer with post-thrombotic sequelae, characteristic location above medial malleolus, and surrounding venous insufficiency skin changes. Hemodynamically significant venous disease confirmed by ultrasound.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old patient presents with hip pain and difficulty with lower limb mobility that began 6 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Pain is exacerbated by certain movement directions. Patient is currently progressing through rehabilitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient demonstrates guarded movement in specific hip directions (notably combined flexion with adduction and internal rotation). Weight-bearing is limited; ambulation only achievable with walking aid. Stairs and transfers are significantly restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent unilateral hip surgery 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. No prior hip pathology or revision. Comorbidities include age-related bone quality concerns. No neurological history or contralateral symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip abductor strength is 3/5 throughout available range. Hip flexion to 80°, abduction to 25°, internal rotation severely limited. Trendelenburg sign present. No groin pain on palpation. No signs of swelling or warmth.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs confirm well-positioned prosthetic components with satisfactory bone ingrowth pattern. Cup and stem angles within normal parameters. Minimal periosteal reaction. No evidence of loosening, subsidence, or periprosthetic fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Hip Replacement shows early post-operative period (6 weeks) with expected precautions, limited motion, and gradual weight-bearing progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man with shoulder calcification presents with recurrent episodes of acute shoulder pain. Previous episode 2 years ago, now recurring. Sudden exacerbation of symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 58-year-old man with recurrent episodes of acute shoulder pain. Previous similar episode 2 years ago resolved spontaneously. Now experiencing sudden return of severe symptoms without obvious trigger.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead activities severely limited. Nighttime pain preventing sleep. Unable to perform shoulder abduction and forward elevation beyond 90 degrees. Functional losses similar to previous episode.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tender supraspinous region on palpation. Pain reproduction with painful arc maneuver. Guarding with shoulder motion. Scapular dyskinesis evident on movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Supraspinous tenderness on palpation. Painful arc reproduction between 60-120 degrees abduction. Scapular dyskinesis noted with active elevation. Strength testing normal. Neurovascular examination unremarkable.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic supraspinatus calcification with asymptomatic deposits causing chronic pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 19-year-old soccer player presents with lateral midfoot pain after an inversion ankle sprain 2 weeks ago. Pain localized to lateral foot, not at ankle joint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty walking and running. Pain with weight-bearing on lateral foot edge. Unable to participate in training and competition. Worsens with plantarflexion and inversion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inversion injury 2 weeks ago. Initial ankle swelling resolved within days, but isolated midfoot pain has persisted and worsened. No previous foot or ankle injuries. Patient is otherwise healthy with no relevant comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness over cuboid bone palpated from lateral foot. Pain with pronation and plantarflexion loading. Resisted plantarflexion and inversion reproduces pain. Positive cuboid squeeze test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Foot X-rays show normal ankle and calcaneous alignment, but cuboid appears slightly subluxed relative to navicular. Talonavicular joint alignment assessed. No fractures identified.

puzzles.explanation: Cuboid syndrome involves subtle misalignment of the cuboid bone causing persistent lateral midfoot pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 72-year-old woman with history of atrial fibrillation presents with sudden onset of speech difficulty and left-sided weakness. Symptoms began during morning routine. Immediate hospital admission.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to maintain balance or walk independently. Requires assistance with all mobility tasks and grooming. Left-sided limbs felt 'heavy' and uncoordinated.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known atrial fibrillation for 8 years, not on anticoagulation due to patient non-adherence. Hypertension managed. Previous TIA with full recovery 2 years ago. No recent head trauma or seizures.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left facial droop with asymmetric smile. Left upper extremity weakness 2/5, lower extremity 3/5. Pronator drift present. Hyperreflexia on left side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI brain with diffusion-weighted imaging shows acute infarction in left middle cerebral artery territory, spanning cortical and subcortical regions.

puzzles.explanation: Acute left MCA stroke with large territory involvement causing contralateral motor deficit, speech disturbance, and visual field loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman, 4 weeks post-acute cardiac event, referred for supervised exercise program. Reports dyspnea, chest discomfort with exertion, fatigue, and anxiety about physical activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Exercise tolerance 2 METs at baseline. Dyspnea and chest discomfort with greater exertion. Anxiety about cardiac status limiting activity participation. Fatigue prominent.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Inferior STEMI 4 weeks ago treated with primary percutaneous intervention and stent placement. Currently on guideline-directed medical therapy. Hypertension and hyperlipidemia managed. Quit smoking since event.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 78 bpm. Blood pressure normal at current status. No arrhythmias on telemetry monitoring. Cardiac murmurs absent. Peripheral edema absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Stress echocardiography demonstrates wall motion improvement in previously infarcted territory compared to acute phase. No new wall motion abnormalities. No ischemia provoked at achieved workload. LV ejection fraction unchanged at 40%.

puzzles.explanation: Elderly post-MI rehab patient with multiple comorbidities and reduced exercise capacity. Requires modified rehabilitation approach addressing orthostatic symptoms and arthritic limitations.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old typist reports catching sensation in right index finger with frequent triggering during work. Clicking sensation during finger flexion and extension. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform sustained gripping and typing activities. Morning stiffness in the right index finger that resolves within 30 minutes with activity. Most limited in data entry work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Occupational repetitive strain from data entry work 8 hours daily. Gradual progressive onset. No trauma. Previous history of mild wrist pain from RSI.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodule palpable at MCP level of index finger. A1 pulley tenderness on palpation. Triggering demonstrated with active finger motion. Mild swelling over pulley.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows flexor tendon nodule and pulley thickening at A1 level index finger. Mild effusion around tendon. No partial tear. Dynamic ultrasound confirms tendon nodule catching.

puzzles.explanation: Multi-digit trigger finger in diabetic patient, more severe and difficult to treat than typical single-finger presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old cyclist with chronic lateral ankle pain and lower leg aching. Pain present over 4 weeks, worsening with lateral foot-loading activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with prolonged cycling and hill training; also reports giving way sensation on uneven ground or lateral stepping movements. Significantly impacting training frequency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous inversion ankle sprain 18 months prior that resulted in chronic lateral ankle instability. Cycling training frequency increased 2 months ago. Weak ankle stabilizers noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Peroneal tendon palpable thickening and tenderness behind lateral malleolus. Pain reproduction with resisted foot eversion in plantarflexion. Normal anterior drawer test and talar tilt; ankle ROM preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows peroneal tendon thickening with increased echo texture heterogeneity. Mild subcutaneous fluid. No tendon subluxation. MRI demonstrates tendinopathy without tear.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic peroneal tendinopathy often results from chronic ankle instability and compensatory overuse.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old mechanic reports sudden onset of weakness in the forearm and hand 3 days ago. Notices difficulty with fine motor tasks and pinching activities. No pain, numbness, or trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform precision gripping tasks required at work (holding small tools, fasteners). Weakness predominantly affecting thumb and index finger — other hand strength appears normal.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute onset of weakness with overhead reaching activity at work 3 days ago. No trauma but possible nerve compression from muscle hypertrophy or pronator teres tightness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Thumb IP flexion weak/absent. Index finger DIP flexion weak/absent. Grip and wrist flexion strength intact. Sensation normal in median nerve distribution including first web space. Pronation strength normal bilaterally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowed motor conduction in anterior interosseous branch of median nerve. EMG demonstrates acute denervation changes in flexor pollicis longus and flexor digitorum profundus.

puzzles.explanation: Anterior interosseous syndrome with motor-only deficits and preserved sensation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents 3 weeks after a fall from standing height. She reports significant anxiety, hypervigilance, and reluctance to move. Physical examination reveals only minor bruising.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient refuses to walk unassisted and has become housebound. She reports waking at night with panic and catastrophic thoughts about another fall, despite no residual pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred while reaching for an object on a shelf. Prior to fall, patient was fully independent, lived alone, and walked regularly. Now refuses to leave home; family visits have become infrequent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait cautious and slower, avoids turning. Functional reach test limited due to fear rather than physical limitation. No demonstrable neurological deficit. Cognitive assessment normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Functional mobility assessment (Timed Up and Go) shows marked slowing primarily during turning phase. DEXA scan normal for age. Further psychological screening (GAD-7, PCL-5) shows elevated anxiety and intrusive thoughts specific to falling.

puzzles.explanation: Post-fall anxiety syndrome causes activity avoidance and deconditioning, increasing actual fall risk and disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old woman develops acute onset severe neck pain after turning to reach something while seated. Neck rigidity and inability to move head. Pain sudden and severe.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lift head from pillow without assistance. Arm and hand functional (no neurological deficit). Pain radiates across shoulders. Significant functional impairment.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden neck movement during reaching; no preceding trauma or chronic neck complaints. Symptoms began immediately without gradual progression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant cervical muscle spasm and rigidity palpable. Neck rotation severely limited in both directions. Cervical flexion-extension significantly restricted. No neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays normal; no fracture, subluxation, or significant degenerative change. Findings consistent with acute mechanical neck dysfunction without nerve root involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck occurs in a young person with activity-related acute onset and rapid symptom progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old soccer player reports acute onset severe pain to the lateral leg after direct contact injury during match. Immediate swelling noticed. Pain worsens with weight-bearing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant swelling developed within minutes. Unable to weight-bear or walk normally. Ankle movements painful. Could not return to play.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct blow from another player's knee to lateral leg. Pain and swelling were instantaneous. No prior history of leg or ankle injury. No chronic conditions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant swelling and ecchymosis over lateral leg, mid-fibular region. Fibular head palpable bone tenderness. Pain with ankle plantarflexion and eversion loading. Ankle dorsiflexion intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show simple transverse fracture of fibula at mid-shaft level. No associated tibia fracture. No proximal fibula injury. No ankle syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Fibular fractures may be isolated or associated with ankle fractures requiring appropriate management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive bilateral leg weakness beginning 3 days after viral illness. Weakness started in the feet and is moving upward. Now unable to stand without support.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressing difficulty with fine motor tasks in hands. Facial weakness now apparent. Difficulty chewing and swallowing noted today.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Upper respiratory tract infection 1 week prior with cough and congestion. Complete recovery from respiratory symptoms. 3 days post-infection, leg weakness began acutely.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral lower extremity weakness 4/5 proximally, 3/5 distally. Patellar and Achilles reflexes absent bilaterally. Plantar responses downgoing. Sensation intact to light touch and pinprick throughout. Cranial nerves II-XII intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows acute demyelinating pattern with prolonged latencies and conduction blocks. Cerebrospinal fluid albuminocytologic dissociation (elevated protein, normal cells). Nerve conduction slowing distally.

puzzles.explanation: GBS presenting as ascending paralysis with areflexia following Campylobacter infection, with albuminocytologic dissociation on LP and declining FVC requiring close respiratory monitoring.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational athlete presents with acute onset anterior thigh pain following direct contact during sport. Immediate swelling noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Knee cannot flex beyond 90 degrees. Walking is severely limited. Unable to climb stairs or bear full weight comfortably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury sustained 2 hours ago. No prior injury to this thigh. No anticoagulant use or bleeding disorder history. Swelling rapidly progressive.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant ecchymosis and swelling over quadriceps region. Knee flexion limited to 75 degrees due to pain and swelling. Quadriceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Palpable muscle firmness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle hematoma within quadriceps. Muscle fiber disruption visible with heterogeneous echotexture. No fracture seen on adjacent X-ray imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Muscle contusion is classic 'dead leg' presentation with direct trauma history, localized swelling, and hematoma formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 41-year-old woman reports progressive shoulder weakness and difficulty with pushing activities over the past 3 weeks. No clear injury event. Pain is minimal; the main complaint is loss of strength in the upper extremity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with wall push-ups and bench press-type movements. Able to perform overhead lifting with the arm raised, but pushing motions cause significant weakness. Everyday activities like door closing and pushing off furniture are affected.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No acute trauma identified. Possible nerve injury from heavy lifting or overhead activity. Gradual progressive onset suggesting nerve inflammation or injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular winging present at rest and exacerbated with arm protraction. Serratus anterior strength 3/5 on manual muscle testing. No sensory deficits in upper extremity. Shoulder flexion and abduction ROM full and pain-free. Trapezius and rhomboid strength intact. No cervical radiculopathy signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Electromyography shows denervation of serratus anterior muscle. Motor unit action potentials reduced and reinnervation changes variable. Nerve conduction normal for other upper extremity nerves.

puzzles.explanation: Long thoracic nerve palsy causing serratus anterior paralysis with characteristic winged scapula and weakness of scapular protraction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old racquet sport player presents with lateral elbow pain and functional limitation. The patient reports pain with gripping and upper extremity use. Onset followed an increase in training intensity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational athlete reports gradual onset of lateral-sided elbow pain over the past 6 weeks, coinciding with increased training frequency. Pain is activity-dependent and worse with repetitive arm use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient cannot comfortably perform overhead activities or sustained gripping tasks. Pain worsens progressively through the day with continued use. Rest relieves symptoms within hours.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symptoms developed gradually over 8 weeks without a specific traumatic event. Patient recently changed racquet and doubled weekly training sessions. No prior elbow injuries. No constitutional symptoms or night pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasonography demonstrates tendon thickening and hyperechoic changes in the extensor carpi radialis brevis tendon. Fluid is not typically seen. Imaging findings confirm lateral epicondylitis.

puzzles.explanation: Atypical presentation in a younger athlete with rapid onset, likely from biomechanical errors and sudden increase in activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 13-year-old obese boy presents with groin pain and limping gait. Pain began 2 weeks ago with insidious onset. Hip pain worse with internal rotation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot participate in sports or climb stairs. Limping gait with externally rotated position preference. Pain worsens significantly with weight-bearing over the past 2 weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rapid growth during puberty. Obesity (BMI 32 at age 13). No preceding trauma. Pain with physical activity but worsens with continued loading.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip internal rotation severely restricted (near 0°) and painful throughout range. Hip external rotation preserved. Positive Patrick's test reproduction of groin pain. Knee examination and neurovascular status normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip X-rays (frog-leg view) show widened and irregular epiphyseal plate with femoral head slippage relative to neck. Posterior displacement of capital femoral epiphysis visible. MRI confirms SCFE.

puzzles.explanation: SCFE is a pediatric emergency requiring urgent orthopedic management to prevent complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 62-year-old man admitted 3 weeks ago with severe sepsis requiring mechanical ventilation. Now extubated but reports extreme fatigue and inability to perform basic movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot lift legs against gravity or roll in bed. Requires maximal assist to sit upright. No respiratory distress or pain with attempted movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: ARDS from severe sepsis requiring intubation 2 weeks. Prolonged sedation with paralytic agents. Minimal mobilization during acute illness. Nutritional support inadequate.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness in all muscle groups. Hip strength 2/5, knee extensors 1+/5. Hand grip minimal bilaterally. Tone decreased. Reflexes intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: EMG shows diffuse myopathic pattern without denervation. CK normal. Muscle biopsy demonstrates Type 2 fiber-selective atrophy with minimal inflammatory infiltrate.

puzzles.explanation: Post-COVID ICU-acquired weakness presentation with motor-predominant deficits requiring intensive rehabilitation. Long-term recovery potential given younger age and improving inflammatory markers.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 6-week-old female infant. Parents noticed asymmetry in thigh appearance during diaper changes. No reported pain or fever. Detected during routine newborn screening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Asymmetric skin folds noted. Asymmetric hip abduction when hips flexed and externally rotated. No pain noted as infant non-verbal but shows discomfort with maneuvers.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Born full-term vaginally, no complications. Female sex, first-born, positive family history of hip dysplasia in paternal grandmother. Breech presentation during labor.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ortolani test produces palpable clunk with hip reduction. Barlow test demonstrates hip instability and redislocation. Asymmetric abduction limited to ~60 degrees bilaterally. Bilateral gluteal fold asymmetry present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hip ultrasound shows dysplastic acetabulum with inadequate coverage. Femoral head position lateralized relative to acetabulum. Graf classification system used for assessment.

puzzles.explanation: DDH involves abnormal development of the hip joint where the femoral head is not properly seated in the acetabulum, detected early through clinical tests.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman reports involuntary urine leakage triggered by physical exertion. Symptoms began approximately 1 year ago and have progressed gradually.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids exercise, impact activities, and public settings due to fear of incontinence episodes. Particularly problematic with jumping, running, and coughing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple vaginal deliveries with instrumentation. Menopause 8 years prior. No prior pelvic floor rehabilitation or pessary use. Denies urgency incontinence or nocturia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor muscles on examination. Positive cough stress test. Normal detrusor function on preliminary assessment. Sphincter laxity evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics documents urethral sphincter incompetence and bladder neck hypermobility on cough stress test. Detrusor function normal. Leak point pressure reduced at 45 cm H2O.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows severe weakness with prolapse in an elderly multiparous female with chronic straining and long-standing pelvic floor damage.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old man, hospitalized 5 days post-abdominal surgery, develops acute onset dyspnea and pleuritic chest pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Hypoxemia present (SpO2 89-90% on room air). Severe dyspnea with minimal exertion; cannot lie flat or mobilize.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Post-operative day 5 from abdominal surgery for small bowel obstruction. Immobility during recovery period. Hypercoagulable post-operative state. No anticoagulation prophylaxis given.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachycardia 115, tachypnea 28. Unilateral leg swelling and calf tenderness absent. JVD not prominent. Lung auscultation: scattered crackles bilateral bases. Lower limb DVT signs absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT pulmonary angiography shows filling defect in right lower lobar pulmonary artery confirming embolus. Bilateral subsegmental emboli visible. Echocardiography shows right ventricular strain.

puzzles.explanation: Classic acute PE presentation with risk factors (immobilization, recent surgery), acute onset dyspnea, and confirmatory imaging showing thrombus.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old man reports progressive weakness and slowing down over the past year. Has lost weight unintentionally.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to climb stairs without stopping. Walking distance reduced to approximately 50 metres before fatigue forces rest. Lives independently but leaving home has become difficult.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No recent illness or injury to explain onset. Lives alone with minimal social contact. Hypertension and type 2 diabetes managed with medications. No recent hospitalisation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip extension strength 3+/5 bilaterally. Gait velocity 0.6 m/s, unsteady with reduced stride length. Current weight 10% below his usual baseline. Hand grip strength bilaterally reduced (unable to quantify precise values with standard dynamometry).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum albumin 3.2 g/dL (low). Haemoglobin 11.2 g/dL (mild anaemia). Serum B12 and folate normal. TSH normal. No acute infection or metabolic derangement on basic metabolic panel.

puzzles.explanation: Frailty syndrome is a geriatric syndrome characterized by reduced physiologic reserve, weakness, and vulnerability to adverse outcomes.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old assembly line worker reports radial-sided wrist pain that began gradually 2 months ago. Pain worse with gripping and pinching activities. No acute injury recalled.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling visible over the radial wrist. Cannot perform repetitive gripping work. Fine pinching tasks are severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive assembly line work for 20 years with poor workstation ergonomics. Symptoms progressed insidiously over 2 months without triggering incident. No prior similar episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finkelstein test positive reproducing pain. Tenderness at radial styloid process. Thumb abduction strength reduced secondary to pain. First dorsal compartment swelling palpable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows APL and EPB tendon thickening with synovial effusion. First dorsal compartment tendinitis evident. No tendon subluxation. Mild surrounding edema.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic refractory De Quervain's in elderly patient, atypical in duration and resistance to treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old manual laborer reports acute onset lower back pain with radiation into one leg. Numbness and weakness have developed over the past week, affecting ability to work.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with forward bending and prolonged sitting. Relief occurs with lying down and leg elevation. Weakness specifically affects ankle dorsiflexion and great toe extension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset followed a heavy lifting episode with acute low back strain. Chronic degenerative disc disease predisposes to nerve root compression. Progressive neurological symptoms indicate ongoing nerve compression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Straight leg raise reproduces ipsilateral leg pain at 45 degrees. L5 dermatomal sensory loss (dorsum of foot) present. Hip abduction and ankle dorsiflexion weakness (grade 4/5). Ankle reflex normal; knee reflex intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows L5 disc herniation with compression of the right L5 nerve root in the lateral recess. Signal changes in the L5 myotome on fat-suppressed sequences confirm active nerve compression.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar radiculopathy occurs in an occupational context with mechanical trauma and clear radicular pattern.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old man presents with progressive loss of muscle mass and strength over 2 years. Difficulty with stairs and arising from chair. Generalized weakness noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant difficulty rising from low chairs without armrests, climbing stairs, and carrying groceries. Walks slowly indoors. Requires rail support for safety.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary lifestyle due to knee osteoarthritis. Retirement and reduced physical activity. Poor nutritional intake with reduced protein consumption. Progressive age-related changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized muscle atrophy visible in thighs and upper arms. Leg strength testing shows hip flexors 3+/5, knee extensors 3+/5. Slow gait velocity. Poor sit-stand performance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry shows low appendicular lean mass index (ALM/height² = 5.8 kg/m²), meeting sarcopenia criteria. Serum biomarkers show elevated creatinine-to-cystatin C ratio, reflecting reduced muscle mass.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related sarcopenia involves progressive loss of muscle mass and strength from aging and reduced physical activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old male with acute onset medial thigh pain during hockey game. Unable to continue playing. Pain began suddenly with no prior groin problems.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform hip adduction maneuvers. Pain significantly worse with skating and rapid direction changes. Antalgic gait limiting walking distance.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Previous groin strains treated conservatively 2 years ago, fully resolved. Current injury occurred mid-stride during rapid deceleration. No neurological symptoms, no night pain, no radiation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Adductor tenderness at insertion. Pain with resisted hip adduction testing. Muscle guarding present. Swelling developing at acute phase. Antalgic gait evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows adductor muscle fiber disruption with hematoma formation. Fluid collection at injury site. Partial muscle tear confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Groin strain shows chronic recurrent adductor strain with muscle weakness and biomechanical dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old distance runner with previous history of medial tibial stress syndrome develops recurrent shin pain. Pain along medial tibia worse with running. Returning to training after recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 26-year-old distance runner with recurrent shin pain along the inner leg. Pain worsens with running and has happened before.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to tolerate tempo runs or high-mileage training weeks. Pain improves with rest but returns when volume increases.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pain began acutely 4 weeks ago after doubling weekly mileage. No recent trauma. Patient is female, no systemic disease. Previously treated successfully with load management and targeted strengthening.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays normal without stress fracture. Bone scan shows uptake along medial tibia consistent with stress response. Similar pattern to previous episode of MTSS.

puzzles.explanation: Bilateral medial tibial stress syndrome suggests systemic biomechanical or training factors rather than isolated pathology.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old with acute onset severe pain in the lower leg following a fall. Immediate inability to bear weight and rapid swelling. No prior leg injury history.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Visible angular deformity of the lower leg. Gross swelling and bruising noted. Unable to tolerate any weight or passive movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mechanism occurred 6 hours ago. No relevant past medical history. No medication use. No prior lower limb injuries. Impact to anterior tibia.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible deformity of lower leg with angulation at fracture site. Significant swelling and ecchymosis. Pain with palpation over fractured area. Distal neurovascular status intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Leg X-rays show transverse fracture of mid-tibial shaft with displacement. Fibula fracture also present (associated injury). No vascular compromise. Open fracture assessment performed.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial shaft fractures require careful management due to common complications like compartment syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old male basketball player presents with acute-onset knee pain below the kneecap. Pain started 2 weeks ago during jump training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is activity-dependent: reliably triggered by jumping and explosive movements during basketball. Pain-free with walking. Cannot tolerate prolonged kneeling or stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Adolescent growth spurt with rapid skeletal maturation. Intensive basketball training with frequent jumping. No prior knee pathology.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness localised to inferior patellar pole on direct palpation. Pain reproduced with resisted knee extension and single-leg hop. No knee effusion. Full passive ROM. Normal patellar tracking.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Knee X-rays show small ossification center irregularity at patellar ligament insertion. MRI demonstrates inflammation at patellar ligament enthesis. Growth plate changes evident.

puzzles.explanation: Sinding-Larsen-Johansson disease is traction apophysitis of the inferior patellar pole from growth plate stress during sports.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old woman presents with diffuse anterior knee pain with functional limitation. Pain with squatting, climbing stairs, and prolonged sitting. Gradual onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform single-leg stance or hop. Knee gives way with changes in direction. Pain worse with pivoting movements and prolonged standing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sedentary occupation with prolonged sitting. Recent home exercise program initiation. Possible hip weakness and flexibility issues contributing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Clarks test reproduces anterior knee pain. Vastus medialis strength reduced. Patellar tracking questionable. Pain with single-leg squat. Tight hip flexors and IT band.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Imaging normal without structural pathology. Patellofemoral cartilage intact on MRI. Q-angle and patellar tilt measured normal. Diagnosis based on clinical examination.

puzzles.explanation: Patellofemoral pain is common in runners and results from patellar maltracking and muscular imbalances.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old woman reports dizziness, palpitations, and fatigue that worsen when standing or exercising.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate prolonged upright posture (meetings, shopping). Symptoms improve when supine or sitting. Previously active but now mostly sedentary.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began abruptly 3 months after viral illness. No prior cardiovascular or autonomic symptoms. Lives in warm climate. Family history of syncope.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Supine HR 68, BP 110/70. Standing 2 minutes: HR 118, BP 108/68. No presyncope relief with squatting. Normal cardiac auscultation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table test: HR rises from 68 to 102 bpm within 3 minutes upright, sustained throughout 10-minute test. Systolic BP drops <10 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine abnormally elevated supine (>600 pg/mL). Echocardiogram and Holter monitor normal.

puzzles.explanation: Severe POTS presentation with autonomic hyperactivity and possible mast cell activation. Marked disability despite therapeutic interventions indicates complex autonomic dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old athlete with recurrent episodes of lateral ankle instability and a catching or popping sensation with activity over the past 8 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate cutting and pivoting movements in sport. Pain and instability worse with rapid ankle plantarflexion and inversion, such as during change of direction drills.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury followed an inversion ankle sprain 8 months ago. At the time of the sprain, imaging suggested peroneal tendon involvement. Episodes of instability have been intermittent but recurring with specific movements.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable subluxation of peroneal tendons posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle position changes. Positive peroneal tendon subluxation test. Pain with resisted eversion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound and dynamic MRI show peroneal tendons subluxing posterior to lateral malleolus with ankle plantarflexion and inversion. Superior peroneal retinaculum laxity noted.

puzzles.explanation: Peroneal tendon subluxation causes dynamic instability from loss of retinacular support.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 75-year-old man with severe COPD presents with progressively worsening dyspnea despite therapy. Continuous cough with minimal sputum. Severe functional decline. Minimal exercise tolerance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 75-year-old man with a lifelong smoking history presents with progressive shortness of breath on exertion. He reports a persistent cough productive of minimal sputum. Over the past year, his exercise tolerance has declined significantly.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dyspnea occurs at rest and with minimal exertion. He requires continuous supplemental oxygen to maintain adequate saturation and has become largely housebound.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe cough. Minimal air movement throughout lungs. Prolonged expiration. Accessory muscle use prominent. SpO2 low even on oxygen. Barrel chest prominent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Spirometry shows severe obstruction, FEV1 25% predicted. Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation and emphysema. ABG shows chronic hypoxemia and hypercapnia. Cor pulmonale suspected.

puzzles.explanation: Non-smoking COPD presentation from biomass exposure (common in developing countries). Demonstrates that smoking is not the only etiology of airway obstruction and emphysema.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with stress urinary incontinence from pelvic floor laxity reports functional limitation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 52-year-old woman reports involuntary loss of urine during certain activities. Onset gradual over several years.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot exercise or engage in high-impact activities. Leakage occurs predictably with coughing, sneezing, heavy lifting, and jumping. Avoids social situations due to unpredictable episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Stress test positive. Bladder neck hypermobile. No urgency incontinence component.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Urodynamics confirms stress incontinence. Sphincter incompetence documented. Normal detrusor function without overactivity.

puzzles.explanation: Stress Urinary Incontinence shows mixed incontinence (stress + urge) with overactive bladder component and elevated post-void residual in an older multiparous woman.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 19-year-old presents 2 weeks post-incident with persistent headaches, difficulty concentrating, and feeling 'off balance'. Reports memory lapses for recent events.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cognitive difficulties including memory problems and concentration issues. Headaches daily with light and sound sensitivity. Dizziness and balance difficulty. Functional limitation with school work.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Witnessed loss of consciousness at scene lasting several hours. Post-traumatic amnesia extends beyond the period of unconsciousness. ICU monitoring initiated due to concern for intracranial injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mild cognitive deficits noted on basic testing. Photophobia and phonophobia present. Balance testing shows mild deficit. Headache reproducible with movement. Mood irritability noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Initial CT brain showed small epidural hematoma (managed conservatively). Follow-up CT normal. MRI shows diffuse axonal injury. Neuropsych testing shows mild cognitive deficits.

puzzles.explanation: Moderate traumatic brain injury with loss of consciousness, subdural hematoma, and diffuse axonal injury secondary to high-velocity motor vehicle collision.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old man, 4 weeks post-rotator cuff repair (supraspinatus and infraspinatus), begins physiotherapy. Arm immobilized with sling. Pain controlled on medication.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 58-year-old man presents with shoulder pain and severely restricted arm use. Symptoms began 4 weeks ago following a surgical procedure. Currently wearing a protective sling and managing pain with medication.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot elevate arm overhead or use arm for any functional tasks. Pain increases sharply beyond approximately 70 degrees of flexion or 30 degrees of external rotation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tear was large and involved two muscles (supraspinatus and infraspinatus). Repaired with open approach and anchor fixation. Patient is compliant with gradual ROM progression per surgeon's protocol.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Passive ROM: abduction 65°, external rotation 30°, flexion 70°. Significant scapulohumeral rhythm guarding and pain-inhibited movement. No neurovascular deficits. Pain 3/10 at rest, 5/10 with passive ROM attempts.

puzzles.explanation: Post-rotator cuff repair requires strict immobilization and protection during early healing phases, followed by cautious progression of passive then active-assisted movements, scapular stabilization training, and eventual strengthening to restore function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete reports sudden-onset knee pain and instability during a pivot-and-plant movement. The knee 'gave way' during activity and has not been the same since.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform directional changes or cutting movements without fear of knee buckling. Weight-bearing in slight knee flexion with trunk rotation reproduces apprehension.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient is a contact sport athlete with a family history of knee instability. This is the first episode of acute instability; no prior dislocations or subluxations reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates fear of redislocation. Medial restraint testing reveals ligamentous insufficiency. Quadriceps strength is diminished. Swelling and joint effusion are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Acute patellar dislocation is traumatic and usually reduces spontaneously but leaves significant effusion and apprehension.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman living independently presents to the emergency department following a fall from standing height at home, reporting severe right hip and groin pain with inability to weight-bear.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: She was unable to stand or take any steps after the fall and required an ambulance. Prior to the fall she was ambulatory with a walking stick indoors, and her mobility had been declining over the preceding months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of osteoporosis with declining bone density despite supplementation. Two previous falls in the last year without fracture, but this fall occurred from standing height only. On warfarin for atrial fibrillation, creating perioperative management complexity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The right lower limb is shortened and externally rotated at rest. There is marked tenderness over the greater trochanteric and proximal thigh region. Any attempt at active hip movement produces severe pain, and a positive log-roll test is noted with axial loading.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Pelvic AP X-ray demonstrates a fracture line below the femoral neck in the region between the greater and lesser trochanters. The fracture is classified as an unstable 4-part intertrochanteric pattern, and orthopaedic referral for dynamic hip screw fixation is arranged.

puzzles.explanation: Intertrochanteric fracture complications from severe osteoporosis may lead to loss of fixation or delayed healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old man reports persistent morning stiffness and aching in the shoulders and hips over the past 6 months. Symptoms gradually worsened before seeking care.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to overhead reach or comb hair due to shoulder pain and stiffness. Hip stiffness limits rising from chair and walking distance. Symptoms worst in early morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent-onset bilateral shoulder and hip pain in a patient with elevated inflammatory markers. Minimal prior joint disease history. Rapid symptom progression over 2–3 months before presentation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral shoulder abduction and external rotation limited to 120° and 45° respectively. Hip flexion and internal rotation restricted. Proximal strength 4+/5 but pain-limited. No focal joint swelling or warmth. Grip strength normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR 52 mm/hr, CRP elevated at 8.2 mg/dL. No evidence of temporal artery tenderness or jaw claudication. Temporal artery ultrasound normal, excluding concurrent giant cell arteritis.

puzzles.explanation: Polymyalgia Rheumatica is complicated by Giant Cell Arteritis (temporal arteritis) with vascular manifestations including headache, jaw claudication, and visual symptoms requiring urgent intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational runner with a 3-month history of posterior ankle pain that worsens during training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse with downhill running and explosive plantarflexion activities. Activities requiring repetitive push-off are increasingly restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive symptoms with running. No specific traumatic event. Previous ankle sprain history. Tried relative rest with minimal improvement.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Posterior ankle tenderness with palpation. Pain with passive plantarflexion and inversion positioning. Flexor hallucis longus palpable and tender. Pain reproduced with plantarflexion resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates posterior ankle impingement with flexor hallucis longus involvement. Soft tissue inflammation in posterior compartment. No bony pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Symptomatic os trigonum syndrome causes chronic posterior ankle pain unresponsive to conservative treatment.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old woman presents with progressive swelling of the right arm and hand over the past 2 years. Gradual onset, no acute trauma. Reports heaviness and difficulty with fitting of rings and bracelets.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to wear normal-fitting sleeves. Describes affected arm as persistently heavy. Increased difficulty with overhead activities and fine motor tasks of the hand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Right breast cancer with axillary dissection 3 years prior. No recurrent cancer. Gradual progressive swelling over 2-year period.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Right arm swelling with pitting edema initially, now non-pitting and indurated. Circumference difference 4 cm at midarm. Skin thickened and fibrosed. Reduced shoulder ROM secondary to edema.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lymphoscintigraphy demonstrates impaired lymphatic drainage in right upper extremity. Ultrasound shows increased soft tissue thickness in affected arm. Diameter discrepancy documented.

puzzles.explanation: Secondary lymphedema post-cancer surgery, most common cause in developed countries. Arm circumference difference and fibrotic changes confirm diagnosis. Management focuses on compression and exercise.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old competitive gymnast reports wrist pain with apparatus training. Pain worsens with hand weight-bearing and wrist extension.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform landings or apparatus dismounts. Grip-based training also provokes pain. Symptoms worse after high-volume training days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms began 4 weeks ago during intensive off-season conditioning. No acute injury event. Increased training volume preceding onset. No prior wrist injury or treatment.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pinpoint tenderness over distal radial physeal region. Wrist ROM full in flexion/extension and radial/ulnar deviation. No significant effusion. Grip strength intact. Pain reproduced with loaded wrist extension but not isolated passive motion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Wrist X-rays show widened distal radial growth plate with possible irregularity. MRI shows inflammatory changes at growth plate. Physis intact without displacement.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate fractures require careful management to prevent premature closure and subsequent limb length or alignment complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old woman in a nursing home presents with localized redness over the sacrum that she noticed after her recent hip surgery. She reports minimal discomfort in this area.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient bedbound post-operatively with minimal independent repositioning. Requires full assistance from care staff to change position. Spends extended periods in same position during recovery.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip fracture ORIF 1 week ago. Patient post-operative, immobilized during healing phase. Nursing home resident receiving turning assistance. No prior pressure injuries documented.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Non-blanchable erythema over sacrum. Blanching test negative. Skin surface intact with no ulceration or tissue breakdown. Subcutaneous induration palpable beneath erythema. No purulent drainage. Sensation intact in area.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: No imaging required for diagnosis. Clinical assessment confirms non-blanching erythema without tissue breakdown, consistent with Stage 1 pressure injury per NPUAP classification.

puzzles.explanation: Early pressure injury recognition allows intervention with repositioning, pressure relief, and skin care to prevent wound progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete experienced acute knee pain and instability during a twisting pivot-and-plant maneuver in sport. The knee gave way suddenly, and they felt something shift in the knee before it felt 'normal' again moments later.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform cutting or pivoting movements in sport. Apprehension and fear of the knee giving way limit weight-bearing during lateral movements and weight shifts. Patient reports needing to modify or avoid multi-directional agility tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute patellar dislocation occurred during athletic activity with a pivot-and-plant mechanism. Underlying anatomical predisposition such as increased Q-angle or ligamentous laxity may be present. This is the initial episode of dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension test reproduces fear of redislocation. Lachman and anterior drawer tests are normal, ruling out ACL injury. Patellar tracking is abnormal with lateral glide preference. Moderate joint effusion present. VMO strength noticeably diminished compared to VL.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals acute tear of the medial patellofemoral ligament with bone marrow edema. Small osteochondral fragment visible on lateral femoral condyle. No evidence of other knee ligament injuries or meniscal pathology. Patella is concentrically reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic recurrent patellar dislocation often involves underlying anatomical factors and systemic hypermobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old female ballet dancer presents with a clicking sensation around the hip that started 12 months ago. Initially painless, it has recently become painful with activity and is affecting her training.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The snap occurs reproducibly during hip flexion-extension and lateral movement patterns typical of her dance routine, with sharp brief pain that worsens with rehearsal intensity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: She has no history of hip trauma or prior hip pathology. The onset coincided with a significant increase in her dance training hours twelve months ago. She notes the sound is audible to others in the room during warm-up exercises.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive hip flexion-extension reproduces the snap palpable over the anterior-lateral hip. Ober's test is positive. Hip ROM is full with no restrictions. Pain is elicited with resisted hip flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the hip are normal with no bony abnormality. Dynamic ultrasound imaging confirms snapping of the iliotibial band over the greater trochanter during the provocative movement, with no underlying bursitis or labral pathology identified.

puzzles.explanation: Posterior snapping from ligamentum teres is less common but may coexist with hip instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with an 8-month history of progressive ocular and oral dryness, with constant dry eye sensation affecting daily comfort.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Significant difficulty with sustained near vision tasks (reading, screen work). Also reports difficulty eating dry foods and persistent thirst despite fluid intake.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Insidious progressive onset over several months. Associated fatigue and joint aching. No prior autoimmune history. Symptoms progressive and worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Reduced tear production on Schirmer test (<5 mm in 5 minutes bilaterally). Xerostomia evident on examination. No focal parotid swelling. Ocular surface drying evident on slit lamp.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Anti-SSA/Ro and anti-SSB/La antibodies positive confirming Sjögren syndrome. Labial salivary gland biopsy shows lymphocytic infiltration. Rheumatoid factor positive.

puzzles.explanation: Sjögren's syndrome requires multidisciplinary approach including symptom management, salivary and lacrimal gland support, management of systemic manifestations, monitoring for complications like lymphoma, and fatigue management strategies.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old painter reports lateral elbow pain that started 6 weeks ago. Pain is worse with work activities but he cannot pinpoint a specific injury.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to work at full capacity. Pain limits ability to perform sustained gripping and any forceful wrist movements. Symptoms worsen as the day progresses with continued activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms started without a specific injury event. Patient has continued working despite pain. No prior elbow injuries or treatment. No systemic illness or medications relevant to joint pain. Pain does not wake him at night.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lateral elbow tenderness at epicondyle. Painful resisted wrist extension. Mill's test positive with pain reproduction. Grip strength reduced bilaterally more on affected side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound common extensor origin shows tendinopathic changes with heterogeneity. Mild surrounding edema. No complete rupture. Lateral collateral ligament intact on assessment.

puzzles.explanation: Lateral epicondylitis in an occupational context (not tennis-related), highlighting that repetitive gripping and wrist extension in manual workers is the most common cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old construction worker sustains blow to arm during work accident causing elbow injury. Acute severe pain and deformity. Swelling developing rapidly. Emergency care indicated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 42-year-old construction worker. Acute severe arm pain after direct blow to elbow during work accident. Visible deformity and rapid swelling. Severe pain limiting all movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Arm held in fixed, abnormal position at elbow. Unable to passively extend or flex. Severe pain prevents any weight-bearing or functional use of arm.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: No prior elbow problems. First major injury. Mechanism: caught between machinery at high force. Immediate onset; patient did not attempt self-reduction. Brought to ED within 30 minutes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs show elbow dislocation with possible associated fracture. CT performed for further characterization. Neurovascular assessment. Post-reduction imaging planned.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent/chronic elbow instability after initial dislocation in young athlete with functional deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 81-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty with stairs and rising from a chair over the past 18 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to carry groceries or perform household tasks. Requires assistance with dressing and bathing. Lives with family due to safety concerns.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Lifelong relatively low physical activity. Post-menopausal with inadequate nutrition. Recent decline in function over 1-2 years. Multiple fall history from weakness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked generalized weakness bilaterally. Slow gait with small steps. Hip and knee strength severely reduced 2-3/5. Grip strength markedly diminished. Visible wasting.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA and bioelectrical impedance analysis show appendicular lean mass index <5.4 kg/m² (female threshold for sarcopenia). Muscle quality severely diminished.

puzzles.explanation: Severe sarcopenia in advanced age creates profound functional loss and vulnerability to complications from minimal stress.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman with progressive groin and medial thigh pain reports numbness and weakness. Pain radiates along medial thigh. Progressive symptoms over 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports functional limitation with hip flexion past 90 degrees and internal rotation. Pain worsens with prolonged walking. No difficulty with abduction or external rotation. Able to perform most daily activities but avoids stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding trauma or injury. Patient denies recent hip surgery or groin procedures. No history of pelvic mass or malignancy. Symptoms began insidiously while doing yoga with deep hip flexion and adduction positions. No improvement with rest or NSAIDs over 2 months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip adductor (gracilis, adductor longus) strength 4/5 bilaterally, weaker on right. Hip flexion strength intact. Sensory testing shows patch of diminished sensation on medial thigh below knee. Palpable tenderness at groin with hip flexion and internal rotation. Hip abduction strength normal. No gluteal involvement. Femoral nerve examination normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows possible nerve compression at obturator foramen. Obturator nerve pathway visualized with compression noted. No obvious mass. Possible anatomic variation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-surgical obturator nerve injury with selective hip adductor weakness and medial thigh sensory loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 31-year-old with acute wrist pain and swelling after fall. Wrist held protectively. Unable to bear weight through hand or perform fine motor tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with radial/ulnar deviation and gripping. Swelling visible within 30 minutes. Bruising emerging over dorsal wrist.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Fall occurred during sport. Patient recalled immediate sharp pain but continued activity briefly before realising severity. No prior wrist injury. Wearing standard shoes, no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Wrist swelling and ecchymosis present. Tenderness over scaphoid region and throughout wrist. Wrist motion limited by pain. Neurovascular intact distally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays negative for fracture. MRI shows soft tissue injury between scaphoid and lunate with surrounding edema and fluid. No skeletal involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wrist sprain with scapholunate ligament injury from typical FOOSH mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 32-year-old athlete reports sudden onset groin pain during sport. Sharp quality, acute functional loss.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain provoked specifically with hip adduction and hip flexion/external rotation activities. Difficulty with side-stepping and diagonal movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior groin injuries. Recent increase in training volume over past 3 weeks. Flexible hips but limited adductor flexibility. Warm-up completed before injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along medial thigh in longitudinal distribution. Pain on resisted hip adduction (30° hip flexion). Adductor bulk palpable but no obvious discontinuity. Antalgic gait with hip adduction avoidance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows muscle fiber disruption with surrounding inflammation. Hematoma forming. Adductor muscle involvement confirmed. No complete rupture.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic adductor strain develops gradually from overuse or inadequate flexibility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man with chronic dizziness for 10 months reports constant swaying sensation. Balance difficulty with movement. Anxiety about symptoms prominent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids crowded environments and driving. Symptoms worse when focusing on visual targets or when walking in open spaces. Social withdrawal due to fear of symptom onset in public.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mild head injury with brief dizziness 10 months prior. Dizziness persisted after physical recovery. Progressive anxiety worsened symptoms. Multiple specialist evaluations unrevealing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural sway present but mild on standing. Dizziness reproducible with rapid head turns and gaze shifts. Romberg test normal; tandem stance mild instability. Hyperventilation provokes dizziness and lightheadedness. Nystagmus absent. Dix–Hallpike and head roll tests negative.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Caloric testing normal, head impulse test normal. videonystagmography within normal limits. MRI head and internal auditory canals normal. No evidence of benign paroxysmal positional vertigo, vestibular neuritis, or central pathology. Diagnosis anchored to persistent symptoms and anxiety profile rather than objective abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness shows primary PPPD without documented prior vestibular event, with prominent anxiety and visual/postural triggers with completely normal objective testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 15-year-old basketball player with knee pain during intensive training. Onset gradual over past 4-6 weeks, worse with jumping and running.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate plyometric training or high-impact sports. Symptoms worsen with repetitive loading. Some relief with rest.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive training program with frequent jumping and running. Rapid growth during adolescent spurt. Inadequate recovery between training sessions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Localized tenderness over medial aspect of proximal tibia. Pain reproduction on single-leg hop test. No effusion. Knee ligament stability tests normal. Full ROM.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Widened growth plate with increased T2 signal on MRI at proximal tibial physis. No metaphyseal fracture line or avulsion. Physis remains open and intact.

puzzles.explanation: Growth plate injury complications include premature closure causing limb length and angular deformities requiring corrective surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 29-year-old rugby player injured their ankle during a tackle. Reports pain above the ankle joint, worse with pivoting and weight-bearing movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight comfortably or change direction quickly. Pain reproduced with external rotation movements of the ankle.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 3 days ago. Patient reports immediate swelling and severe pain, preventing return to sport. No prior ankle injuries. No physiotherapy yet attempted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Syndesmotic tenderness above ankle joint, between tibia and fibula. Pain with external rotation stress test positive. Squeeze test positive (pain with compression of tibia/fibula).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ankle X-rays with weight-bearing show widened tibiofibular space suggesting syndesmotic disruption. Stress radiographs demonstrate increased talar rotation. CT further characterizes injury.

puzzles.explanation: High ankle sprains involve the syndesmotic ligaments and carry risk of chronic pain and instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male boxer presents with acute hand pain and swelling after a punch during a match.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot make a closed fist. Inability to clench hand without sharp pain over the knuckle area.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during training. Player felt immediate sharp pain and heard/felt a crack. No prior hand injuries or fracture history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Fifth metacarpal head swelling, ecchymosis, and angulation. Pain with palpation over fracture site. Prominence of knuckle reduced (depressed fracture appearance).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand X-rays show fifth metacarpal neck fracture with apex dorsal angulation (classic 'boxer's fracture'). Displacement and angulation measured on X-rays.

puzzles.explanation: Boxer's fracture shows fifth metacarpal neck fracture from punching mechanism with typical angulation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old recreational tennis player presents with posterior elbow pain that worsens with forceful arm movements. Pain began 4 weeks ago and has been gradually worsening.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead throwing or serving motions. Pushing and pressing movements (like bench press or wall push-ups) reproduce pain. Training intensity significantly reduced.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Increased tennis training volume focusing on serving technique. Repetitive overhead serving causing triceps overload. Progressive symptoms over 4 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness over triceps insertion at olecranon. Resisted elbow extension reproduces sharp pain. Triceps strength 4/5 due to pain inhibition. Possible palpable swelling or thickening at insertion site.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows triceps tendon thickening at insertion with heterogeneous echotexture. Possible small tear or chronic inflammation. Olecrannon bursa may show fluid.

puzzles.explanation: Triceps tendinopathy from repetitive extension and throwing activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 45-year-old woman with low back pain and reduced spinal mobility 6 weeks after lumbar surgery. Pain controlled with analgesics. Incision healing well.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot bend forward beyond 20 degrees or extend backward beyond 10 degrees without pain or apprehension. Difficulty with stairs and prolonged sitting (>15 minutes). Unable to lift more than 5 kg.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Prior non-operative management failed (6 months physiotherapy and analgesia). Imaging pre-operatively showed L4-L5 disc degeneration with facet arthropathy and intermittent neurogenic claudication. Symptoms progressively worsened over 2 years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar ROM: flexion 20°, extension 10°, lateral flexion 15° bilaterally. Straight leg raise 70° bilaterally, pain-free. Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Neurological examination normal. Incision well-healed, no signs of infection. Pain 2-3/10 at rest, 4-5/10 with movement.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-ray (6 weeks): Fusion hardware properly positioned across L4-L5. Lateral view shows appropriate restoration of disc height and sagittal alignment. No evidence of hardware loosening, malposition, or breakage. Early bony bridging between vertebral bodies visible on CT.

puzzles.explanation: Post-lumbar fusion rehabilitation requires long-term graduated progression, emphasis on core stabilization without spinal motion, modification of activity patterns, and realistic expectations for return to function given the extensive surgical trauma and fusion site healing requirements.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old assembly line worker presents with onset of dry cough and shortness of breath that worsens during work shifts. Symptoms began 2 months ago and have progressively increased in frequency. History of allergic rhinitis but no prior asthma diagnosis.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cough and dyspnea are significantly worse during work shifts and improve markedly during weekends and vacations. Patient notes associated wheezing and chest tightness but denies orthopnea, PND, or symptoms at rest during non-work periods.

clues.label: History
clues.text: 3-year cumulative exposure to workplace chemicals without consistent respiratory protection. Symptom onset temporal relationship to recent workplace environmental change. No prior asthma, but baseline allergic rhinitis. No previous occupational lung disease screening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spirometry shows obstructive pattern with FEV1/FVC ratio 65% and reversibility with bronchodilators. Peak flow variability exceeds 20% during work weeks versus non-work days. Bronchial hyperresponsiveness confirmed on methacholine challenge test.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest unremarkable, excluding alternative parenchymal or structural pathology. Serological testing for specific occupational sensitization markers pending.

puzzles.explanation: Occupational asthma shows work-triggered bronchoconstriction from occupational sensitization or irritant exposure.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old otherwise healthy adult presents with sudden onset severe vertigo upon waking this morning. No hearing loss reported. Recent upper respiratory viral illness 5 days prior to symptom onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Vertigo is constant and disabling, with dizziness exacerbated by head movements and eye closure. Patient experiences significant nausea with vomiting. Symptoms slightly improve with bed rest and head immobilization.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior episodes of vertigo. Patient reports gradual return to upright posture tolerance over 48 hours. No medication changes. Works in office environment with minimal physical demands.

clues.label: Examination
