clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in bilateral upper extremities and thighs. Hip flexor and knee extensor strength 4/5 bilaterally; shoulder and elbow strength 4+/5. Tight hip flexors, otherwise no contractures. Normal tone. No tremor, fasciculations, or sensory loss. Mild difficulty with single-leg stance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan: fat-free mass index 5.2 kg/m² (below sarcopenia cutoff <5.75). MRI thigh: marked reduction in muscle cross-sectional area with diffuse intramuscular fat infiltration; no focal muscle edema or signal change to suggest inflammatory myopathy. Prealbumin 17 mg/dL (low, <20). Albumin 3.1 g/dL (low). CRP 2.1 mg/L (normal). TSH, creatine kinase normal. Vitamin D 18 ng/mL (deficient).

puzzles.explanation: Severe sarcopenia with deconditioning results in loss of independence and high risk for adverse outcomes in elderly patients.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 35-year-old woman presenting with chronic widespread pain and stiffness 8 months post-motor vehicle collision. Reports sleep disturbance and significant anxiety. Avoids driving and has reduced exercise tolerance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens with unexpected loud noises and situations resembling the accident environment. Demonstrates guarded, protective movement patterns. Displays heightened vigilance and exaggerated startle response during examination.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Severe motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness and hospitalization. Reported whiplash mechanism with extensive soft tissue injuries. Persistent intrusive memories of event and active avoidance of vehicles and driving. Elevated mood symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Upper cervical and thoracic ROM restricted in all planes with protective guarding. Cervical paraspinals and upper trapezius demonstrate elevated resting tone and multiple trigger points. Forward head posture present. Movement reproduction fails to identify structural pain source. Neurological examination unremarkable.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI cervical spine normal — no fracture, disc herniation, or cord pathology. Mild paraspinal muscle edema noted. Pain catastrophizing and fear-avoidance beliefs scale scores in upper range, indicating significant pain-related anxiety and cognitive amplification of symptoms.

puzzles.explanation: PTSD in rehabilitation shows complex PTSD with severe symptoms significantly limiting rehabilitation despite physical recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete experiences acute knee pain and instability following a twisting injury during sport. The knee felt like it 'gave out' during activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot perform lateral movements or cutting maneuvers without fear of instability. Weight-bearing on a bent knee triggers apprehension. Cannot return to pivoting sports.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The injury occurred during a sudden deceleration with foot planted. Patient reports this is the first time this has happened. Physiotherapist notes family history of knee instability and patient's naturally loose joints.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Apprehension sign present with lateral patellar pressure. Medial patellofemoral ligament tenderness on palpation. Significant knee effusion restricts active extension in final 10 degrees.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate a patella that is reduced in anatomical position with possible osteochondral findings. MRI shows acute ligamentous disruption of the medial patellofemoral ligament. Imaging confirms acute patellar dislocation.

puzzles.explanation: Recurrent patellar subluxation indicates structural or neuromuscular predisposition requiring stabilization.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old retired teacher reports dizzy spells upon standing from a seated or supine position, occurring several times daily. Experiencing presyncope without loss of consciousness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms resolve within seconds to minutes of remaining still. Patient restricts activities due to fall risk concern. Slower positional changes help reduce episodes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of hypertension managed with antihypertensive medications including ACE inhibitor and calcium channel blocker. Recent dose increase 2 weeks prior. No cardiac arrhythmias noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Systolic blood pressure drops 25 mmHg when rising from supine to standing (sitting 142/88, standing 117/82). Heart rate appropriately increases from 62 to 80 bpm. No orthostatic tachycardia or paradoxical bradycardia. Gait steady, no balance deficits. No focal neurological signs.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Home blood pressure log over 7 days shows reproducible 20–30 mmHg systolic drops in morning hours and early evening. Orthostatic vital signs measured at multiple times of day confirm consistent pattern. No postural tachycardia or compensatory HR elevation variance.

puzzles.explanation: Postural hypotension from medications or autonomic dysfunction causes blood pressure drops with position change, increasing fall risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 61-year-old man 6 weeks post-major cardiac surgery presents with progressive dyspnea and chest discomfort triggered by minimal activity. Exercise tolerance severely limited. Reports fatigue affecting daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Dyspnea limiting walking to 100 metres on flat ground. Chest wall pain restricts overhead reaching and shoulder mobility. Marked tachycardia response: HR 110 at rest, 140 with minimal walking. Disproportionate heart rate response compared to activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six weeks post-CABG x3 with saphenous vein grafts. Ejection fraction improved from 35% to 50% post-operatively. Pre-operative sedentary lifestyle. Comorbidities: hypertension, hyperlipidemia, type 2 diabetes. No recent complications reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sternotomy incision well-healed without dehiscence. Shoulder abduction limited to 100 degrees due to guarding and incisional pain. Paradoxical breathing pattern with chest wall splinting evident. Orthostatic vital signs normal. Gait slow and cautious. No signs of acute cardiac compromise at rest (HR 85, BP 138/82).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative echocardiography: ejection fraction 50% with anterior wall motion normalising. ECG normal sinus rhythm, no ischemic changes. Cardiac biomarkers (troponin, BNP) normalized. Stress testing at 6 weeks: no ischemic changes, target heart rate achieved. Conclusion: exercise limitation attributable to deconditioning and musculoskeletal guarding rather than cardiac ischaemia or graft compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Early post-CABG rehabilitation phase focusing on sternotomy healing and deconditioning. Pain management and graduated activity progression are primary concerns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 24-year-old hockey player presents with acute onset anterior chest wall pain after collision during play. Reports sudden swelling and visible deformity over medial shoulder region.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to adduct arm across body without sharp anterior chest pain. Cross-body reach test highly restricted. Overhead reaching also limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during high-impact collision with direct anterior force. No prior shoulder or clavicular pathology. No neurovascular symptoms reported at time of injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness over sternoclavicular joint. Medial clavicle palpably prominent and mobile superiorly. Pain reproduction with cross-body adduction. Upper extremity strength 5/5 throughout. Pulses and sensation intact distally. Deep palpation suggests soft tissue involvement extending posteriorly toward sternum.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP and 40-degree cephalic tilt views) shows anterior displacement of medial clavicle with joint space widening. CT chest demonstrates clavicle positioned anterior to sternum. No vascular compromise on imaging. Soft tissue swelling around joint evident on advanced imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Sternoclavicular joint dislocation with anterior deformity and risk of mediastinal complications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old man reports progressive anterior shoulder pain over 6 months. He struggles with tasks like putting on shirts and reaching behind his back. Pain is worse at night and disrupts sleep. History of overhead work activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform the lift-off test (hand locked against back). Weakness with internal rotation strength testing. Sharp anterior pain on resisted internal rotation. Scapular winging observed during arm elevation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic rotator cuff pathology with degenerative changes. Progressive symptoms over 6 months despite physical therapy. Age-related tissue degeneration contributing. History of overhead work stress. Poor proprioceptive control and scapular dyskinesis noted.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lift-off test impossible (0/5 power, hand cannot lift from back). Bear-hug test shows 3/5 weakness. Positive anterior slide test. Tenderness over subscapularis insertion. Full passive internal rotation preserved; weakness only with resistance.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows subscapularis tendon tear with full-thickness component at musculotendinous junction. Associated rotator cuff pathology with supraspinatus and infraspinatus involvement. Subscapularis muscle atrophy evident. Bone marrow edema at lesser tuberosity insertion site. Ultrasound confirms tear with loss of normal tendon architecture.

puzzles.explanation: Subscapularis tear with loss of internal rotation strength and positive lift-off test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 82-year-old woman reports two falls in the past month. Describes dizziness preceding each event. Expresses reduced confidence with mobility.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Requires contact guard during walking. Complains of lightheadedness when changing position from sitting to standing. Demonstrates hesitant, slow movement pattern. Avoiding previously tolerated activities due to fear of recurrence.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 82 with multiple fall risk factors: osteoporosis, hypertension on multiple medications causing orthostatic hypotension, mild cognitive impairment, vision reduced from cataracts. Lives alone in cluttered home environment. History of vitamin D insufficiency.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Timed up and go test 28 seconds (abnormal >12 seconds). Single leg stance 3 seconds each leg (abnormal <10 seconds). Berg Balance Scale score 38/56 (high fall risk <45). Orthostatic vital signs positive (systolic BP drop 20 mmHg on standing). Gait assessment shows reduced stride length, shuffle pattern, and reduced arm swing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DEXA scan confirms osteoporosis (T-score -2.9). Brain MRI normal. Vestibular assessment normal. Medication review confirms orthostatic-inducing antihypertensive regimen contributing to BP drops on standing.

puzzles.explanation: High-risk recurrent falls in frail elderly with multiple comorbidities require comprehensive assessment and preventive interventions.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 34-year-old man catches ring finger in door resulting in hyperflexion injury. Immediate pain over distal phalanx with inability to extend distal interphalangeal (DIP) joint. Minimal swelling and no gross deformity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 34-year-old man sustained acute finger injury when hand caught in door 1 day ago. Reports pain at tip of finger with inability to straighten the finger. Minimal visible swelling.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot actively straighten the distal joint of affected finger. Swelling mild and localised to fingertip. Sensory function and grip strength normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Injury occurred 1 day ago with no prior finger problems or systemic disease. Patient has low occupational trauma risk but reports immediate functional loss after incident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: DIP joint held in 30-degree fixed flexion. Cannot actively extend against gravity or resistance. Passive extension elicits pain. Extensor mechanism appears intact proximally. No skin lacerations or bruising on dorsal surface. Neurological examination normal.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic untreated mallet finger with secondary deformities and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old woman reports palpitations, lightheadedness, and tremor that consistently occur when standing up from sitting or lying down.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate standing for >10-15 minutes without symptom worsening. Exercise causes disproportionate fatigue and palpitations. Lying down provides rapid symptom relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms present for 3 years with gradual onset post-viral illness. No cardiac history. Symptoms worsen with heat, dehydration, and menstrual cycle.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting supine vitals normal (HR 62 bpm, BP 118/76). On standing, heart rate rises to 115 bpm within 5 minutes with systolic BP drop <10 mmHg. Patient reports palpitations and lightheadedness with standing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Tilt table testing (70° for 10 minutes): heart rate increases from 64 to 118 bpm while systolic BP remains stable at 116 mmHg. Plasma norepinephrine elevated when upright (500 pg/mL). Echocardiography and ECG normal.

puzzles.explanation: Classic POTS presentation with sustained excessive heart rate increase on postural change, post-viral onset, and autonomic dysfunction confirmed by tilt testing. High prevalence in young women.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young athlete presents with recurrent shoulder instability during sports activities. The patient reports sensation of shoulder slipping or apprehension with certain movement patterns. Functional limitation affects upper extremity performance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Young athlete reports intermittent shoulder symptoms affecting throwing and overhead activities. Onset occurred during sports participation. Patient describes episodes of fear or hesitation with certain arm positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient unable to perform throwing activities or rapid overhead arm movements. Loss of confidence in shoulder control during sports-specific positions. Difficulty returning to pre-injury athletic performance.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive apprehension test indicates instability fear. Relocation test (Jobe's) relieves apprehension. Shoulder range of motion in external rotation is increased. Generalized capsular laxity is evident on examination.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Apprehension test positive at 90° abduction and external rotation. Relocation (Jobe's) test reproduces confidence. Sulcus sign present. Compared to contralateral side, significant asymmetry in external rotation range. Positive load-and-shift test indicating humeral head translation.

puzzles.explanation: First-time anterior shoulder dislocation in young athlete with typical mechanism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old man with a 20-year history of skin disease presents with 6 months of progressive joint pain affecting fingers, wrists, and knees. Morning stiffness lasting 1.5 hours. Insidious onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to grip effectively (reduced functional grasp). Difficulty with fine finger movements and wrist flexion/extension. Morning stiffness prevents normal morning routine for first 1-2 hours.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Skin condition flared 2 months before joint symptoms began. Strong family history of both skin disease and inflammatory arthritis. No previous joint problems. No recent infections or trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Asymmetric polyarthritis with dactylitis (sausage appearance of fingers). Nail dystrophy with pitting and onycholysis. Psoriatic plaques on elbows, knees, and scalp. Tenderness at Achilles insertions bilaterally. Wrist and finger joint swelling with warmth. Reduced grip strength (28 kg). Joint flexion contractures developing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ESR elevated at 42 mm/hr, CRP 8.2 mg/dL. Rheumatoid factor negative, anti-CCP negative (seronegative pattern). HLA-B27 positive (positive in 70% of PsA). Hand and wrist X-rays show periosteal new bone formation (fluffy periostitis) and pencil-in-cup deformity early stages. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity indicating active inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Psoriatic Arthritis shows a predominantly axial/spinal presentation with minimal skin manifestations, demonstrating spondyloarthropathy features with late skin diagnosis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute posterior thigh pain during 400-meter race. Pain onset abrupt during acceleration phase. Immediate functional loss with difficulty continuing sprint.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 28-year-old athlete presents with acute onset posterior thigh pain during high-intensity training. Pain occurred suddenly during an explosive movement. Unable to continue activity immediately.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain triggered by hip flexion and knee extension in combination. Cannot weight-bear without protective antalgic gait. Swelling and bruising visible within 24 hours of onset.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness at mid-belly of hamstring. Swelling and warmth at mid-thigh location. Positive straight leg raise test with pain (limited to 40 degrees). Weak hip extension against resistance (3/5) due to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stride length. Visible ecchymosis beginning to appear.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Palpable tenderness at mid-musculotendinous region with localized warmth. Straight leg raise limited to 40 degrees due to pain. Hip extension weakness (3/5) with preserved proprioception. Gait markedly antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected limb.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic hamstring symptoms often follow previous injury and reflect ongoing weakness and flexibility deficits.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old woman presents with catching and locking sensation in the right ring finger at the MCP joint. Clicking noise with finger flexion and extension. Worsens in morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with fine motor tasks including writing and typing. Painful with full finger extension. Functional limitation in hand dexterity and grip activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset over 3 months. No acute trauma. Repetitive gripping activities in workplace (packing work). Symptoms progressive with worsening locking episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Nodular thickening palpable over A1 pulley at MCP joint. Triggering reproduced with active finger flexion and extension. Triggering relieved with passive extension by opposite hand. Swelling at pulley level.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates nodular thickening of flexor tendon at A1 pulley with increased fluid signal around tendon. Swelling is localised to pulley, with no evidence of osteoarthritic changes or joint pathology. Findings consistent with stenosing tenosynovitis.

puzzles.explanation: Classic trigger finger with morning stiffness and clicking, typical middle-aged presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old sprinter presents with acute onset posterior thigh pain. Pain began mid-competition during a high-speed running effort. Unable to continue activity immediately following onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Sharp pain localizes to posterior mid-thigh during running and kicking motions. Visible swelling and bruising developed within 24 hours. Antalgic gait with markedly reduced stride length.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute traumatic mechanism during sprinting with forceful hip flexion and knee extension (acceleration phase of running). Young athletic male with high-demand sport. No prior hamstring injury. Pre-existing hip flexor tightness noted. Inadequate warm-up prior to race.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tender mass at mid-thigh with localized warmth. Straight leg raise limited to 40° due to pain. Hip extension strength 3/5 against resistance. Pain on resisted knee flexion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI confirms Grade II partial thickness hamstring strain at musculotendinous junction of biceps femoris. Hematoma present at injury site (4cm x 3cm). Surrounding muscle edema. Intact fascial integrity (not retracted). Ultrasound shows hypoechoic fluid collection at strain site with intact muscle architecture proximally and distally.

puzzles.explanation: Partial hamstring strains can present more insidiously, often related to inadequate warm-up or prior tightness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old man with 5-year history of progressive lower back pain and bilateral leg pain. Symptoms worsening over past 6 months with increasing heaviness and fatigue in legs with walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Back pain and leg symptoms worse with standing and lumbar extension; significantly relieved by sitting and forward bending. Walking tolerance severely limited; symptoms do not improve when standing still, only with rest in flexion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual progressive course. Prior imaging noted degenerative disc changes in lower lumbar spine. Occupational history: 20 years heavy lifting and repetitive twisting. Limited core strength and sedentary lifestyle outside work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar extension reproduces back pain and bilateral leg heaviness. Forward flexion limited (hands to knees). Right ankle dorsiflexion weakness 4/5 (L5 myotome). Right ankle reflex diminished. Bilateral straight leg raise limited to 45° by pain in posterior leg.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows L4-5 spondylolisthesis with L4 vertebra slipped anteriorly 3mm relative to L5. Degenerative disc changes at L4-5 and L5-S1. MRI confirms spondylolisthesis with significant central spinal stenosis at L4-5 level (50% canal narrowing). Ligamentum flavum hypertrophy and disc bulging contributing. Foraminal stenosis on left side.

puzzles.explanation: Lumbar spondylolisthesis presents as an acute traumatic event in a young patient with significant inflammation and functional loss.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents 12 weeks after a significant hip injury with progressive loss of motion, pain, and swelling in the hip region. Activities of daily living increasingly difficult.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot dress or perform hip-intensive ADLs due to motion loss. Hip flexion contracture present; internal and external rotation markedly restricted. Pain limits all hip movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: High-energy posterior hip dislocation with complex femoral head fracture 12 weeks prior. Significant soft tissue trauma and prolonged immobilization. Male, age 42.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture present (unable to achieve full extension). External and internal rotation severely limited (<15 degrees each direction). Abduction limited to 10 degrees. Palpable stiffness with firm end-feel on passive motion. Tenderness over hip region. Functional limitation with gait and transfers.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows extensive bony bridging across hip joint with mature ossification. CT demonstrates mature bone formation in soft tissues surrounding hip capsule. Associated joint space narrowing noted.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification is extensive and disabling with multiple sites of ectopic bone formation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old warehouse worker presents with chronic lower back pain for 3 years exacerbated by work activities. Pain worse at end of work week. Associated work-related absence and functional limitations.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 38-year-old warehouse worker with 3 years of intermittent lower back pain. Notices pain builds throughout the work week and improves slightly with weekends off. Has taken several weeks off work in the past year due to flare-ups.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot sit comfortably for more than 30 minutes. Standing tolerance improving as day progresses. Symptoms improve with gentle movement but worsen sharply with repetitive lifting. No radiation below the knee.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Limited forward flexion to fingertips 15cm from toes due to pain. Straight leg raise limited to 60 degrees by hamstring tightness and pain. One-legged stance provocative on both sides with pain reproduction. Lumbar paraspinal tenderness with guarding. Gait antalgic. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Forward flexion limited to 15cm from toes with pain at end-range. Bilateral SLR 60° limited by pain (not neurological irritation). Hip and knee ROM full and pain-free. Lumbar rotation worse than flexion. Neurologically intact with normal lower limb reflexes and strength. Palpable spasm in lumbar paraspinals bilateral; pain reproduced with resisted Valsalva.

puzzles.explanation: Lower back pain shows chronic occupational low back pain with significant functional limitation and psychological overlay.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old man presents with progressive morning stiffness in lumbar spine lasting 2 hours daily for 8 months. Pain improves with activity and worsens with rest. Nocturnal pain awakening him from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Spinal stiffness particularly in morning and with inactivity. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Pain improves with activity and exercise. Progressive loss of spinal mobility with difficulty bending forward. Associated fatigue and malaise.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Morning stiffness and spinal rigidity limiting forward bending. Buttock and lower back pain described as deep ache. Associated fatigue and malaise. Pain improves significantly with activity and exercise, worsening with prolonged rest.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Insidious onset over 8 months without preceding infection, trauma, or occupational exposure. No significant medical history. Family history of autoimmune or rheumatologic disease unknown. Morning stiffness pattern and improvement with activity suggest inflammatory rather than mechanical aetiology. No prior imaging or rheumatologic workup performed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI sacroiliac joints shows bilateral sacroiliitis with erosions and inflammatory signal changes. Lumbar spine shows syndesmophyte formation connecting vertebral bodies (bamboo spine early phase). HLA-B27 positive. ESR elevated at 48 mm/hr, CRP 9.2 mg/dL. Complete blood count and metabolic panel normal.

puzzles.explanation: Ankylosing spondylitis presents in young males with inflammatory back pain pattern (morning stiffness >30 min, improved by exercise), extra-articular features, and bilateral sacroiliitis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 54-year-old man, post-operative day 3 from major abdominal surgery. Worsening shortness of breath and oxygen saturation drop to 90% on room air. Fever 38.5°C. Significant pain at surgical site limiting ability to breathe deeply.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform incentive spirometry or effective coughing due to pain. Prefers shallow, rapid breathing pattern. Reluctant to mobilise or change position due to discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Anesthesia 3 hours. Standard post-operative analgesia regimen (opioid-based). No prior lung disease or smoking history. Mobilisation deferred due to pain severity. Patient reports feeling 'unable to take a deep breath' and avoiding coughing.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: RR 24/min. Breath sounds diminished at both lower lobes, particularly left. Dullness to percussion over left lower lobe field. Coarse crackles in lower zones on inspiration. SpO₂ 90% RA, improves to 95% with supplemental oxygen. Fever 38.5°C. Chest wall splinting evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows opacification and collapse of left lower lobe fields consistent with atelectasis. Right lower lobe shows similar but less severe changes. Elevated hemidiaphragm on left. CT chest confirms left lower lobe consolidation and volume loss. No pneumonia (no peripheral consolidation pattern initially).

puzzles.explanation: Atelectasis from splinting due to rib fracture pain. Demonstrates importance of pain control and respiratory efforts post-trauma, particularly in patients with underlying lung disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old woman with 8 years of chronic lower back pain. Gradual onset without inciting event. Pain mechanical in nature, worse with activity and sitting.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes that improves with movement. Difficulty with forward bending. Declining standing tolerance by end of day. Pain relief with specific position changes and recumbency.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 62 with degenerative changes expected with aging. Occupational prolonged standing and repetitive bending for 35 years. History of heavy lifting in younger years. Sedentary lifestyle contributing to deconditioning. Smoking history 30 pack-years with known effects on disc health.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Lumbar forward flexion limited to fingertips 20cm from toes. Straight leg raise normal (70 degrees) without radiculopathy. Lumbar paraspinal muscles tight with guarding. One-legged stance normal without pain. Neurologically completely intact. Gait normal without antalgic pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays show multilevel degenerative disc disease with loss of disc height at L4-5 and L5-S1. Osteophyte formation present (bone spurs). Facet joint osteoarthritis. MRI shows disc desiccation with decreased T2 signal in multiple levels. Modest disc bulging at L4-5 without stenosis. No neural compromise.

puzzles.explanation: Degenerative disc disease is a chronic condition in an older patient with imaging findings exceeding clinical symptoms.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 24-year-old elite endurance athlete presents with persistent fatigue and declining athletic performance despite increased training volume over 3 months. Resting heart rate elevated 15 beats above baseline. Multiple minor illnesses over past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to sustain previous training paces. Completes sessions but lacks usual power and speed. Feels 'heavy' during running. Takes 48-72 hours to feel recovered between hard sessions versus previous 24-hour recovery. Irritable and unmotivated about training despite previous enthusiasm.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Intensive endurance training program with progressively increased volume without adequate recovery. Mileage increased 50% over 3 months. Inadequate sleep averaging 6 hours nightly. Poor nutritional recovery with insufficient caloric intake. Psychological stress from competition and team dynamics.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Resting heart rate elevated at 75 bpm (personal baseline 60 bpm). Heart rate recovery after exercise test shows delayed normalization. VO2 max testing shows 10% reduction compared to previous testing. Psychological assessment shows mood disturbance and depression symptoms. Musculoskeletal examination normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Blood work shows normal thyroid function excluding thyroiditis. Normal complete blood count excluding anemia. Serum cortisol elevated with abnormal circadian rhythm. Ferritin at lower limit of normal suggesting iron depletion. CPK mildly elevated at 380 IU/L suggesting muscle stress. No viral serology positive.

puzzles.explanation: Overtraining syndrome shows classic presentation with performance plateau, hormonal dysfunction, and energy deficit despite high training load.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 79-year-old woman with progressive functional decline and multiple comorbidities presents with weakness and fatigue.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 79-year-old woman reports progressive tiredness and difficulty keeping up with daily tasks over the past 12 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot bathe or dress independently. Falls twice in past month without clear trauma. Increasingly confined to home. Family reports she's sleeping more and attending fewer social activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Diabetes and heart disease managed suboptimally. Unintentional 8 kg weight loss over 8 months. Poor appetite. Lives alone, family provides twice-weekly support. Denies recent hospitalization or acute illness. Medications often missed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Hand grip strength 12 kg (very low for age/sex). Knee extension 3/5 bilaterally. Timed up and go 35 seconds. Cannot rise from chair without arm support. Mini-Cog score 2/3 (cognitive impairment). Heart rate 92, BP 138/82 at rest.

puzzles.explanation: Advanced frailty with multiple deficits creates vulnerability to cascading complications and poor prognosis requiring comprehensive geriatric care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An 82-year-old woman reports progressive difficulty walking over the past 18 months. She is increasingly unsteady, has multiple falls weekly despite supervision, and can no longer manage stairs independently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient demonstrates short, shuffling steps (approximately 15cm stride). Arm swing is absent and posture is stiff. Balance is severely impaired even with contact guard. Walking becomes noticeably worse when she tries to perform another task simultaneously (e.g., talking while ambulating).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has severe cognitive impairment limiting her ability to follow movement instructions or adapt to corrections. She has been sedentary in a care facility for several years and has lost 8 kg over the past year. Known hypertension and osteoporosis present.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Gait speed measured at 0.3 m/sec. Hip and knee flexion markedly reduced during swing phase. Patient cannot lift feet properly, dragging them forward. Stance transitions reveal significant postural instability and risk of collapse. Forward head posture and stooped trunk evident. Patient cannot correct gait errors even when instructed verbally.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI shows severe cortical atrophy with enlargement of lateral ventricles and multiple white matter changes. The pattern of atrophy involves frontal and parietal regions including motor and executive control areas. No acute focal lesions, stroke, or intracranial hemorrhage.

puzzles.explanation: Dementia-related mobility decline reflects progressive brain atrophy and neurodegeneration affecting motor control and balance.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old presents with insidious-onset knee pain and stiffness over several years. Pain worsens with activity and is present first thing in the morning.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports pain peaks mid-to-late afternoon after activity. Swelling appears after prolonged standing. Walking on flat surfaces is less symptomatic than walking downhill or descending stairs.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No significant acute trauma. Patient reports previous knee ligament injury 15 years ago. Progressive worsening over past 3 years despite activity modification. One prior intra-articular steroid injection 6 months ago with temporary relief.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint line tenderness on palpation. Crepitus on active ROM throughout range. Flexion limited to 100°, extension lag of 10°. Mild palpable effusion. Positive McMurray's test (ambiguous). Quadriceps strength 4/5 bilaterally, hip abductors 4+/5.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs demonstrate joint space narrowing, osteophyte formation, and subchondral sclerosis. Cartilage loss is evident. Imaging confirms osteoarthritic changes.

puzzles.explanation: Knee osteoarthritis results from progressive cartilage loss and is common with aging and prior injury.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old construction worker reports lateral lower back pain that developed gradually over 6 weeks. Pain worsens with twisting and bending movements typical of his job.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform sustained rotational work or reach across his body. Simple bed rest relieves symptoms within 24 hours. Functional impact prevents normal construction work duties.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset correlates with a particularly demanding construction project involving repetitive lifting and twisting in awkward postures. Prior lumbar strain 2 years prior resolved with conservative care. No acute trauma or fall. Patient admits to minimal exercise outside of work.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness along thoracolumbar fascia region bilaterally (flanks at L4-5 level). Pain with trunk rotation against resistance. Reduced lumbar rotation in both directions (limited by pain). Palpable muscle guarding in quadratus lumborum region. Single leg stance provokes lateral back pain. Neurologically intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Lumbar spine X-rays normal excluding fracture or structural abnormality. MRI shows fascial thickening and edema in thoracolumbar fascia region. Quadratus lumborum muscle shows edema and signal changes consistent with myofascial injury. No nerve compression or disc pathology. Ultrasound confirms fascial thickening with echo changes.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracolumbar fascia pain is a complex regional pain from fascia and muscular structures.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old woman with 6-week history of gradual-onset joint pain and swelling affecting both hands. Symptoms worse in the morning, improving somewhat with activity. Reports fatigue and general malaise.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty gripping objects, dressing, and fine motor tasks. Pain and swelling predominantly in hands bilaterally. Functional impact worse after prolonged rest or overnight inactivity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No preceding injury or trauma. Family history significant for autoimmune thyroid disease and lupus. Recent mild weight loss. No improvement with rest or non-steroidal anti-inflammatories. Patient previously well with no medical comorbidities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symmetric swelling and warmth of metacarpophalangeal and proximal interphalangeal joints. Grip strength bilaterally reduced (14 kg versus baseline 30 kg). Rheumatoid nodules palpable on elbows. Positive squeeze test of metacarpophalangeal joints reproducing pain. Reduced hand function with difficulty making fist.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Rheumatoid factor positive (titer 1:1280). Anti-CCP positive (256 IU/mL). ESR elevated at 52 mm/hr, CRP 18 mg/dL. Hand X-rays show early periarticular osteopenia with marginal erosions. Ultrasound demonstrates synovial hypertrophy with power Doppler activity in affected joints. Complete blood count shows mild anemia (Hb 10.8).

puzzles.explanation: Rheumatoid Arthritis shows more aggressive disease with extra-articular manifestations in an older male, including nodules and pulmonary involvement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 38-year-old woman with 8 months of pain during sexual activity. Postmenopausal for 1 year. Significant relationship impact and mood disturbance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain occurs only with penetration attempt, preventing intercourse. No pain with non-penetrative contact or daily activities. Pain severity has worsened progressively over 8 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Gradual onset 8 months ago without clear inciting event. Post-menopausal status 1 year prior contributing to tissue changes. No prior pelvic trauma or surgery. Stress and relationship factors contributing. History of anxiety and depression. Recent hormonal changes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor examination shows hypertonic pelvic floor muscles with trigger points palpable. Tenderness on introital palpation. Pelvic floor muscle spasm evident during examination. Levator ani muscles tight bilaterally. Vulvar erythema present in introital region. Speculum examination normal excluding other causes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transvaginal ultrasound shows increased echogenicity and thickening of pelvic floor muscles. No endometriosis, adenomyosis, or structural lesions. Findings consistent with myofascial hypertrophy and spasm.

puzzles.explanation: Dyspareunia without obvious structural cause likely involves pelvic floor dysfunction, myofascial restrictions, or central sensitization. Treatment requires pelvic floor physical therapy, trigger point release, education, and pain neuroscience approaches.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman with 4-year history of persistent widespread pain following low back injury. Extensive prior investigations normal without clear structural cause. Functional capacity significantly reduced with mood disturbance and social withdrawal.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 55-year-old woman with 4-year history of persistent widespread pain that began after a low back strain. Extensive imaging and investigations have been normal. Reports significant disability and mood changes, but functional loss seems disproportionate to any structural findings on imaging.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pain reported in back, neck, shoulders, and hips. Constant baseline pain with variable intensity throughout day. Sleep severely disrupted with unrefreshing sleep pattern affecting daytime alertness and concentration.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Physical examination shows non-anatomic pain patterns and inconsistent findings. Pain reproducible with non-organic examination maneuvers. Widespread tender points throughout body. Psychological distress evident with mood changes during examination. Functional guarding with inconsistent effort during testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Multiple prior imaging studies normal including MRI spine, pelvis, and brain. Extensive lab work unrevealing (normal inflammatory markers, metabolic panel, thyroid). Psychological evaluation shows depression (PHQ-9 score 22), anxiety (GAD-7 score 18), and pain catastrophizing. Pain amplification scale elevated suggesting central sensitization component.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic pain syndrome shows persistent pain beyond tissue healing with psychological factors and complex pain behaviors.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 16-year-old female athlete presents with insidious-onset pain in the forefoot. Pain worsens with weight-bearing activities over the past 2 months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to tolerate impact activities and sports. Swelling visible over the forefoot. Antalgic gait with avoidance of push-off phase during walking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Athlete in repetitive jumping/running sport. No acute injury. Symptoms gradually worsening despite activity modification and rest. No prior treatment sought yet.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable tenderness directly over second metatarsal head (dorsal aspect). Swelling and prominence of metatarsal head region. Pain with passive dorsiflexion of second metatarsophalangeal joint. Limited metatarsophalangeal joint motion secondary to pain. Gait antalgic with reduced stance phase on affected foot.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows flattening and fragmentation of second metatarsal head consistent with Freiberg's infraction (osteonecrosis). Dorsal hypertrophic changes present. MRI shows osteochondral lesion with bone marrow edema. T1 signal hypointensity within metatarsal head indicating necrosis. Associated cartilage irregularity.

puzzles.explanation: Freiberg's disease involves avascular necrosis of a metatarsal head, typically the second.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old male athlete sustains anterior shoulder dislocation during contact sport. Violent arm positioning with forced external rotation and abduction. Immediate severe pain with arm held in protective position.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 22-year-old male athlete presents with acute severe shoulder pain and inability to move the arm following a tackle during contact sport. Arm held protectively at side. Onset was immediate.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cannot abduct or externally rotate shoulder. Arm guarded against any movement away from torso. Visible prominence of anterior shoulder contour.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Mechanism involved a tackle with arm driven into forced abduction and external rotation. No history of prior shoulder instability, generalized hypermobility, or connective tissue disorder. No prior dislocations or sprains.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, scapular Y, and axillary views) confirms anterior humeral head dislocation. Associated Bankart lesion (glenoid rim fracture) visible on specialized imaging. CT scan shows intra-articular loose body and osseous Bankart fragment. MRI post-reduction shows rotator cuff integrity with possible labral involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Shoulder dislocation presents as acute anterior glenohumeral dislocation from traumatic mechanism with structural damage evident on imaging.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 28-year-old overhead athlete reports intermittent shoulder pain with a sensation of the shoulder 'giving way' during throwing. Symptoms are vague but reproducible with certain movements in sport.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate cocking phase of throw without apprehension. Unable to perform weighted activities (e.g., push-ups, bench press) without feeling shoulder is unstable. Symptoms worse in positions of shoulder abduction and external rotation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Overhead athlete with progressive symptoms over past 2 seasons correlating with increased throwing volume. No discrete traumatic injury. Denies prior shoulder surgery or known hypermobility syndrome. Reports sense of fatigue in shoulder during extended practice.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive sulcus sign (inferior shoulder subluxation with traction). Positive apprehension and relocation tests. Laxity demonstrated with inferior translation in dependent position (drawer sign). Scapular dyskinesis evident during active motion. Reduced internal rotation relative to external rotation (reverse position). Strength 4+/5 indicating dynamic stabilizer weakness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows enlarged rotator cuff interval with increased capsular volume. Posterior labral fraying (SLAP pathology) associated lesion. Excessive glenohumeral translation documented on imaging. No discrete labral detachment (rules out Bankart). Ultrasound demonstrates increased capsular volume and laxity on dynamic maneuvers.

puzzles.explanation: Multidirectional instability with generalized ligamentous laxity in young patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Neonate presents with upper limb weakness and loss of function noted shortly after birth. Parents report decreased movement and apparent pain or discomfort with arm handling during the first few days of life.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Infant demonstrates severe limitation in shoulder movement. The arm is held in a characteristic resting posture with reduced abduction and external rotation. Upper limb protective positioning is evident; infant resists passive shoulder movement.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Delivery was complicated by shoulder dystocia requiring manual traction to deliver the infant. Neonatal exam performed within 24 hours of birth noted the motor deficit. No prior upper limb trauma or congenital anomalies.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: biceps and deltoid strength grossly weak (0-1/5). Moro reflex is absent on affected side; lower limb Moro intact. Passive shoulder abduction and external rotation severely limited. No claw hand or wrist drop observed.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brachial plexus reveals avulsion of the C5-C6 nerve roots from the spinal cord. The upper trunk demonstrates signal abnormality and discontinuity. Nerve root avulsion is confirmed with imaging findings consistent with preganglionic injury.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic Erb's palsy with incomplete recovery requires assessment for nerve regeneration potential and consideration of surgical intervention.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 30-year-old athlete reports recurrent episodes of ankle giving way, particularly during cutting or pivoting movements. The instability started after a previous ankle injury several months ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk confidently on uneven terrain without fear of ankle giving way. Struggles with quick directional changes and single-leg activities. Normal weight-bearing walking is mostly stable, but sudden movements trigger instability.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chronic ligamentous laxity follows previous ankle sprains and incomplete rehabilitation. Proprioceptive deficits persist despite symptom resolution. Multiple recurrent inversion episodes have established chronic instability.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, positive anterior drawer test indicates significant tibiotalar translation. Inversion stress testing demonstrates hypermobility. Proprioceptive testing reveals impaired balance. Ankle proprioception is diminished.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI demonstrates chronic lateral ligament attenuation with scar tissue. Anterior talofibular ligament (ATFL) shows partial thickening and signal changes consistent with healed but weakened ligament. No osteochondral lesion or syndesmotic involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic ankle instability results from incomplete healing and proprioceptive deficits after previous sprains.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A young child presents with developmental delay in motor milestones and abnormal movement patterns. Muscle tone abnormalities and stiffness restrict normal motor function. The condition is nonprogressive but becomes more apparent with age and development.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A young child with delayed motor development and increased muscle stiffness. Parents report abnormal movement patterns emerging over the first months of life. The condition has remained stable but becomes more functionally limiting as the child grows.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Child walks on toes with knees bent and legs crossing in front of each other. Hip adduction is pronounced bilaterally. Unable to achieve normal heel-strike during ambulation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Birth complicated by prolonged labour and low Apgar scores. Motor difficulties were apparent within first weeks of life. Parents note the pattern of movement dysfunction has not worsened or improved significantly over years.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain demonstrates periventricular leukomalacia with signal changes in white matter around the ventricles. Ventricular dilation may be present secondary to white matter loss. The imaging findings correlate with the pattern of spastic diplegia observed clinically.

puzzles.explanation: Cerebral palsy is a non-progressive motor disorder from perinatal brain injury, typically presenting with spasticity and developmental delays.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports feeling low in mood and anxious. Describes lack of motivation and mental fatigue impacting daily function.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient finds it difficult to engage in work and social activities. Reports feeling most functional and engaged during and immediately after structured physical activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Six-month history of low mood and anxiety. Previously tried antidepressant medications with modest response. Noticed spontaneous improvement when started attending gym 3× weekly. No other recent life changes or medical events.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cardiovascular fitness testing shows improved VO₂ max and resting heart rate compared to baseline 3 months ago. Mood questionnaire (PHQ-9) score improved from 16 to 8. Patient demonstrates consistent attendance and progressive training intensity.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Baseline psychiatric evaluation ruled out bipolar disorder and primary mood disorder requiring pharmacotherapy. Sleep study normal. No abnormalities on basic metabolic or thyroid screening. Exercise-based intervention has produced sustained mood improvement without additional medication or psychotherapy changes.

puzzles.explanation: Exercise-induced mood improvement shows positive psychological response to regular physical activity in depressed patient.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Middle-aged woman with diabetes presenting with shoulder pain. Onset gradual over recent weeks. Pain present at rest and with activity, disrupting sleep and daily routines.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to reach overhead or behind back. All shoulder movements restricted. Patient reports difficulty with dressing, grooming, and sleeping on affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Risk factors include middle age, female gender, and poorly controlled diabetes mellitus. The condition often follows minor shoulder trauma or immobilization. Onset is typically gradual with progressive loss of motion over weeks to months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive external rotation severely limited (near 0 degrees). Scapulohumeral rhythm abnormal with early scapular elevation. Discomfort at end-range in capsular pattern. No weakness detected.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs of the shoulder are typically normal without bony abnormality. MR arthrography demonstrates thickening and enhancement of the joint capsule. The rotator cuff interval is tightened with loss of the normal axillary pouch distensibility.

puzzles.explanation: Adhesive capsulitis presents with insidious onset of global shoulder stiffness in a capsular pattern, strong association with diabetes and thyroid disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old woman presents with a 2-year history of urinary urgency and frequency that significantly impacts her quality of life. She experiences a persistent urge to urinate 12-14 times daily and 3-4 times nightly, even when the bladder contains minimal urine.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient has modified her activities extensively to stay near bathroom facilities and has experienced occasional urgency incontinence when unable to reach the toilet immediately. Social activities and travel are limited by her symptoms, and sleep is disrupted by nocturia.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has restricted activities to remain near bathrooms and experiences occasional leakage when unable to toilet immediately. Sleep is disrupted by frequent nighttime voids. Social and travel plans are significantly limited.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, vital signs are normal and abdominal examination reveals no masses or tenderness. Pelvic floor muscle assessment shows normal strength but evidence of increased tone. Neurological examination of lower extremities and perineal sensation are intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Bladder ultrasound performed post-void demonstrates normal post-void residual volume of 25 mL. Renal ultrasound is unremarkable with no hydronephrosis. Urinalysis is completely normal with negative culture. Cystoscopy was performed and revealed no structural abnormalities or malignancy.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive Bladder shows neurogenic OAB secondary to CNS disease (stroke, neurological decline) with impaired detrusor function and elevated post-void residual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Young athlete reports anterior knee pain that worsens with jumping and cutting activities. Pain is activity-dependent, onset gradual over weeks to months.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain during landing from jumps and deceleration activities. Single-leg stance and stairs provoke symptoms. Pain often resolves during activity but returns severely after sport, with stiffness the next morning.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Onset follows increased training volume or intensity, particularly jumping-based sports. Weakness in hip or core muscles may contribute to abnormal loading patterns. Previous patellar or knee pathology may predispose to tendinopathy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, tenderness is localized to the patellar tendon insertion at the tibial tubercle. Single leg squats demonstrate poor motor control and knee valgus. Pain occurs with resisted knee extension testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound imaging reveals thickened patellar tendon with mixed echogenicity and increased vascularity on Doppler. MRI demonstrates signal changes within the tendon substance. Imaging findings correlate with symptom duration and severity.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic patellar tendinopathy results from cumulative microtrauma and incomplete healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old male presents with acute-onset weakness and numbness in the shoulder region following a severe fall with direct shoulder impact.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate any movements requiring shoulder abduction. Passive range of motion is preserved. Weakness is the primary functional limitation rather than pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Direct trauma from fall onto shoulder from standing height. No prior neurological symptoms, shoulder pathology, or recurrent dislocations. First neurological symptoms noted immediately post-injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, deltoid muscle is atrophic and significantly weakened with shoulder abduction strength 2/5. Sensation is diminished over the lateral shoulder in the regimental badge distribution. Axillary nerve traction test is positive. Rotator cuff strength is intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Shoulder X-rays rule out fracture or dislocation. MRI shows no rotator cuff tear but reveals signal changes in the axillary nerve course with surrounding soft tissue edema. Electromyography demonstrates denervation potentials in deltoid and axillary nerve conduction slowing.

puzzles.explanation: Post-dislocation axillary nerve injury with deltoid paralysis and sensory loss in axillary nerve distribution.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports shoulder pain and difficulty moving the arm. Pain is worse with attempted active motion. Onset was acute, following a surgical procedure several weeks ago.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead activities or reaching tasks. Cannot lift arm away from body or sleep on affected side. Requires assistance with dressing and grooming. Functional limitation is global rather than movement-specific.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient underwent major shoulder surgery approximately 6 weeks ago for chronic degenerative joint disease. Pre-operative ROM was severely restricted. Recovery has been slower than anticipated; passive stretching causes apprehension.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Passive external rotation is limited to 20°; forward flexion 60° with pain. Apprehension noted at end-range passive motion. No strength testing performed. Rotator cuff integrity not formally assessed. Shoulder girdle musculature appears atrophic.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative X-rays show secure component fixation with no signs of loosening or subsidence. No heterotopic ossification noted. Soft tissue shadow consistent with post-operative edema.

puzzles.explanation: Post-Total Shoulder Replacement shows mid-term recovery (4 months) with good pain control but persistent stiffness and weakness requiring intensive strengthening.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A woman in her 60s reports sensation of heaviness and pressure in the pelvis that worsens as the day progresses. She also notices increased urinary frequency and occasional urgency.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports she must avoid prolonged standing and cannot perform heavy lifting without significant symptom exacerbation. Symptoms improve within minutes of lying flat.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Parity and vaginal deliveries are significant risk factors for pelvic floor dysfunction. Chronic straining from constipation or chronic cough may contribute. Advancing age and menopause worsen pelvic floor laxity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On pelvic examination, anterior vaginal wall bulging is evident with Valsalva maneuver. The bladder prolapse may be visualized or palpated during straining. Grade of prolapse is classified from 1-4 based on extent of descent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dynamic pelvic MRI shows anterior compartment prolapse with bladder descent of >2 cm below pubic symphysis during straining, consistent with grade 2-3 cystocele. No posterior compartment involvement.

puzzles.explanation: Cystocele management requires pelvic floor strengthening, pessary fitting for conservative management, weight reduction, smoking cessation, and consideration of surgical repair (anterior colporrhaphy) if conservative measures fail and functional limitation significant.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient in the immediate post-operative period following total hip arthroplasty reports pain at the surgical site. Hip motion is restricted by protective positioning and precautions. Functional mobility is severely limited in early post-operative phase.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: 64-year-old presents with acute hip pain and severe functional limitation. Onset was 5 days ago following a surgical procedure. Pain is worst with hip movement, particularly flexion and internal rotation. Patient is non-weight bearing and using assistive devices.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient cannot weight bear independently and requires walker for mobility. Hip abduction is markedly limited. Dressing, toileting, and bed transfers are severely restricted. Pain increases sharply with hip flexion beyond 60 degrees and adduction past midline.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, surgical site shows appropriate wound healing without excessive drainage. Hip motion is limited per post-operative precautions. Muscle guarding is present with pain on end-range motion testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Surgical wound healing well with minimal erythema or effusion. Hip ROM: flexion 45 degrees, abduction 15 degrees, internal rotation severely restricted. Straight leg raise weak and painful. Trendelenburg sign present. No calf swelling or warmth. Hip flexor and abductor muscle guarding evident.

puzzles.explanation: THA rehabilitation requires strict hip precautions in the acute phase, progressive hip abductor strengthening to prevent Trendelenburg gait, hip flexor stretching, and functional mobility training to optimize long-term prosthetic function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 6-week postpartum woman reports visible abdominal bulging at the midline and generalized abdominal weakness. Concerned about functional recovery.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform sit-ups or roll bed-side without support. Difficulty carrying infant. Symptoms worsen with straining or Valsalva maneuver but improve with abdominal bracing.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Uncomplicated vaginal delivery 6 weeks prior. Weight gained 16 kg during pregnancy. Return to exercise initiated without postpartum rehabilitation guidance. No diastasis history.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpable gap of 3 fingers width between rectus muscles at midline above umbilicus, reducing to 2 fingers below umbilicus. Rectus muscles separate further with abdominal contraction. Intact fascia, no hernia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms diastasis rectus abdominis with interrectal distance 3.5 cm. Linea alba thickness normal. No ventral hernia present. Dynamic ultrasound shows muscle separation increase with contraction.

puzzles.explanation: Diastasis Rectus Abdominis shows postpartum separation in early recovery period following single uncomplicated vaginal delivery with visible bulging and palpable gap.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A weightlifter reports sudden sharp pain in the anterior chest during a heavy bench press. Immediate weakness and inability to continue lifting. Notices visible change in chest appearance.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot initiate or perform any pushing movements. Unable to perform horizontal adduction against gravity. Pain increases with any attempt to adduct the arm across the body.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Rupture typically occurs during heavy weightlifting with eccentric loading during bench press. The distal tendon attachment at the humerus is the most common rupture site. Steroids or chronic tendon pathology may predispose to rupture.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Strength testing confirms profound weakness in horizontal adduction and internal rotation. Resisted adduction produces no contraction. Palpation along the anterior chest reveals a palpable gap or indentation where muscle continuity is lost. Asymmetry with the contralateral side is striking.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the chest reveals discontinuity of the pectoralis major tendon near its humeral insertion. Muscle retraction and edema are evident at the rupture site. Imaging confirms the site and extent of rupture for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Pectoralis major rupture from maximal weight lifting effort with acute onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports knee pain with intermittent mechanical symptoms. Pain is localized around the knee joint. Swelling develops gradually, particularly worse after prolonged weight-bearing activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to perform rotational movements of the knee under load. Squatting and deep flexion with rotation are significantly limited. Occasional episodes of knee 'giving way' or sudden inability to extend fully.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tear may follow a specific injury with twisting mechanism or develop insidiously in degenerative knees. Previous meniscal pathology or knee injuries increase risk. Degenerative tears are common in middle-aged and older patients.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Joint line palpation reproduces localized tenderness. Knee effusion present. Range of motion limited in flexion, particularly with rotation. Special tests for meniscal irritation are positive.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the knee demonstrates meniscal tear in the medial or lateral meniscus with classification of tear pattern. T2 signal intensity within the meniscal substance indicates tear. Imaging localizes the tear and guides treatment decisions.

puzzles.explanation: A medial meniscal bucket-handle tear presents with mechanical locking, joint line tenderness, delayed effusion (distinguishing from ACL), and positive McMurray's test.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old man reports progressive hearing difficulty for past 5 years affecting both ears symmetrically. Difficulty hearing high-pitched sounds and female voices. Wife notes television volume excessively loud.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty understanding speech in background noise, described as 'cocktail party problem'. Words sound muffled and distorted. Tinnitus present. Withdrawn from social gatherings and group meetings due to communication difficulty.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 68 with expected age-related hearing changes. 45-year occupational noise exposure as machinist without ear protection. No ototoxic medications. No prior ear surgery or trauma. Family history of hearing loss in father. Smoking history 40 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weber test lateralizes to right ear (better hearing). Rinne test shows air conduction > bone conduction bilaterally but air-bone gap present. Otoscopy reveals cerumen impaction bilaterally; post-removal, gap persists. Speech discrimination score 68%.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Audiometry shows bilateral sensorineural hearing loss predominantly at frequencies >2000 Hz, with air-bone gaps of 15-25 dB across all frequencies. Bone conduction thresholds elevated at 4000-8000 Hz. Tympanometry normal (type A). Acoustic reflexes intact bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Age-related hearing loss (presbycusis) involves progressive sensorineural hearing loss, particularly in high frequencies, affecting communication.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: An athlete reports insidious onset of pain in the lower abdomen and groin region over several weeks. Pain is activity-related and worsens with sport participation. No acute injury or trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is provoked by hip adduction and flexion under load. Crossing legs, rapid directional changes, and forceful kicking reproduce symptoms. Symptoms settle with activity modification but recur with return to sport.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Condition occurs in athletes with repetitive hip adduction and flexion activities. Asymmetric hip strength and flexibility contribute to excessive shear at the symphysis. Previous hip or groin injuries increase risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited over the pubic symphysis, worse with direct palpation. Pain is reproduced with resisted hip adduction in supine, and with combined flexion-adduction against resistance. Pain is NOT reproduced with hip abduction or external rotation.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate symphyseal joint changes including widening, sclerosis, and bone resorption. Bone scintigraphy shows increased uptake at the pubic symphysis. MRI reveals edema and inflammatory changes at the symphyseal joint.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic osteitis pubis often involves pelvic instability and may be associated with lumbar or hip dysfunction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 76-year-old woman presents with progressive weakness and functional decline over 2 years. Difficulty rising from chair without armrest support. Walking slower and shorter distances than previously. Recent fall from trip on uneven pavement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Reduced grip strength (12 kg versus 22 kg in contralateral hand). Difficulty with stairs and walking long distances. Loss of independence with heavy household tasks. Slow gait velocity (0.7 m/sec versus normal 1.4 m/sec). Difficulty getting out of bed and toilet independently.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Age 76 with sedentary lifestyle and reduced physical activity. Inadequate protein intake (40g daily versus recommended 1.0-1.2 g/kg body weight). Loss of appetite with reduced food intake. Multiple comorbidities including osteoarthritis and chronic obstructive pulmonary disease limiting activity.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced grip strength 12 kg (abnormal cutoff <16 kg for women). Slow gait speed 0.7 m/sec (abnormal <1.0 m/sec). Reduced lower extremity strength particularly hip flexors and knee extensors (4/5). Difficulty rising from chair in <12 seconds (requires 18 seconds).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Visible muscle wasting in upper extremities and thighs. Reduced hip flexor and knee extensor strength (4/5 bilaterally). Calf muscle bulk reduced. No focal neurological deficits. No joint swelling or active inflammatory signs. Normal proprioception and balance on tandem stance; difficulty initiating movement rather than coordination loss.

puzzles.explanation: Sarcopenic obesity combines muscle loss with excess adiposity, masking underlying sarcopenia and significantly impairing function.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports progressive dyspnea on exertion with gradual loss of exercise capacity. Dry cough that does not improve persists for months. Fatigue and general weakness accompany respiratory symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports significant exertional dyspnea with limited exercise tolerance. Breathlessness is disproportionate to activity—walking short distances causes substantial respiratory distress.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Progressive symptoms over 1–2 years. Occupational dust exposure (silica, asbestos, or organic antigens) or significant smoking history present. No acute infectious trigger identified.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On auscultation, fine end-inspiratory crackles are heard bilaterally at lung bases. Digital clubbing may be evident with nail bed changes. Cyanosis may appear with advanced disease and hypoxemia.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates reticular opacities with traction bronchiectasis in a usual interstitial pneumonia pattern. Pulmonary function testing shows restrictive ventilatory defect with significantly reduced DLCO.

puzzles.explanation: Idiopathic pulmonary fibrosis with classic presentation of progressive dyspnea, dry cough, clubbing, and UIP pattern on HRCT. Reduced DLCO is characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old female cyclist with chronic anterior knee pain reports increasing difficulty with cycling distances. Pain worsens with climbing hills and longer rides. Progressive functional decline.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Difficulty with squatting and descending stairs. Pain on deep knee bend. Unable to perform single-leg activities. Activity limitations more pronounced during eccentric loading tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Cycling hobby with recent increase in distance and intensity. No acute injury. Gradual progressive onset. Multiple attempted conservative interventions with limited benefit.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive J-sign patellar tracking. VMO strength weak. Patellar irritation with movement. Pain with loaded knee extension. Tight hip muscles evident.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows normal articular cartilage and no structural pathology. No meniscal involvement. Mild synovial effusion present. Femoral anteversion noted on imaging. No evidence of ligamentous injury or osteochondral lesion.

puzzles.explanation: Runner's knee shows chronic patellofemoral pain with significant biomechanical dysfunction and functional limitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old reports acute sharp chest wall pain that worsens with deep breathing. Onset was sudden, 2 days ago. Pain is focal and constant but exacerbated by movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to lie on affected side. Reaching across body and bending forward are particularly limited. Difficulty with overhead activities and sustained postures.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Strain typically follows direct trauma, coughing, or sudden twisting movement. Heavy lifting or forceful physical exertion may precipitate the injury. Previous chest wall strain increases recurrence risk.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On palpation, tenderness is elicited along the intercostal muscles. Reproducing pain with intercostal space compression confirms muscular origin. Restricted breathing pattern is evident with protective guarding.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows localized edema and small hematoma within the 5th–6th intercostal muscles. Rib imaging is normal; no fracture or pneumothorax.

puzzles.explanation: Intercostal muscle strain is acute mechanical injury from repetitive or forceful activity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Adult with Down syndrome presents with intermittent neck pain and episodes of stiffness. Symptoms worse with certain head movements. No prior trauma reported.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot turn head fully to either side without discomfort. Difficulty looking down when flexing neck. Avoids rapid or large cervical movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known connective tissue laxity throughout the body. No recent trauma or falls. Two episodes of transient arm weakness over past 6 months. Never had rheumatoid arthritis screening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-Purser test reproduces transient tingling in fingers. Bilateral upper limb hyperreflexia (3+) and positive Babinski bilaterally. Lower limb strength intact 5/5. Sensation intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the cervical spine demonstrates increased atlantodental interval measuring greater than 5mm. Cervical myelopathy is evident with T2 signal changes in the spinal cord. The odontoid process position and cord compression severity are critical findings.

puzzles.explanation: Atlantoaxial instability is a serious condition with risk of serious neurological or vascular compromise requiring specialist care.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports a palpable lump on the wrist or hand that may be painless. The cyst may enlarge and shrink over time. Some patients report aching pain or functional limitation depending on cyst location and size.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports difficulty with sustained gripping or repetitive wrist extension. Pain worsens when bearing weight through the hand (e.g., leaning on palm).

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient recalls gradual onset without clear injury, though minor wrist trauma occurred 2-3 months prior. No constitutional symptoms. No prior treatment attempted. Similar lesions have not appeared elsewhere.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Palpation reveals a firm, smooth, non-compressible mass fixed to underlying structures. The mass transilluminates brightly. Wrist extension ROM is restricted compared to unaffected side. Scaphoid tenderness is absent.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates a unilocular, anechoic lesion arising directly from the scapholunate joint capsule, measuring 1.2 cm with clear cystic boundaries and no internal septations.

puzzles.explanation: Classic ganglion cyst with painless presentation and typical dorsal location.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old female rugby player sustains direct blow to left thigh during match. Acute severe pain and rapid swelling over lateral thigh. Unable to continue playing.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient unable to weight-bear on left leg. Rapid onset of extensive bruising visible within minutes. Unable to perform kicking or change of direction movements.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Tackle occurred 2 hours ago. No previous thigh injuries or bleeding disorders. Patient takes no anticoagulants. Swelling has continued to progress since impact.

clues.label: Examination
