clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Midline calcaneal tenderness is elicited with direct palpation and medial-lateral compression of the calcaneus reproduces pain. Ankle dorsiflexion is limited, and gastrocnemius tightness is evident on assessment. The heel appears mildly swollen relative to the contralateral side.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs show fragmentation and sclerosis of the calcaneal apophysis consistent with apophysitis. No frank fracture or epiphyseal displacement is present. Imaging findings, combined with age, activity level, and clinical presentation, confirm the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Sever's disease may require more intensive management of calf flexibility and activity progression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman presents with progressive height loss over several years and chronic mid-back pain. She sustained a wrist fracture from a minor fall 2 weeks ago. Reports increasing stooped posture.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to bear weight through wrist for 3 weeks post-injury. Thoracic pain prevents lying flat and limits walking distance to <10 minutes. Difficulty rising from low chairs and bed.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menopause at age 57 without hormone replacement. Long-standing low calcium and vitamin D intake. Sedentary lifestyle with minimal weight-bearing exercise. Family history of osteoporosis in mother. Limited sunlight exposure and dietary restrictions.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Height loss 2 inches confirmed. Thoracic kyphosis ~50 degrees on measurement. Grip strength 3/5 bilateral. Percussion tenderness over T7 spinous process. Timed Up and Go 18 seconds (prolonged). No lower limb neurological deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Dual-energy X-ray absorptiometry (DEXA) scan shows T-score of -2.8 at lumbar spine and -2.5 at femoral neck (osteoporotic range). Vertebral fracture assessment shows compression fracture at T7 (15% height loss). Biochemical markers show elevated P1NP and CTX-1 indicating high bone turnover.

puzzles.explanation: Osteoporosis is secondary in a younger patient with identifiable systemic cause.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old with a history of mild osteoporosis presents with sudden-onset sharp chest wall pain following a minor fall from standing height. Pain is strictly localized to one side.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is markedly aggravated by deep inspiration, coughing, and any trunk flexion or rotation movements. The patient splints the injured side when coughing. Lying on the affected side is intolerable.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has been on corticosteroids for 2 years due to polymyalgia rheumatica, increasing fracture risk. No previous rib injuries are reported. Smoking history is significant with 30 pack-years.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Precise point tenderness elicited over the posterolateral chest wall at one rib level. Direct palpation reproduces sharp pain. Intercostal muscle guarding is marked.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: High-resolution CT chest demonstrates a linear cortical break through one rib with minimal displacement. No associated flail segment or pulmonary compromise identified.

puzzles.explanation: Rib fracture is acute post-traumatic with clear mechanism and localized findings.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old patient reports gradual-onset knee pain over 2 years. Pain is worse in the morning but eases with light activity. Pain returns with prolonged walking or standing. Patient reports difficulty with stairs.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports knee swelling by end of day after activity. Pain is mechanical: worse with weight-bearing but improves with rest overnight. Morning stiffness typically lasts 15–20 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient sustained a meniscal tear in right knee 5 years ago; mild surgery was avoided. BMI is 31. Works in construction with repetitive squatting. Progressive onset; no acute injury precipitated current worsening.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, knee effusion may be present with positive ballottement test. Crepitus is palpated with knee motion. Range of motion is limited with pain at end-range flexion and extension.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing AP radiographs show medial compartment joint space narrowing with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. Lateral and skyline views show mild patellofemoral changes.

puzzles.explanation: Early osteoarthritis may present with inflammatory symptoms and variable pain patterns.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old desk worker presents with chronic upper back and neck tension with associated cervical headaches. Symptoms have developed gradually over 3 years of sedentary work. Multiple musculoskeletal patterns may contribute to this presentation.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Symptoms worsen significantly with prolonged computer work and improve with movement breaks and stretching. Forward shoulder posture increases pain within 15-20 minutes. Thoracic extension and scapular retraction relieve symptoms temporarily.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient works 9-10 hours daily at a desk with a poorly positioned monitor and keyboard. Previous physiotherapy provided only temporary relief. No significant trauma history, though postural habits have been poor since adolescence.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Postural assessment reveals forward head posture and rounded shoulders. Palpation confirms significant upper trapezius and pectoralis tightness. Scapular dyskinesis noted on movement screening. Lower trapezius and serratus anterior strength testing shows grade 3-4/5 bilaterally. Cervical extension ROM limited to 35 degrees. No neural signs on upper limb neurodynamic testing.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical and thoracic spine X-rays negative for disc herniation, spondylosis, or subluxation. Postural analysis using plumb-line assessment shows head projection 3 cm anterior to acromion. Scapulohumeral rhythm is asymmetrical with restricted upward rotation bilaterally. Findings confirm postural dysfunction without underlying structural disease.

puzzles.explanation: Upper crossed syndrome is postural dysfunction from modern sedentary work with predictable muscle imbalances.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old office worker presents with bilateral posterior neck stiffness that emerged 4 weeks ago. Symptoms are worse at end of day and improve with rest on weekends.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Neck tension worsens progressively through the workday and is worst by late afternoon. Taking frequent breaks and performing neck stretches provides 30-45 minutes of relief. Symptoms are minimal on weekends and during vacations.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient transitioned to a home office setup with suboptimal ergonomics (laptop screen positioned too low). No history of neck trauma, whiplash, or previous neck symptoms. Stress levels have increased due to workload.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bilateral upper trapezius muscle tightness with palpable trigger points. Cervical ROM is full but restricted by muscle guarding. Forward head posture noted. No neurological signs, nerve tension, or radicular pattern.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical X-rays normal—no degenerative changes, fracture, or alignment abnormality. Imaging ruled out structural disease; diagnosis confirmed by symptom resolution with ergonomic correction over 2 weeks.

puzzles.explanation: Neck tension from desk work shows occupational strain from computer work and forward head posture.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 71-year-old with long-standing cigarette smoking history presents with acute worsening of chronic dyspnea, productive cough, and wheezing. Yellow-tinged sputum noted.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to walk more than 20 metres without severe breathlessness. Previously could manage light household tasks; now struggling with self-care activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: URI symptoms began 1 week ago with fever and malaise. Recent hospitalisation 2 months ago for similar exacerbation treated with antibiotics and steroids.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tachypnea (RR 26), accessory muscle use marked. Diminished breath sounds with wheezes. Productive cough. Cyanosis noted in nail beds. Mental status alert.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows hyperinflation with possible infiltrate suggesting infection. Sputum culture growing bacteria. Spirometry consistent with obstructive pattern. ABG shows hypoxemia and hypercapnia.

puzzles.explanation: Classic COPD presentation with significant smoking history, progressive airflow obstruction confirmed by spirometry, and characteristic clinical findings. represents the post-smoking-cessation patient with established disease.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old presents 6 weeks post-operatively with persistent knee pain and stiffness that has not improved as expected. Pain is present at rest and with activity.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient is unable to fully extend the knee during walking and struggles with stair descent. Knee swelling increases with activity lasting longer than 30 minutes. Quadriceps control is noticeably weak.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had severe pre-operative knee flexion contracture of 15 degrees. A continuous passive motion machine was used post-operatively for 10 days. Compliance with home exercises has been suboptimal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Knee extension is -10 degrees (extension lag present during straight leg raise). Knee flexion reaches 85 degrees actively. Quadriceps strength is 4/5. Significant anterior knee swelling is noted with a positive joint effusion sign. No signs of infection or deep vein thrombosis are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative radiographs show correct component alignment and positioning. No evidence of hardware failure, loosening, or fracture. Mechanical integrity of the reconstruction is sound.

puzzles.explanation: Suboptimal TKA outcomes require intensified extension lag correction through electrical stimulation and biofeedback, aggressive flexion stretching, patellar mobilization, and neuromuscular retraining to restore functional mobility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old female presents 8 weeks post-operative from left total mastectomy with axillary lymph node dissection for invasive ductal carcinoma. Restricted shoulder function and chest wall tightness are limiting daily activities. Post-surgical complications including seroma and lymphedema must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 54-year-old female presents with shoulder and upper chest wall symptoms 8 weeks following left-sided surgical intervention. Functional limitations with daily activities are worsening. No acute pain or fever.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The surgical incision healed well without dehiscence. No chemotherapy or radiation therapy has been initiated yet. Lymph nodes were removed from levels 1 and 2. The patient is right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Left axillary lymph node dissection (levels 1–2) performed 8 weeks ago. Surgical wound has healed without breakdown. No chemotherapy or radiation initiated. Right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound of the axilla shows no evidence of seroma recurrence or lymph collection. Soft tissue imaging confirms scar tissue adhesions in the axillary region. Imaging does not identify mechanical complications requiring surgical intervention.

puzzles.explanation: Post-mastectomy rehabilitation requires early scar mobilization, gradual shoulder range of motion restoration, lymphedema prevention through compression and elevation, and preparation for adjuvant therapies that may affect rehabilitation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 26-year-old overhead athlete presents with progressive anterolateral shoulder pain during explosive overhead activities. Onset was 8 weeks ago and has worsened despite activity modification. Pain is reproducible with sport-specific movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is specific to overhead activities performed explosively and is worst during the late cocking and acceleration phases of throwing. Rest provides partial relief but symptoms recur immediately upon return to sport. Repetitive activities reproduce pain reliably.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Training volume increased significantly 2 months prior to onset. No traumatic event. History of postural dysfunction (lower crossed syndrome). Previous shoulder episodes responded to rest but this presentation is more persistent.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Scapular medial border winging noted during active arm elevation. Serratus anterior and lower trapezius weakness on strength testing. Tightness in posterior shoulder and pectoralis. Supraspinatus sign positive (pain 80–120 degrees abduction).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI: mild supraspinatus tendinopathy without full-thickness tear. Rotator cuff otherwise intact. No labral pathology or glenohumeral joint abnormality. Subacromial space narrowing consistent with impingement secondary to scapular positioning.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic scapular dyskinesis with significant functional loss and muscle weakness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old presents 12 weeks post-partum with pain during intercourse and occasional loss of control of intestinal gas. She reports these symptoms began after delivery but have not fully resolved.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Stress urinary incontinence occurs with coughing, sneezing, and running. The patient has avoided exercise and high-impact activities for 12 weeks due to fear of leakage and pelvic discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The labor lasted 16 hours with instrumental assistance in the second stage. Perineal tears occurred but were repaired immediately post-delivery. The patient is breastfeeding exclusively, which affects hormonal status. No episiotomy was performed.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor muscle strength is reduced (2/5 on manual assessment). Voluntary contraction fatigue occurs rapidly within 2–3 repetitions. Palpation reveals tender scar tissue and fascial restrictions in the perineal body. Anal sphincter tone is intact and symmetric.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Transperineal ultrasound shows intact external anal sphincter and no defects in the perineal body. Pelvic floor muscle thickness is reduced compared to age-matched controls, consistent with muscular atrophy rather than structural trauma.

puzzles.explanation: Postpartum pelvic floor dysfunction from sphincter injury requires specialist management potentially including colorectal and gynecologic consultation, scar tissue release and mobilization, biofeedback therapy, and careful progression recognizing complexity of sphincter healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old recreational athlete presents with insidious-onset anterolateral shoulder pain over 3 months, worsened by activity and overhead movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain is worse with overhead reaching and cross-body adduction. Sleeping on the affected side aggravates symptoms. Rest provides gradual relief over days to weeks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient plays competitive tennis 3 times weekly and has recently increased intensity. No acute trauma or fall occurred. The dominant (right) shoulder is affected. Previous shoulder problems are denied.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Hawkins and Neer impingement tests. Supraspinatus weakness present but no complete loss of strength. Scapular kinematics normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows thickening and heterogeneity of the supraspinatus tendon with intact fiber continuity confirming tendinopathy. No full-thickness tear is present. Subacromial subdeltoid bursa shows mild fluid consistent with secondary bursitis.

puzzles.explanation: Classic rotator cuff tendinopathy from occupational overhead activities in middle-aged individual.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 62-year-old with chronic ankle pain and stiffness progressing over 5 years, particularly with recent acceleration. Long history of recurrent ankle injuries in the same ankle.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Pain worsens significantly with ambulation on uneven ground and with pivoting movements. Walking tolerance has decreased from 45 minutes to 20 minutes due to progressive ankle pain. Morning stiffness lasting 30 minutes is reported upon waking.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Multiple ankle sprains over 30 years have progressively damaged the joint surfaces and ligamentous support. No significant trauma in the past year, but cumulative joint loading has been high from walking/hiking activities. Previous physiotherapy for ankle stability was completed 10 years ago.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ankle dorsiflexion is limited to 5 degrees (normal 15 degrees) and plantarflexion to 35 degrees (normal 50 degrees). Significant anteromedial ankle joint line tenderness is elicited. The gait demonstrates a stiff ankle pattern with circumduction. Weight-bearing calf raise produces pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weight-bearing radiographs show marked joint space narrowing at the tibiotalar joint with marginal osteophytes and subchondral sclerosis. No significant subluxation or instability is evident. Imaging confirms advanced ankle osteoarthritis with degenerative changes consistent with post-traumatic disease.

puzzles.explanation: Ankle OA typically develops after prior injury or chronic instability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A patient reports deep groin pain with activities involving hip flexion and internal rotation. Mechanical symptoms including catching or clicking may occur. Functional limitation occurs with running and pivoting activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A patient reports deep groin or anterior hip pain during athletic activities. Onset was gradual over several weeks. Pain is intermittent but reproducible with certain movements. Mechanical symptoms may accompany pain.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient cannot tolerate combined movements of hip flexion and adduction (e.g., bringing knee to opposite shoulder). Pain worsens with prolonged sitting. Walking is tolerated but high-impact activities are limited.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, the FABER test and anterior impingement test may reproduce symptoms. Internal rotation of the hip in flexion triggers pain. Hip flexibility deficits are often noted.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: On examination, the hip shows restricted internal rotation in flexion (less than 10 degrees). Pain is reproduced with combined flexion, adduction, and internal rotation. No significant capsular end-feel. Palpable psoas and adductor tenderness may be present. Hip abductor strength is intact.

puzzles.explanation: Labral tears often occur with underlying impingement or instability. Mechanical symptoms like clicking are characteristic.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old recreational athlete reports sudden-onset severe pain in the posterior lower leg during a tennis match. A sharp, tearing sensation was felt, followed by immediate difficulty with weight-bearing. No prior calf symptoms.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient reports severe swelling has developed over the first 24-48 hours post-injury. Bruising is becoming visible extending from mid-calf toward the ankle. Morning stiffness and pain with any weight-bearing are significant.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports an episode of acute calf pain 10 years prior that self-resolved without intervention. Current injury occurred during explosive movement in tennis. No chronic calf problems or previous Achilles issues. No recent corticosteroid use or systemic illness.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is noted with bruising extending toward the ankle over days post-injury. Plantarflexion strength is severely weakened (1-2/5) with pain limiting effort. The Thompson test is negative (indicating intact Achilles tendon). A midcalf muscle defect may be palpable on careful examination. Considerable ecchymosis is present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius-soleus muscle complex with edema and hemorrhage consistent with a partial muscle tear (grade 2-3 strain). The tear is localized to the medial gastrocnemius. No Achilles tendon discontinuity is identified.

puzzles.explanation: Tennis leg shows post-injury gastrocnemius weakness with healing muscle tear and functional deficit.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old desk worker presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the medial hand (ring and little fingers) over 4 months. Symptoms are worse at night and have begun affecting grip strength.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing exacerbates symptoms significantly. Sleeping with the elbow flexed triggers symptoms that wake the patient during the night. Activities requiring sustained elbow flexion (holding phone to ear) reproduce paresthesias within 5-10 minutes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient works with poor ergonomic setup: armrest too low, keyboard too high, elbow flexed for prolonged periods. No recent trauma to the elbow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing the ulnar nerve at the elbow (just posterior to the medial epicondyle). The Froment test is positive with inability to adduct the thumb without flexing the interphalangeal joint. Intrinsic hand muscle atrophy is subtle but present in the interossei. Two-point discrimination is diminished in the little finger.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of conduction velocity across the elbow segment consistent with cubital tunnel syndrome. EMG demonstrates denervation activity in intrinsic hand muscles and mild motor involvement. MRI shows compression of the ulnar nerve at the level of the cubital tunnel.

puzzles.explanation: Classic cubital tunnel syndrome with nocturnal symptoms and elbow flexion aggravation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 26-year-old rugby player. Ring finger caught forcefully during tackle. Sharp pain and immediate inability to bend finger at one joint. Swelling developed quickly in the forearm.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot actively straighten the ring finger at rest; finger held in bent position. Pain worsens when attempting to bend the finger. Foregrip and gripping activities severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 hours ago during competitive contact sport. No prior hand or finger injuries. Patient is fully active athlete with high functional demands.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Ring finger held in extension at DIP joint with inability to actively flex (0/5 DIP flexion). PIP flexion intact at 4/5 strength. Mild swelling noted in volar forearm proximal to wrist. Tender palm with fullness along flexor digitorum profundus tendon region. No sensory loss or vascular compromise. No wound or skin disruption.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray shows no bone avulsion at distal phalanx base (distinguishes from bony mallet). MRI demonstrates complete rupture of flexor digitorum profundus tendon at level of wrist crease. Tendon retracted proximally with gap of 3cm. Associated edema in forearm soft tissues. Ultrasound confirms tendon discontinuity with fluid collection (hematoma) tracking proximally in forearm.

puzzles.explanation: Jersey finger from acute traction injury during sports with FDP avulsion from distal phalanx.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 67-year-old woman with pelvic floor dysfunction reports mixed symptoms of urgency and stress incontinence. Frequency significant with 12+ voidings daily. Nocturnal symptoms with multiple awakenings.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Functional limitation from incontinence and frequency. Difficulty with social activities and travel. Sleep disruption from nocturia. Significant quality of life impact.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to engage in social outings, avoid travel. Sleep severely disrupted by nocturia (wakes 4+ times nightly). Anxiety about public situations due to incontinence episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Weak pelvic floor on assessment. Pelvic heaviness sensation. Palpable pelvic floor laxity. Possible mild pelvic organ prolapse. Guarding from pain possible.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Weak voluntary pelvic floor contraction (grade 2/5 strength, poor endurance). Pelvic heaviness on Valsalva. Palpable anterior vaginal wall laxity. Unable to isolate deep core muscles. No significant pain-related guarding noted.

puzzles.explanation: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction shows post-traumatic anal sphincter injury with fecal incontinence resulting from significant childbirth trauma and incomplete recovery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with 6 months of burning pain in the ball of the foot. Pain radiates into the toes and is notably aggravated by tight footwear and prolonged standing. Symptoms are intermittent.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Walking on hard surfaces and ascending stairs exacerbate symptoms. Patient reports a sharp shooting sensation into specific toes during gait, particularly in the push-off phase. Activities requiring forefoot gripping (e.g., standing on tiptoes) are avoided.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient has a documented history of the same condition on the contralateral foot 8 years ago, resolved with conservative measures. Symptoms are recurrent in the same occupation. No history of foot trauma or inflammatory arthropathy. No sensory disturbance elsewhere in the foot.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Direct compression between the metatarsal heads reproduces sharp pain into the 3rd and 4th toes (Mulder's test positive). A palpable click or 'pop' may be felt with this compression. Plantar palpation elicits exquisite tenderness in the interspace. Sensation to light touch is normal in the affected toes.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic nodular mass between the 3rd and 4th metatarsal heads consistent with a neuroma (typically 3-5mm). MRI confirms a focal mass in the plantar intermetatarsal space with T2 signal intensity consistent with nerve tissue edema. Imaging rules out other forefoot pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Chronic or recalcitrant Morton's neuroma may require advanced interventions including injection or surgery.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old admitted 4 days ago following hip fracture repair. Family reports sudden onset of confusion and disorientation that seems to come and go. Patient alternates between agitation and lethargy. Baseline cognition was borderline—family notes he was becoming more forgetful before admission.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: During the day, patient is drowsy and difficult to engage in therapy. At night, he becomes agitated and tries to get out of bed unsupervised. Staff report he doesn't seem to know where he is or what time of day it is. Mood and alertness shift unpredictably hour to hour.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Pre-admission cognition: early dementia with slow decline. Post-operatively: opioids introduced for pain, two sleep aids prescribed. Medical team has investigated and ruled out infection, electrolyte abnormality, and dehydration. Patient has no prior delirium episodes. This is his first time in hospital.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Bedside cognition testing: cannot name the date or hospital location. Digit span: struggles to repeat 4 digits forward. Attention test (months backwards): stops after 3 months. No focal weakness, sensation intact, cranial nerves normal. Heart rate 78, BP 138/82, temperature 37.1°C.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: CT head performed to exclude acute intracranial pathology. No acute stroke, no subdural hematoma, no bleed. Only finding: chronic small vessel ischemic changes consistent with age. Structural causes ruled out. Clinical diagnosis of delirium confirmed.

puzzles.explanation: Delirium superimposed on dementia has poor prognosis with limited functional recovery potential and high mortality risk.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old man presents with progressive stiffness and loss of motion in the hip, developing over the past 3 months. Pain and swelling in the hip region limiting daily activities like dressing and walking.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Progressive loss of hip internal and external rotation. Hip flexion contracture developing (unable to fully extend hip). Abduction and adduction limited. Pain with attempted motion. Functional decline with difficulty dressing and toileting.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Hip posterior dislocation with femoral head fracture sustained 12 weeks ago from high-energy trauma. Significant soft tissue injury. Prolonged immobilization in acute phase followed by delayed mobilization.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Hip flexion contracture (unable to achieve neutral extension). Severe restriction of internal rotation and external rotation (<15° each direction). Firm, non-painful end-feel on passive range. Palpable stiffness in periarticular tissues. No neurological deficit. Antalgic gait.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray hip demonstrates extensive bone formation in soft tissues surrounding the hip joint with bridging across the articulation, graded as severe. CT imaging shows mature ossified bridges in capsular and periarticular tissues. MRI confirms ossification signal consistent with mature bone.

puzzles.explanation: Heterotopic ossification is post-traumatic or post-surgical bone formation in soft tissues causing stiffness.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 58-year-old with type 2 diabetes for 12 years presents with progressive pain in the feet and lower legs over the past 8 months. Pain is described as burning, electrical, or 'pins and needles' with a distal-to-proximal pattern.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Light touch or clothing contact triggers sharp pain out of proportion to stimulus severity. Symptoms are notably worse at night and wake the patient from sleep. Prolonged standing or walking significantly exacerbates discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Glycemic control has been inconsistent (HbA1c 7.8–8.5% over 2 years). Patient is also hypertensive. No preceding injury or trauma to the feet. Symptoms began insidiously without clear trigger.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sharp-dull discrimination is diminished in the feet bilaterally with a stocking distribution. Vibration sense is reduced at the ankles. Monofilament testing (10g) shows loss of sensation in the plantar surface. Temperature sensation is mildly altered. Reflexes are diminished at the ankles. Motor strength is normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show reduced motor and sensory conduction velocities in bilateral lower extremities consistent with demyelination. EMG demonstrates mild denervation in distal foot muscles. DN4 neuropathic pain questionnaire score is elevated, supporting neuropathic origin.

puzzles.explanation: Neuropathic pain presents with characteristic burning/electric quality in nerve distribution with confirmed nerve pathology on testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 78-year-old reports increasing difficulty remembering recent events and managing complex everyday activities over the past 18 months. Spouse notices the patient often forgets recent conversations and becomes confused with familiar tasks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot manage new learning or complex sequential tasks without written support. Medication management with multiple prescriptions is now unreliable; requires supervision for unfamiliar activities.

clues.label: History
clues.text: History of transient ischemic attack 5 years prior. Currently managed for hypertension and high cholesterol but medication adherence is inconsistent. Depression screening unremarkable. No acute neurological events in recent months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Formal cognitive screening using Montreal Cognitive Assessment shows score of 22/30 (below age-adjusted norms). Memory is impaired for recent events but long-term memory is preserved. Attention and executive function are mildly affected. Speech is normal and no focal neurological deficits are present.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI reveals small vessel ischemic disease and mild age-appropriate brain atrophy. No acute infarction, hemorrhage, or mass. Imaging pattern consistent with vascular contributions to cognitive decline but does not exclude underlying neurodegenerative process.

puzzles.explanation: Mild cognitive impairment represents decline between normal aging and dementia, with memory and cognitive deficits affecting complex activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 72-year-old woman fell onto an outstretched hand from standing height and reports immediate wrist pain with visible swelling.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot grip or lift objects. Wrist movement in all directions is severely limited by pain. She is unable to perform self-care tasks involving hand use.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Known osteoporosis with progressive balance impairment contributing to falls. No significant prior wrist injuries. Fall occurred at standing height without additional trauma context.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The wrist demonstrates a 'dinner fork' deformity with dorsal displacement of the distal fragment. Marked swelling of the dorsal wrist is evident. Tenderness is greatest over the distal radius. Radial and ulnar pulses are intact. Hand color and temperature appear normal, and finger sensation is preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain wrist radiographs (posteroanterior and lateral views) show a dorsally displaced metaphyseal fracture of the distal radius with comminution of the dorsal margin. Ulnar styloid fracture is present in 50% of cases. CT may be used to assess fracture complexity for surgical planning.

puzzles.explanation: Complex comminuted Colles fracture from high-energy trauma requiring surgical management.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 7-year-old presents with a fourth fracture in 18 months after falling from a low height during play. Parents report the child has always bruised easily and been unusually fragile compared to siblings.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Child is unable to weight-bear on the fractured limb and refuses to participate in running or contact play. Motor milestones have been delayed; walking began at 20 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Mother and maternal grandmother have history of recurrent fractures and hearing loss. Child's growth has trailed below 10th percentile since infancy. Audiometry at school screening shows conductive hearing loss. Dental clinician noted enamel hypoplasia with unusually early decay despite good hygiene.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Generalized ligamentous laxity evident; passive range of motion at all major joints exceeds normal limits. Muscle bulk and tone are diminished throughout. Respiratory effort appears increased at rest. Skin is translucent with visible veining. Palate is high-arched.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-rays of the fractured limb show the acute fracture with osteopenic bone (reduced bone density) and thin cortices. Generalized skeletal survey reveals healed fractures in various stages of healing and evidence of previous long bone deformities. Bone density assessment (DEXA scan) shows significantly reduced bone density. Genetic testing confirms collagen I abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Osteogenesis imperfecta is a genetic connective tissue disorder with severe bone fragility.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 54-year-old presents with 2 months of progressive deep shoulder pain and weakness. Pain is localised posteriorly but non-radiating. Weakness is noted with certain arm movements.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform overhead activities without compensation and reports sharp pain with throwing. Reaching behind the back is markedly limited. Interestingly, passive range of motion appears relatively preserved.

clues.label: History
clues.text: A fall on an outstretched arm with external rotation occurred 3 months ago, initially attributed to a minor strain. Symptoms have progressively worsened rather than improving. No significant prior shoulder history. The patient is right-hand dominant.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked weakness of external rotation (1-2/5) with reproduction of pain. Supraspinatus strength is preserved. Posterior shoulder atrophy is evident. Infraspinatus tenderness elicited on palpation beneath posterior deltoid.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows a full-thickness tear of the infraspinatus with mild retraction of the muscle belly. The supraspinatus is normal. Fatty infiltration is absent, indicating acute injury. Ultrasound confirms infraspinatus tear with gap at the musculotendinous junction. The tear size and patient age determine management approach.

puzzles.explanation: Infraspinatus tear presenting with external rotation weakness and posterolateral pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old previously healthy teacher presents with acute-onset fever, cough, and chest discomfort of 3 days duration. The cough is productive of greenish-yellow sputum and is associated with dyspnea with minimal exertion.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 42-year-old previously healthy teacher presents with a 3-day history of fever, persistent cough, and chest discomfort. The cough is productive of discolored sputum. Shortness of breath occurs with minimal exertion.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Exertional dyspnea is limiting daily activities; patient unable to return to work. Lying flat worsens cough and breathing; patient sleeps propped up. General fatigue prevents non-work activities.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination, temperature is 38.8°C, respiratory rate 22 breaths per minute, and oxygen saturation 94% on room air. Auscultation reveals crackles in the left lower lobe with egophony present. Dullness to percussion noted over affected area. Tachycardia present at 102 bpm.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray demonstrates lobar consolidation in the left lower lobe with air bronchograms, consistent with community-acquired pneumonia. Sputum culture is pending. White blood cell count is elevated at 14,500 cells/mL. Procalcitonin level is elevated supporting bacterial etiology.

puzzles.explanation: Elderly pneumonia presentation with atypical presentation (confusion without fever, no prominent cough). Common in geriatric patients where infection may present as delirium rather than fever.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old desk worker presents with swelling over the posterior aspect of the elbow that developed insidiously over 2 weeks. Pain is mild and localised to direct pressure on the swollen area; elbow movement remains largely pain-free.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Swelling is significantly worse after resting or leaning on the elbow for extended periods. Activities requiring active elbow movement are tolerated well; however, passive end-range elbow extension and direct contact with the swollen area provoke discomfort.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient recalls no specific trauma but notes increased time leaning on elbows while working at a desk over the past 3 months. No fever, chills, or systemic symptoms are reported. No intravenous drug use. Rheumatoid arthritis screening has been negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: On examination: prominent soft, fluctuant swelling localised to the olecranon bursa. Overlying skin is normal in appearance without erythema or warmth. Palpation of the bursal fluid elicits tenderness, but the olecranon bone itself is non-tender. Elbow range of motion is full and pain-free. No systemic signs: no fever, no local erythema, lymph nodes normal.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a fluid collection in the olecranon bursa without internal echoes (simple fluid) or thick septations, consistent with bursitis. Bursal wall is thickened. No osteophytes or bone erosion is evident. Imaging rules out septic bursitis (which would show echogenic debris or thick loculations).

puzzles.explanation: Chronic aseptic olecranon bursitis from repetitive pressure and leaning, classic student presentation.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A runner reports sudden-onset midfoot pain on the outer border of the foot. Pain began during or immediately after a push-off movement or twisting incident. Weight bearing is significantly limited.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Lateral foot pain is reproducible with single-leg hop and push-off. Swelling develops within hours. Pain does not resolve with rest and anti-inflammatory management over several days.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred 2 weeks ago during running. Initial swelling and bruising were pronounced. Patient attempted to continue activity. Pain has not improved despite relative rest. No prior foot injuries.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is sharply localized to a small area at the lateral midfoot base; palpation reproduces exact pain location. Weight bearing on the affected foot is severely limited. Ankle inversion and eversion are pain-free. Resisted plantarflexion provokes sharp lateral foot pain.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate transverse fracture at the metaphyseal-diaphyseal junction of the fifth metatarsal. CT imaging assesses fracture displacement and healing. Advanced imaging guides timing of weight bearing progression.

puzzles.explanation: Jones fracture at the metatarsal base is a common sport injury with risk of delayed healing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old presents with pain localized to the anterior ear region bilaterally, clicking in both temporomandibular joints, and difficulty with wide mouth opening. Symptoms have developed over 2 months. Myofascial and intra-articular disorders must be differentiated.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 45-year-old presents with pain around both ears and clicking in the jaw joints. Mouth opening has become more difficult over the past 2 months.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Chewing tough foods and prolonged talking aggravate the pain. The patient notices the jaw deviates to one side when opening and experiences morning stiffness of the jaw.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: The jaw deviates to the left on opening (10mm at maximum). Bilateral temporomandibular joint palpation elicits mild tenderness and is felt clicking during opening and closing. Masseter and temporalis muscle palpation is tender with trigger points. Maximum mouth opening is limited to 35mm (normal 40-50mm).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Palpation of both temporomandibular joints elicits clicking during opening and mild tenderness. Maximum mouth opening is 35mm. Masseter and temporalis muscles are tender with palpable trigger points bilaterally. Jaw deviates 10mm to the left at maximum opening.

puzzles.explanation: TMJ disorder presents with disc displacement pattern, clicking, and functional limitation with clear stress-related trigger.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 38-year-old presents with sharp anterior chest wall pain on the left side for 3 weeks, worse with deep breathing and coughing. The pain is localized and reproducible. Cardiac causes are being considered in the differential.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Movements that increase chest wall tension or rib cage excursion aggravate symptoms, particularly heavy lifting and repetitive pushing. Sleeping on the left side is uncomfortable. Symptoms improve with arm support and reduced upper body activity.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient had an upper respiratory infection with persistent cough 2 months ago; however, the cough has resolved, but pain persists. No trauma to the chest wall or weight lifting occurred. Previous episode of similar pain occurred 3 years ago and resolved in 6 weeks.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Tenderness is elicited on direct palpation of specific points on the anterior rib cage. Chest wall ROM is unrestricted. Cardiac auscultation is normal. Respiratory examination reveals no abnormalities.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Chest X-ray shows no acute findings. ECG is normal, excluding cardiac causes. Clinical examination alone is sufficient to establish the diagnosis.

puzzles.explanation: Costochondritis is the classic mechanical presentation with clear tender points and mechanical reproduction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 22-year-old presents with a lifetime history of joint instability, frequent dislocations (particularly shoulders and fingers), and diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. The patient reports being 'double-jointed' since childhood. Systemic connective tissue disorder must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 22-year-old reports a lifelong pattern of joint instability and recurrent dislocations (particularly shoulders and fingers), alongside diffuse joint pain that worsens with activity. Friends have always remarked on unusual flexibility. No single injury explains the pattern.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient cannot sustain repetitive fine motor activities (typing, playing instruments) without pain and apprehension. General exercise tolerance is preserved, but dynamic multi-joint movements (jumping, pivoting, contact sports) consistently provoke instability episodes.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Family history: mother has documented joint hypermobility. Patient reports easy bruising, delayed wound healing, and a childhood able to perform extreme flexibility feats (thumb-to-wrist bending, full splits). No connective tissue diagnosis yet established in the family.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Beighton score 6/9 (thumbs touch forearm, fingers hyperextend >90°, palms flat on floor with knees straight, elbow hyperextension, knee hyperextension). Shoulder girdle strength reduced symmetrically. Proprioceptive testing shows subtle deficits; postural stability during single-leg stance is reduced.

puzzles.explanation: Generalised joint hypermobility occurs in an athletic context where flexibility is advantageous until pain develops.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old recreational tennis player presents with anterior shoulder pain for 3 weeks. Pain began during overhead serve motion. Reports occasional clicking sensation in shoulder during reaching.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot tolerate overhead throwing or serving. Pain worsens with sustained reaching above shoulder height. Symptoms improve partially with rest and ice, but night pain disturbs sleep, particularly when lying on the affected side.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Repetitive overhead sports participation (tennis 4x/week) for past year. Gradual onset suggesting tendinopathy rather than acute tear. No prior shoulder surgery. Underlying rotator cuff dysfunction noted on screening examination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Speed's test (supinated elbow flexion against resistance) reproduces anterior shoulder pain. Yergason test (supinated forearm with elbow flexion) also positive. Palpable tenderness over bicipital groove. Apprehension test negative. Rotator cuff strength 4+/5 suggesting dynamic instability.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound demonstrates biceps tendon thickening (5mm) with hypoechoic echotexture and increased Doppler signal indicating active inflammation. MRI confirms intrasubstance degeneration of the proximal biceps tendon with intact fibres and no full-thickness tear.

puzzles.explanation: Biceps tendinopathy associated with rotator cuff pathology, secondary component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 35-year-old presents 4 weeks post-operatively from a high-energy elbow injury requiring surgery. Progressive pain and severe loss of motion have developed despite appropriate post-operative management. Swelling persists.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Elbow extension severely limited to approximately 60 degrees of flexion; passive attempts provoke significant pain and guarding. Basic functional activities like reaching overhead and grooming are now impossible.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sustained the original injury 4 weeks ago from a fall from height. Required open surgical reduction and internal fixation due to fracture-dislocation. Significant soft tissue trauma at time of injury. Post-operative wound healing and rehabilitation have progressed normally otherwise, but motion loss and pain are worsening despite therapy.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Severe soft tissue swelling persists over the antecubital fossa and dorsal elbow. Elbow flexion is approximately 90 degrees. Attempts at passive extension reproduce significant pain and apprehension. Skin temperature is slightly elevated. Radial and ulnar pulses remain intact. Fingers are warm with normal color.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Plain radiographs reveal cloud-like opacities within soft tissues around the elbow with early demarcation—consistent with heterotopic bone formation. CT demonstrates extent of ossification in relation to the surgical fixation. Bone scan shows marked uptake in the affected region.

puzzles.explanation: Myositis ossificans is pathological bone formation in soft tissues causing significant disability.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: Patient reports sharp shoulder pain following a direct blow to the top of the shoulder during a contact sport. Pain worsens with arm movements that cross the midline or reach overhead.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform bench press or push-ups. Symptoms flare when throwing or reaching across the body, such as fastening a seat belt. Overhead lifting is markedly painful.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sprain typically follows direct blow to the shoulder or fall onto the outstretched arm. The injury grades from 1-6 based on ligament disruption and clavicular displacement. Higher grade sprains demonstrate clavicular subluxation or complete dislocation.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked tenderness directly over the AC joint with joint line palpation. Crossover arm adduction test reproduces sharp pain at the joint. Shoulder abduction strength is intact but adduction under load causes discomfort. No signs of glenohumeral instability or rotator cuff pathology.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Radiographs demonstrate clavicular displacement and AC joint space abnormalities based on sprain grade. Weighted views show degree of clavicular elevation if AC ligament is disrupted. MRI reveals ligament injury and associated findings.

puzzles.explanation: AC joint osteoarthritis from chronic overhead loading in weightlifter with insidious onset.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 64-year-old presents 7 days post-operatively from abdominal surgery with acute pain at the surgical site and visible leakage of fluid from the incision. Patient reports sudden onset during the recovery period.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot perform normal post-operative mobilisation. Straining, coughing, or bending triggers sharp pain and increased fluid drainage from the site. Ambulation is severely limited.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial post-operative course was unremarkable until day 6, when sudden pain and drainage developed. Patient has significant post-operative cough and BMI of 32. No prior wound complications or immunosuppression.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Visual inspection of the incision reveals partial separation of the wound edges with visible subcutaneous tissue in the area of separation. Serosanguineous drainage is present. The surrounding skin is erythematous. No systemic signs of infection are present (normal temperature, vital signs stable). Some wound edges remain intact.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound confirms separation of fascial and subcutaneous layers with a fluid collection measuring 3 cm. No evidence of loculated abscess. Intact deep fascia noted on imaging.

puzzles.explanation: Surgical wound dehiscence with infection requires urgent wound care, infection management with antibiotics, possible reoperation and reclosure, infection prevention, nutritional support, and modified rehabilitation with careful wound protection.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 32-year-old man wakes with sudden severe unilateral neck pain and inability to move his head normally. Onset during sleep with no recall of trauma.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot turn head to the affected side. Rotation in opposite direction is possible but painful. All neck movements exacerbate symptoms.

clues.label: History
clues.text: No prior neck problems. No trauma recalled. Symptoms started immediately upon waking; patient had slept on an unfamiliar pillow.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant muscle spasm in ipsilateral sternocleidomastoid and upper trapezius visible. Neck rotation severely restricted in direction of pain, approximately 20 degrees. Palpable muscle tightness.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cervical spine X-rays show normal alignment and no acute bony pathology. No evidence of subluxation or fracture.

puzzles.explanation: Acute wry neck is the classic acute torticollis with sudden onset and severe movement restriction.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with persistent shoulder pain and stiffness 2 weeks after a surgical procedure. Despite the intervention, overhead movements remain difficult and sleep is disrupted. Pain is present with most arm activities.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Unable to perform overhead tasks or reach behind the back for dressing. Pain escalates sharply with any arm elevation beyond 90 degrees of abduction. Night pain prevents comfortable sleeping positions.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Patient reports pre-operative symptoms were consistent with classic impingement: pain worse with overhead activity, night pain waking them from sleep. Imaging prior to surgery showed degenerative changes and inflammation. Physiotherapy compliance post-op has been good.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Special tests reveal positive supraspinatus sign and apprehension with external rotation. Scapular dyskinesis is noted during arm elevation. Palpation elicits pain over the anterior shoulder and acromioclavicular joint. Strength testing intact but guarded.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Post-operative ultrasound shows complete osteophyte removal and enlarged subacromial space. Rotator cuff is intact with no evidence of iatrogenic tear. Bursa is clear. However, there is persistent mild subdeltoid edema suggesting ongoing inflammation.

puzzles.explanation: Post-subacromial decompression arthroscopy requires early range of motion to prevent stiffness, gradual strengthening of rotator cuff and scapular stabilizers, and activity modification to prevent recurrent impingement.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old presents with progressive mid-back pain (thoracic spine) and bilateral lower extremity pain with a girdle-like distribution around the chest at the level of the thoracic spine. Pain is localized to the thoracic region with radiation bilaterally. Myelopathic features must be assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 52-year-old with progressive mid-back pain and bilateral lower limb pain. Pain has a banding quality around the trunk. Symptoms developed insidiously over 3 months without preceding injury.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Symptoms developed insidiously over 3 months without trauma. The patient has a sedentary desk job. Previous episodes of mid-back pain have been minimal. No significant trauma or falls. Progressive nature of symptoms is concerning.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Sedentary desk worker. Symptoms began 3 months ago without trauma or clear inciting event. Minimal prior back history. Progressive worsening despite conservative measures (rest, activity modification).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI shows central or paracentral disc herniation at the thoracic level compressing the spinal cord. The cord shows signal change (T2 hyperintensity) consistent with myelomalacia. CT can clarify bone anatomy if surgical intervention is contemplated. Imaging demonstrates cord compression with edema indicating significant pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Thoracic disc herniation is a rare and diagnostically challenging condition with serious cord compression implications.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 42-year-old with sudden onset of severe vertigo and nausea, started 3 days after an upper respiratory infection resolved. Vertigo is worse with any head movement.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient cannot walk without severe imbalance and falls risk. Cannot tolerate reading, screen use, or rapid eye movements. Has vomited multiple times since onset.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Sudden hearing loss noted in left ear since vertigo onset. No prior episodes of dizziness or hearing problems. No head trauma, no medication changes. Lives alone, unable to care for self currently.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Spontaneous nystagmus present, fast component beating toward left ear. Head impulse test abnormal on leftward rotation—unable to maintain target fixation. Romberg test positive. Nausea reproduced with rapid head turns. Weber test lateralizes to left (conductive pattern). Rinne shows air-bone gap on left.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI with contrast shows T2 hyperintensity in the labyrinth on the affected side consistent with labyrinthitis. The vestibular nerve may show enhancement. Audiometry documents sensorineural hearing loss in the affected ear. Imaging and audiometry confirm viral labyrinthitis with associated hearing loss.

puzzles.explanation: Acute labyrinthitis requires vestibular rehabilitation, balance retraining, gait training for safety, psychological support given acute distress, and monitoring for spontaneous recovery while preventing deconditioning and chronicity.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old woman presents with chronic dizziness and vertigo sensation lasting 8 months. Symptoms fluctuate but persistently present. Not true vertigo but persistent dizziness.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Avoids busy visual environments and crowded spaces. Difficulty walking straight lines. Symptoms worse with head movements and self-motion. Better when standing still or holding onto stable support.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Dizziness onset post-vestibular neuritis 8 months prior. Acute vestibular symptoms resolved but dizziness persisted and worsened. Anxiety developed from symptoms.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Head impulse test normal (preserved VOR gain). Tandem stance and Romberg stable. Positive visually-evoked vestibulo-ocular reflex sensitivity (symptoms provoked by visual motion). Gaze stability normal. No spontaneous nystagmus.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: ENG: previously showed vestibular asymmetry now resolved. MRI brain: normal. Caloric testing: normal vestibular function bilaterally. Diagnosis of PPPD confirmed by chronic symptoms persisting 8+ months post-vestibular neuritis with normalization of objective vestibular testing and absence of CNS pathology.

puzzles.explanation: Persistent Postural-Perceptual Dizziness is a functional vestibular disorder following initial vestibular event, characterized by chronic non-vertiginous dizziness, postural sway, and visual motion intolerance with normal vestibular testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 44-year-old secretary reports progressive numbness and tingling in the thumb, index, and middle fingers bilaterally, worse on the right. Symptoms are particularly troublesome at night, frequently awakening her from sleep.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Prolonged typing aggravates symptoms within 30 minutes of work. Gripping objects produces numbness and tingling. Driving worsens symptoms. Night symptoms interfere with sleep quality, requiring frequent position changes.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Works 8 hours daily at a computer with poor ergonomic positioning. No prior wrist trauma or fracture history. Recent thyroid screening was normal.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign is elicited by percussing over the median nerve at the wrist crease. Phalen's test (wrist flexion held 30 seconds) reproduces numbness and tingling within 10 seconds bilaterally. Two-point discrimination is diminished on the thumb and index finger. Hand strength is normal in the intrinsic muscles tested.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show prolonged latency of median sensory and motor nerves across the wrist compared to the forearm, confirming conduction slowing at the carpal tunnel level. Electromyography shows mild denervation in the abductor pollicis brevis. MRI shows flattening of the median nerve at the wrist. Studies confirm carpal tunnel syndrome bilaterally.

puzzles.explanation: Classic carpal tunnel syndrome with nocturnal paraesthesia in the median nerve distribution, provocable signs, and risk factors of pregnancy and hypothyroidism.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: 22-year-old collegiate soccer player with acute anterior knee pain during a cutting maneuver. Reports sensation of something tearing. Immediate inability to continue activity with rapid swelling onset.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot perform straight leg raise or active knee extension. Sharp pain with any attempt to straighten the knee or bear weight through extended leg. Functional guarding of the entire knee.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Young athletic male with high-demand sport (soccer). Acute traumatic mechanism during cutting maneuver. No prior knee surgery or injury. Recent growth spurt (18 years old) with some residual cartilage sensitivity. Full participation in sport up to injury.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Marked anterior knee swelling with visible hematoma over tibial tuberosity. Complete loss of active knee extension strength (0/5). Quadriceps musculature palpable but unable to contract. Large knee effusion. Patellar tendon tense and exquisitely tender.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: X-ray (AP, lateral, and Merchant views) shows avulsion of tibial tuberosity fragment with 1.5cm displacement. CT confirms fracture extends into articular surface of tibial plateau. MRI shows complete disruption of patellar tendon at bone insertion with severe edema. Associated bone marrow edema in proximal tibia. Soft tissue trauma with hematoma extensive.

puzzles.explanation: Tibial tuberosity avulsion is a serious injury occurring at the junction of growth plate and tendon insertion.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 42-year-old presents 8 weeks after an ankle inversion injury. Initial injury seemed minor, but pain and swelling have progressively worsened and remain out of proportion to the original trauma. Pain is described as burning and constant.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient cannot tolerate any weight-bearing and avoids contact with the affected foot. Even light touch provokes severe burning pain that is disproportionate to normal sensory stimulation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Initial ankle sprain from walking. Pain has escalated progressively over 8 weeks despite appropriate rehabilitation and ligamentous healing. Patient reports secondary depression and anxiety.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Examination reveals bright red, warm, edematous ankle and foot with shiny skin appearance. Sudomotor changes are evident with increased sweating and skin moisture. Vasomotor changes include color variation from red to purple depending on dependency. Nails appear brittle. Pain assessment with gentle touch demonstrates allodynia. Strength testing is limited by pain, not by motor deficit.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Three-phase bone scan shows increased uptake in the delayed phase with blood pooling pattern. Radiographs demonstrate spotty, patchy osteoporosis. MRI reveals edema in bone and soft tissues. Combined clinical and imaging findings meet diagnostic criteria for the suspected condition.

puzzles.explanation: CRPS presents as classic Type 1 (no confirmed nerve injury) with severe pain, edema, autonomic changes, and motor dysfunction following minor trauma.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old with hypertension and atrial fibrillation presents with acute onset of severe headache, dizziness, vomiting, and gait imbalance. The patient had a sudden fall and is unable to ambulate. Posterior circulation stroke must be rapidly assessed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 68-year-old with hypertension and atrial fibrillation wakes with acute severe headache, dizziness, vomiting, and inability to walk. Patient fell immediately upon attempting to stand.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Unable to walk or sit upright without severe dizziness and nausea. Any fine motor task or balance attempt triggers vertigo and loss of coordination.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Symptoms began upon waking this morning with no warning. Multiple vascular risk factors: resistant hypertension, untreated atrial fibrillation, hyperlipidemia, and heavy smoking history. No prior cerebrovascular events.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nystagmus present. Dysmetria on finger-to-nose testing bilaterally. Speech is ataxic and irregular. Cognition preserved. Gait wide-based and severely unsteady, unable to perform tandem or single-leg stance.

puzzles.explanation: Acute cerebellar stroke with significant edema causing deteriorating consciousness and risk of herniation requiring surgical decompression.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 52-year-old tennis player felt a sudden sharp sensation in the back of the lower leg during an explosive pushing movement in play. Immediate severe pain and inability to continue. No prior significant leg injuries.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Cannot rise up on toes or push off the ground. Severe pain and instability with any attempt to plantarflex the ankle under load. Walks with foot held in slight plantarflexion. Stair climbing severely restricted.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Injury occurred during explosive push-off movement. No warm-up prior to match. Otherwise sedentary between competitions; age 52. No prior significant calf problems.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Significant calf swelling is immediately visible, with bruising developing within hours and advancing distally toward the ankle. Plantarflexion strength is 0-1/5 (severe weakness). The Thompson test is negative (calf squeeze does not produce plantarflexion, suggesting gastrocnemius-soleus tear rather than Achilles rupture). Calf tenderness is marked in the midsubstance region.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Ultrasound shows a hypoechoic disruption in the gastrocnemius or soleus muscle with surrounding edema and hemorrhage (grade 2-3 muscle strain). The musculotendinous junction is intact. Achilles tendon is continuous. Imaging grade guides management (conservative for grade 1-2, possibly surgical for grade 3).

puzzles.explanation: Acute calf muscle strains result from sudden forceful plantarflexion or push-off activities.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 5-year-old boy is referred by his parents because he is falling frequently and struggling to keep up with age-matched peers during play. Over the past year, he has noticed increasing difficulty with running and climbing stairs. His parents report he was reaching normal developmental milestones until recently.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The child demonstrates a characteristic sign when rising from the floor: he uses his hands to 'climb up' his own legs. He walks with an exaggerated waddling gait and toe-walks when running. Stairs require bannister support; jumping is impossible. Generalized proximal weakness is evident.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The child achieved normal early motor milestones. His maternal uncles had similar childhood-onset progressive weakness. No siblings or father are affected. Onset was insidious—no trauma or specific event triggered the decline. Progressive functional loss over one year.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Strength testing: hip and shoulder girdle 2-3/5; ankle plantarflexion preserved at 5/5 (disproportionate to hip weakness). Calf muscles appear enlarged despite weakness. Waddling gait with marked lumbar lordosis. Scapular winging. No sensory loss. Reflexes preserved.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Serum creatine kinase markedly elevated (>10,000 IU/L). Muscle biopsy immunostaining shows complete absence of dystrophin protein. Genetic testing confirms mutation in the DMD gene. EMG demonstrates myopathic pattern with brief, small polyphasic motor units.

puzzles.explanation: Duchenne muscular dystrophy with classic presentation in young boy, marked CK elevation, and confirmed dystrophin deficiency on genetic and histochemical testing.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness. A concussion was diagnosed acutely after loss of consciousness. Persistent post-concussion symptoms are limiting return to work. Diagnostic criteria must be confirmed.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 28-year-old presents 6 weeks after a motor vehicle accident with loss of consciousness. Ongoing headache, difficulty concentrating, and dizziness are limiting daily function.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The accident involved moderate-speed motor vehicle collision with loss of consciousness for 15-20 seconds. No focal neurological injury was identified acutely. The patient is slowly returning to activities but symptoms persist weeks beyond expected recovery. Previous concussion history is negative.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Cognitive testing shows mild slowing on reaction time tasks and impaired working memory. Oculomotor testing (smooth pursuit) shows minor asymmetry. Balance testing (tandem stance) is mildly abnormal. Vestibulo-ocular reflex testing may show minor impairment. Headache provocation testing reproduces pain with cognitive exertion.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Brain MRI is structurally normal with no focal lesions, hemorrhage, or contusion. Diffusion tensor imaging may show subtle white matter microstructural changes but is not routinely indicated. The imaging is reassuring, and diagnosis is clinical using the SCAT5 (Sport Concussion Assessment Tool). Imaging rules out structural complications.

puzzles.explanation: Post-concussion syndrome requires multidisciplinary approach including vestibular rehabilitation, cervical rehabilitation, cognitive rehabilitation, gradual return-to-activity progression, mood screening and management, and monitoring for persistent post-concussive syndrome.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 55-year-old presents with 3 weeks of intermittent vertigo, head movement intolerance, and oscillopsia (visual blur with head movement) following a viral upper respiratory infection. The patient tilts the head toward the unaffected side to reduce symptoms. Peripheral vestibular dysfunction is likely.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: A 55-year-old presents with 3 weeks of intermittent vertigo and oscillopsia (visual blur with head movement) that began shortly after a viral upper respiratory infection. Head movements trigger symptoms, and the patient has noticed tilting the head toward one side provides relief.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Acute vertigo onset occurred 3 weeks prior following a viral URI with vertigo lasting several days. Residual dizziness and head movement intolerance persist. No hearing loss is reported (unlike labyrinthitis). No recent trauma.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Vertigo onset was abrupt 3 weeks ago and lasted approximately 5 days, with residual dizziness and head movement intolerance persisting since. No hearing loss, ear fullness, or tinnitus has developed. No prior episodes of vertigo or head trauma.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: MRI of the brain and internal auditory canal is normal, ruling out retrocochlear pathology. Videonystagmography (VNG) shows impaired vestibulo-ocular reflex gain on the affected side. Caloric testing shows reduced response on the affected side (unilateral hypofunction). Imaging rules out stroke or tumor.

puzzles.explanation: Unilateral Vestibular Hypofunction/Labyrinthitis shows acute viral labyrinthitis with combined vestibular AND cochlear involvement (hearing loss), differentiating it from pure vestibular neuritis.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 45-year-old recreational cyclist presents with progressive numbness and tingling in the ring and little fingers after recent increases in cycling volume (now 40+ miles weekly). Weakness in finger spreading movements is developing. Onset was gradual over the past 3–4 weeks.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Gripping handlebars reliably triggers numbness in the affected fingers within 30 minutes of cycling. Fine motor tasks (typing, writing) are increasingly difficult. The patient reports finger clumsiness when attempting delicate hand tasks.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Long-distance cycling is the main recreational activity with recent increase in cycling distance (from 20 to 40+ miles per week). Handlebar type and positioning have not changed. No previous nerve problems reported.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Finger abduction and adduction strength are reduced (4/5) indicating intrinsic hand muscle weakness. Two-point discrimination is decreased in the ring and little fingers. Sensation to light touch is mildly reduced. The ulnar pulse is palpable at the wrist. No atrophy is evident yet (recent onset).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of ulnar nerve conduction across the wrist and palm, consistent with compression at the wrist level (Guyon canal region). EMG shows mild denervation activity in interossei muscles. Ultrasound may show compression of the ulnar nerve at the wrist from external pressure. Imaging confirms ulnar neuropathy from compression.

puzzles.explanation: Cyclist's palsy shows severe chronic ulnar nerve compression from repeated cycling trauma with muscle atrophy.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 68-year-old male presents with progressive urgency and urinary frequency of 6 months duration. He reports an urgent need to void 10-12 times per day and 4-5 times nightly, with a sensation of incomplete emptying persisting after each void.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Patient has significantly restricted daily activities: avoids travel and public outings due to unpredictable urgent need to void. Sleep is severely fragmented, limiting daytime function. He denies symptoms related to specific movements or postures—urgency occurs regardless of activity level.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Recent diuretic initiation for hypertension coincided with symptom escalation 6 months ago. Long-standing history of benign prostatic hyperplasia, stable on medical management. No history of urinary tract infection, trauma, or neurological disease. Lives independently; symptom impact is primarily on social participation and sleep.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Pelvic floor assessment reveals normal voluntary contraction strength and tone; no dyssynergia on assessment. Lower limb neurology intact. Post-void residual by ultrasound is 60 mL (mild retention). Abdominal and digital rectal examination unremarkable except mild prostatic enlargement without suspicious nodules.

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Cystometry or urodynamic testing shows detrusor overactivity during filling phase with involuntary contractions unrelated to voluntary void initiation. Normal bladder compliance. Renal function and imaging are normal; no hydronephrosis or structural abnormality.

puzzles.explanation: Overactive Bladder shows idiopathic detrusor overactivity in postmenopausal female with predominant urgency and frequency without stress component.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 48-year-old presents with several months of burning pain in the sole of the foot and medial ankle region. Symptoms worsen with activity and are particularly bothersome at night. Mild ankle swelling is noted. Pain is described as sharp and burning rather than dull ache.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: Standing and walking aggravate the burning sensation in the foot. Tight shoes worsen symptoms. Sleeping position changes are frequent due to nighttime foot pain. Running is not attempted due to symptom aggravation.

clues.label: History
clues.text: The patient has a history of ankle inversion sprains and currently has mild ankle instability. Mild varicose veins are present. No significant trauma precipitated current symptoms, which developed insidiously over months.

clues.label: Examination
clues.text: Positive Tinel's sign at the posterior tibial nerve (behind medial malleolus) reproduces tingling into the sole of the foot. Pain is localized to the medial ankle and sole of foot (not lateral ankle as in peroneal neuropathy). Toe sensation is reduced, particularly medial toes (plantar surface).

clues.label: Imaging
clues.text: Nerve conduction studies show slowing of posterior tibial nerve conduction velocity specifically across the ankle region. EMG demonstrates denervation pattern in abductor hallucis. MRI reveals thickening of the flexor retinaculum compressing the posterior tibial nerve within the tarsal tunnel space.

puzzles.explanation: Tarsal tunnel syndrome results from posterior tibial nerve compression within the tarsal tunnel causing radiating foot pain.

clues.label: Complaint
clues.text: A 14-year-old girl presents after her school health check revealed uneven shoulder and hip height. Her mother has noticed this asymmetry has become more obvious over the past 2 years. The patient is otherwise healthy and has no pain.

clues.label: Activity
clues.text: The patient reports occasional mid-back discomfort when sitting at her desk for long periods. Otherwise, she participates fully in sports and daily activities without restriction. Main concern is the visible asymmetry.

clues.label: History
clues.text: Menarche occurred 18 months ago, indicating rapid skeletal growth. The patient is tall for age (85th percentile). No significant family history of scoliosis is reported. General health is good.

clues.label: Examination
